Define dp[i][j]=minimum ASCII sum to delete to make s1[0..i−1] and s2[0..j−1] equal. Base cases: dp[0][0]=0 dp[i][0]=dp[i−1][0]+ASCII(s1[i−1])(delete all of s1)dp[0][j]=dp[0][j−1]+ASCII(s2[j−1])(delete all of s2)If characters match: dp[i][j]=dp[i−1][j−1](no deletion needed).
If they don't: delete from s1 or s2,whichever costs less.

units.unit_name: LeetCode 712 Minimum ASCII Delete Sum for Two Strings - Implementation
units.text:
Here's the full solution:
Pseudocode


```
function minimumDeleteSum(s1,s2)m :=length of s1
    n :=length of s2
    dp :=2D(two-dimensional)array(m+1)x(n+1)dp[0][0] :=0
    for i from 1 to m
        dp[i][0] :=dp[i - 1][0]+ASCII(s1[i - 1])for j from 1 to n
        dp[0][j] :=dp[0][j - 1]+ASCII(s2[j - 1])for i from 1 to m
        for j from 1 to n
            if s1[i - 1]=s2[j - 1]
                dp[i][j] :=dp[i - 1][j - 1]
            else
                dp[i][j] :=min(dp[i - 1][j]+ASCII(s1[i - 1]),dp[i][j - 1]+ASCII(s2[j - 1]))
return dp[m][n]

```

Time: O(mn). Space: O(mn).
The base cases sum ASCII values of deleted characters. The transition minimizes deletion cost: either delete from s1 or s2.

questions.title: Minimum ASCII Delete Sum - Implement Solution

units.unit_name: LeetCode 712 Minimum ASCII Delete Sum for Two Strings - Walkthrough

units.text:
This is Edit Distance from LeetCode,also called Levenshtein Distance. Given two strings,find the minimum operations to convert one to the other. You can insert,delete,or replace a character. For "horse" and "ros": horse → rorse → rose → ros. 3 operations.
Constraints: m,n≤500. Brute-force recursion is exponential. Build up answers for shorter prefixes first to avoid recomputation.

You have three operations at each step:
1. Insert: Add a character to A. Moves from(i,j)to(i,j−1)in DP(you matched B[j] by inserting).
2. Delete: Remove a character from A. Moves from(i,j)to(i−1,j)(discarded A[i]).
3. Replace: Change a character in A. Moves from(i,j)to(i−1,j−1)(aligned A[i] with B[j]). Each operation costs 1. If characters match,no operation needed.

Define dp[i][j] as the minimum operations to convert A[0..i-1] to B[0..j-1]. Base cases differ from LCS: dp[0][j]=j: Converting empty string to B[0..j] requires j insertions. dp[i][0]=i: Converting A[0..i] to empty string requires i deletions.
These non-zero base cases reflect that converting to/from empty strings has a cost. Unlike LCS,base cases here are non-zero: converting to or from an empty string costs exactly the other string's length.

At position(i,j),compare A[i-1] and B[j-1]. If they match: dp[i][j]=dp[i−1][j−1]. No operation needed. If they don't match: dp[i][j]=1+min(dp[i−1][j],dp[i][j−1],dp[i−1][j−1])That's delete,insert,and replace respectively.
Each costs 1. The final answer is dp[m][n]. The formula is the core of any DP solution. It defines how dp[i][j] depends on earlier dp values. Getting this formula right is often the hardest and most rewarding part.

Here's the full solution:
Pseudocode


```
function minDistance(word1,word2)m :=length of word1
    n :=length of word2
    dp :=2D(two-dimensional)array of size(m+1)x(n+1)for i from 0 to m
        dp[i][0] :=i
    // delete all chars from word1
    for j from 0 to n
        dp[0][j] :=j
    // insert all chars into empty string
    for i from 1 to m
        for j from 1 to n
            if word1[i - 1]=word2[j - 1]
                dp[i][j] :=dp[i - 1][j - 1]
            else
                dp[i][j] :=1+min(dp[i - 1][j],dp[i][j - 1],dp[i - 1][j - 1])
return dp[m][n]

```

Time: O(mn). Space: O(mn),reducible to O(n).
The three operations map to: delete(`dp[i-1][j]`),insert(`dp[i][j-1]`),replace(`dp[i-1][j-1]`). When characters match,no operation is needed.

Trace Edit Distance on s1​="horse" and s2​="ros". Operations: insert,delete,replace. dp[i][j]=min operations to transform s1​[0..i−1] to s2​[0..j−1]. If chars match: dp[i][j]=dp[i−1][j−1]. Otherwise: dp[i][j]=1+min(dp[i−1][j],dp[i][j−1],dp[i−1][j−1]).
Answer: dp[5][3]=3. Operations: replace 'h' with 'r',remove 'r',remove 'e'. Or: remove 'h',replace 'r' with 'r',remove 'e'.

The DP gives the minimum count,but which operations? Backtrack from dp[m][n]. At each cell,check which case gave the minimum: diagonal(match or replace),left(insert),up(delete).
If dp[i][j]=dp[i−1][j−1] and chars match: no operation. If dp[i][j]=dp[i−1][j−1]+1: replace. If dp[i][j]=dp[i−1][j]+1: delete from s1​. If dp[i][j]=dp[i][j−1]+1: insert into s1​. When ties occur between operations,prefer the one that gives a more natural edit sequence.

Three patterns to remember:
1. More operations means more transitions. LCS has 2 choices,Edit Distance has 3.
2. Base cases change with the problem. LCS starts with 0s,Edit Distance starts with indices.
3. The same 2D(two-dimensional)structure works for many string problems. dp[i][j] with two strings is universal. You've now learned the two pillars of string DP. Apply these patterns when you encounter similar problems.

How do you recognize a string DP problem?
1. Comparing two strings? Probably 2D(two-dimensional)DP with dp[i][j].
2. Looking for common structure? Think LCS pattern(match/skip).
3. Converting one string to another? Think Edit Distance pattern(multiple operations).
4. Counting ways? Same structure,but sum instead of max/min. The formula always asks: what happens at position(i,j)based on whether characters match?

units.unit_name: LeetCode 516 Longest Palindromic Subsequence - Problem Statement
units.text:
Solve Longest Palindromic Subsequence from LeetCode. Given a string,find the longest subsequence that reads the same forwards and backwards.
Hint: This reduces to LCS. Compare the string with its reverse. The LCS of a string and its reverse is the longest palindromic subsequence. For 'bbbab',the answer is 4: the subsequence 'bbbb' uses indices 0,1,2,4 and reads the same forwards and backwards.

Find the longest palindromic subsequence in s. observation: LPS(s)=LCS(s,reverse(s)). Why? A palindrome reads the same forwards and backwards.
So the common subsequence between s and its reverse is palindromic. Example: s="bbbab". Reverse: "babbb". LCS="bbbb" with length 4. Alternative: direct DP with dp[i][j]=LPS of s[i..j]. Transition: if s[i]=s[j],extend; else take max of excluding either end.

units.unit_name: LeetCode 583 Delete Operation for Two Strings - Problem Statement
units.text:
Solve Delete Operation for Two Strings from LeetCode. Given two strings word1 and word2,find the minimum number of deletions to make them equal. You can only delete characters,not insert or replace.
This connects directly to LCS. If LCS has length L,you need to delete m−L characters from word1 and n−L from word2. Total deletions: m+n−2L.

questions.title: Delete Operation for Two Strings - Implement Solution

units.text:
Solve Distinct Subsequences from LeetCode. Given strings s and t,count distinct subsequences of s that equal t. This is counting DP. dp[i][j]=ways to form t[0..j−1] from s[0..i−1].
If characters match,you can use it or skip it. If they don't match,you must skip. For s='rabbbit' and t='rabbit',there are 3 ways to choose which extra 'b' to drop.

Count how many times t appears as a subsequence of s. Let dp[i][j]=count for s[0..i−1] and t[0..j−1]. If s[i−1]=t[j−1]: we can match this character(dp[i−1][j−1])or skip it(dp[i−1][j]). Total: dp[i][j]=dp[i−1][j−1]+dp[i−1][j]. If they don't match: must skip s[i−1]. dp[i][j]=dp[i−1][j]. Example: s="rabbbit",t="rabbit".
Answer: 3(three ways to choose which 'b' to skip).

units.unit_name: LeetCode 1092 Shortest Common Supersequence - Problem Statement
units.text:
This is Shortest Common Supersequence from LeetCode. Given two strings,find the shortest string that has both as subsequences. For "abac" and "cab",the answer is "cabac". This is the inverse of LCS.
Instead of finding the longest common part,you're building the shortest string containing both. The minimum length equals ∣s∣+∣t∣−LCS(s,t). Find the LCS first,then reconstruct the supersequence.

units.unit_name: LeetCode 1092 Shortest Common Supersequence - The LCS Connection
units.text:
The length of SCS relates directly to LCS: ∣SCS∣=∣A∣+∣B∣−∣LCS∣ Why? The LCS appears once in the supersequence. Everything else from A and B must be added. You add ∣A∣−∣LCS∣ characters from A and ∣B∣−∣LCS∣ characters from B. Total: ∣LCS∣+(∣A∣−∣LCS∣)+(∣B∣−∣LCS∣)=∣A∣+∣B∣−∣LCS∣.
To construct the actual string,you need to backtrack through the LCS DP table.

with reconstruction:
Pseudocode


```
function shortestCommonSupersequence(str1,str2)m :=length of str1
    n :=length of str2
    dp :=2D array(m+1)x(n+1),all 0
    for i from 1 to m
        for j from 1 to n
            if str1[i - 1]=str2[j - 1] then
                dp[i][j] :=dp[i - 1][j - 1]+1
            else
                dp[i][j] :=max(dp[i - 1][j],dp[i][j - 1])// Reconstruct SCS by backtracking through LCS
    result :=empty string
    i :=m,j :=n
    while i > 0 and j > 0
        if str1[i - 1]=str2[j - 1] then
            result :=str1[i - 1]+result
            i--
            j--
        else if dp[i - 1][j] > dp[i][j - 1] then
            result :=str1[i - 1]+result
            i--
        else
            result :=str2[j - 1]+result
            j--
    // Add remaining characters
    while i > 0
        result :=str1[i - 1]+result
        i--
    while j > 0
        result :=str2[j - 1]+result
        j--
    return result

```

Time: O(mn). Space: O(mn).
You first compute LCS,then backtrack. When characters match(part of LCS),add once. Otherwise,add the character you're moving away from.

Shortest Common Supersequence contains both strings as subsequences. Length=m+n−LCS length. To construct: trace the LCS path.
Include LCS characters once,non-LCS characters from both strings. Example: s1​="abac",s2​="cab". LCS="ab"(length 2). SCS length=4+3−2=5. SCS="cabac". Construction: walk the DP table. When on diagonal(LCS char),include once. When going up/left,include the skipped char.

This is Wildcard Matching from LeetCode. Given a string and a pattern with wildcards,determine if they match. '?' matches any single character. '*' matches any sequence(including empty).
For s="adceb" and p="ab",the answer is true. The first '' matches "",'a' matches 'a',second '' matches "dce",'b' matches 'b'. The '*' can match zero or more characters,making it harder than '?': you may need to try multiple split positions.

Define dp[i][j]=true if s[0..i−1] matches p[0..j−1].
Base cases: dp[0][0]=true(empty matches empty),dp[0][j]=true if p[0..j−1] is all '*'(stars can match empty),and dp[i][0]=false for i>0(non-empty string can't match empty pattern).
The '*' transition is the tricky part: it can match zero characters(dp[i][j−1])or one-or-more characters(dp[i−1][j]).

Pseudocode


```
function isMatch(s,p)m :=length of s
    n :=length of p
    dp :=2D array(m+1)x(n+1),all false
    dp[0][0] :=true
    // Empty string matches empty pattern
    // Handle leading *'s in pattern
    for j from 1 to n
        if p[j - 1]='*'
            dp[0][j] :=dp[0][j - 1]
    for i from 1 to m
        for j from 1 to n
            if p[j - 1]='*'
                dp[i][j] :=dp[i][j - 1] OR dp[i - 1][j]
            else if p[j - 1]='?' OR p[j - 1]=s[i - 1]
                dp[i][j] :=dp[i - 1][j - 1]
    return dp[m][n]

```

Time: O(mn). Space: O(mn),reducible to O(n).
For  `*` : either use it to match zero characters(`dp[i][j-1]`)or match one more character(`dp[i-1][j]`). For  `?`  or exact match: extend from  `dp[i-1][j-1]` .

Match pattern p with wildcards against string s. '?' matches any char,'*' matches any sequence. dp[i][j]=true if p[0..i−1] matches s[0..j−1].
Handle '' carefully: it can match empty or extend. For '': dp[i][j]=dp[i−1][j](match empty)OR dp[i][j−1](extend match). For '?': dp[i][j]=dp[i−1][j−1]. Example: pattern "a*b" matches "axxxb","ab","axyzzb". Time: O(mn),Space: O(mn)or O(n)with improvement.

You've learned string DP: LCS,Edit Distance,and their variants. The 2D state(i,j)represented positions in two strings. But what if the 2D state represents an interval within one string? That's Interval DP.
In Interval DP,dp[i][j] represents the answer for substring s[i..j]. You'll see problems like Matrix Chain Multiplication and Palindrome Partitioning. The key difference: transitions combine answers from sub-intervals,not from previous positions. Make sure you're comfortable with string DP before moving on.

Look at what you can do now:
1. Solve LCS with 2D DP using match/skip transitions
2. Reconstruct the actual LCS by backtracking
3. Solve Edit Distance with insert/delete/replace operations
4. Reduce problems to LCS(palindrome,delete operation)5. Recognize string DP patterns: two sequences,character comparison,2D table These patterns form the foundation of string algorithms. When you see two strings and need to compare or convert them,think LCS or Edit Distance first.

You've solved problems where dp[i] represents a position or dp[i][j] compares two sequences. Now you'll face problems where the answer depends on how you split a range.
I'll show you Interval DP(dynamic programming): problems where dp[i][j] represents the optimal answer for the subarray from index i to j. The observation is trying all possible ways to split the range. By the end,you'll solve Matrix Chain Multiplication,Burst Balloons,and Palindrome Partitioning. These problems look different but share the same core pattern.

An interval [i,j] is a contiguous subarray from index i to index j,inclusive. The length is j. I+1. In interval DP,you typically iterate by length.
First solve all intervals of length 1(base cases),then length 2,then length 3,up to the full array. The answer you want is usually dp[0][n−1] or dp[1][n],depending on whether you use 0-indexed or 1-indexed arrays.

Most interval DP problems ask: what's the best way to process a range? The answer depends on where you split it. For range [i,j],you try every split point k from i to j-1. Splitting at k means you process [i,k] and [k+1,j] separately,then combine. You pick the k that gives the best result. This creates O(n)choices per state,and O(n2)states,giving O(n3)time overall. Time complexity: O(n3).
Space complexity: O(n2)for the dp table.
Space: O(n2)for the DP table. That's the signature complexity of interval DP.

When you compute dp[i][j],you need dp[i][k] and dp[k+1][j] for various k. Both of those are shorter ranges than [i,j].
So you must solve shorter ranges first. The outer loop iterates over length(from 1),and the inner loop iterates over starting positions. This is different from most DP where you iterate over indices directly. Getting the loop order wrong means you'll access uncomputed values.

units.unit_name: LeetCode 516 Longest Palindromic Subsequence - Problem Statement
units.text:
Given a string s,find the length of the longest subsequence that reads the same forward and backward. Example: for s="bbbab",the answer is 4(the subsequence "bbbb").
This is your first interval DP problem. The important point: any palindromic subsequence on s[i..j] depends on whether s[i]=s[j]. If they match,both characters can be part of the palindrome. If not,at least you must be skipped. Before reading on,think: what's the simplest base case? What happens when i=j?

units.unit_name: LeetCode 516 Longest Palindromic Subsequence - Key Observation
units.text:
Focus on the endpoints of your current range [i,j]. If s[i]=s[j]: both characters can be endpoints of a palindrome. The remaining problem is finding the LPS in s[i+1..j−1],then adding 2 for these matching endpoints.
If s[i]=s[j]: at least one endpoint must be excluded. Either skip s[i](solve s[i+1..j])or skip s[j](solve s[i..j−1]). Take the maximum. This gives us a clean two-case recurrence with no need to try all split points.

units.unit_name: LeetCode 516 Longest Palindromic Subsequence - State Design
units.text:
dp[i][j]=the length of the longest palindromic subsequence in s[i..j]. Why this formulation? Palindromes have a natural interval structure. They're defined by their endpoints. When you add characters at both ends,you need to know the answer for the inner substring.
Base cases: dp[i][i]=1(a single character is a palindrome of length 1). dp[i][i−1]=0(empty range,used when i>j in the recurrence). The final answer is dp[0][n−1],covering the entire string. Fill the table by increasing interval length,so smaller intervals are ready when you need them.

The recurrence has two cases based on whether the endpoints match. If s[i]=s[j]: Both characters can extend a palindrome. Look at dp[i+1][j−1](the inner substring)and add 2.
So dp[i][j]=dp[i+1][j−1]+2. If s[i]=s[j]: At least one endpoint isn't part of the LPS. Take the better of excluding s[i] or excluding s[j]: dp[i][j]=max(dp[i+1][j],dp[i][j−1]). Notice the dependency: dp[i][j] needs dp[i+1][...] and dp[...][j−1]. These are shorter ranges. Iterate by increasing interval length so subproblems are ready.

Trace LPS on s="bbbab". Build dp[i][j]=LPS length for s[i..j]. Base cases: dp[i][i]=1(single char).
Iterate by length. Length 2: dp[0][1]=2("bb"),dp[1][2]=2("bb"),dp[2][3]=1("ba"),dp[3][4]=1("ab"). Length 3,4,5: keep expanding. Final dp[0][4]=4. The LPS is "bbbb". The observation: when endpoints match,they contribute 2 to the palindrome length plus the inner solution.

What you learned:
1. Interval DP uses dp[i][j] to represent answers for ranges [i,j].
2. The recurrence focuses on endpoints: do they match or not?
3. Iteration order matters: compute shorter ranges before longer ones.
4. Not all interval DP needs split point enumeration. LPS is the simplest form. Next,you'll see Matrix Chain Multiplication,where we do need to try all split points. But now you understand the interval structure.

You have n matrices to multiply: A₁×A₂×...×An​. Matrix multiplication is associative,so you can parenthesize in any order. Different orders have different costs. Multiplying an(a×b)matrix by a(b×c)matrix costs a×b×c operations.
Your goal: find the parenthesization that minimizes total operations. This is THE classic interval DP problem. The key: choosing where to split the chain determines which two subchains you multiply first.

The input is an array of dimensions. If dims=[10,20,30,40],you have 3 matrices: A1​ is 10×20,A2​ is 20×30,A3​ is 30×40.
Matrix i has dimensions dims[i−1]×dims[i]. The number of matrices is n=dims.length - 1. Multiplying matrices i through j(after optimal parenthesization)produces a matrix of size dims[i−1]×dims[j]. This fact is key for computing dp[i][j] from smaller ranges. Understanding the problem structure is half the battle.

Define dp[i][j]=minimum cost to multiply matrices from i to j(inclusive). Base case: dp[i][i]=0. A single matrix requires no multiplication. Goal: find dp[1][n],the minimum cost to multiply all matrices. Each dp[i][j] must capture everything needed to solve that range independently.
If you are missing information,you cannot compute the answer correctly. If you include too much,your solution will be slow or use too much memory.

To compute dp[i][j],try every split point k from i to j−1. Splitting at k means you first compute(Mi​…Mk​),then(Mk+1​…Mj​),then multiply the results.
Cost=dp[i][k]+dp[k+1][j]+dims[i−1]×dims[k]×dims[j]. The last term is the cost of multiplying the two resulting matrices.
Take the minimum over all k:
dp[i][j]=mini≤k<j​(dp[i][k]+dp[k+1][j]+dims[i−1]×dims[k]×dims[j])

Pseudocode


```
function matrixChainOrder(dims,n)dp :=2D array [n+1][n+1],all 0
    for len from 2 to n
        for i from 1 to n - len+1
            j :=i+len - 1
            dp[i][j] :=infinity
            for k from i to j - 1
                cost :=dp[i][k]+dp[k+1][j]+dims[i-1] * dims[k] * dims[j]
                if cost < dp[i][j]
                    dp[i][j] :=cost
    return dp[1][n]

```

Fill by increasing chain length so that shorter chains are solved before longer ones. The answer sits at dp[1][n].
Time complexity: O(n3).
Space complexity: O(n2)for the DP table.

The DP gives minimum cost,but what's the optimal order? Track the split point. When computing dp[i][j],record split[i][j]=k where the minimum occurs.
Reconstruct recursively: parenthesize(i,j)="("+parenthesize(i,split[i][j])+parenthesize(split[i][j]+1,j)+")". For our example: split[1][3]=2,so optimal is(A1​⋅A2​)⋅A3​. Store the split point in a separate array. Reconstruct by recursively printing the optimal parenthesization.

Matrix Chain teaches the core interval DP pattern: dp[i][j] for a range,try all split points,iterate by length. The formula has three parts: cost of left range dp[i][k],cost of right range dp[k+1][j],and cost of combining them.
This exact structure appears in many problems. The "combining cost" changes,but the framework stays the same. Parenthesization,optimal BST,and burst balloons all share this 3-part structure.

You have n balloons with numbers on them. Bursting balloon i gives you nums[i−1]×nums[i]×nums[i+1] coins. After bursting,the neighbors become adjacent. Find the maximum coins you can collect by bursting all balloons in the optimal order.
This looks like you could pick the locally best choice each time,but that approach fails. The order matters because bursting changes the neighbors. This is interval DP in disguise.

Here's the observation: instead of thinking which balloon to burst first,think about which balloon to burst LAST in a range. Why? When you burst balloon k last in range [i,j],you know exactly what neighbors k has: the balloons at i-1 and j+1(outside the range). The balloons inside [i,j] are already gone.
This eliminates the dependency problem. The coins from bursting k last=nums[i−1]×nums[k]×nums[j+1]+coins from [i,k−1]+coins from [k+1,j].

Define dp[i][j]=max coins from bursting all balloons strictly between i and j(exclusive). We add virtual balloons with value 1 at positions 0 and n+1.
Base case: dp[i][i+1]=0. No balloons between adjacent positions. Goal: dp[0][n+1],the max coins from bursting all balloons between the virtual boundaries. Choosing what dp[i][j] represents is often the hardest part. It must capture everything needed to solve that range on its own.

For dp[i][j],try each balloon k in(i,j)as the last one to burst. Coins=dp[i][k]+dp[k][j]+nums[i]×nums[k]×nums[j].
The first two terms are coins from clearing the left and right. The last term is from bursting k when only i and j remain as neighbors. dp[i][j]=max over all k in(i,j)of this expression. The formula tells you how to compute dp[i][j] from smaller ranges. Getting this right is the core of DP.

Pad the array with 1s:  `new_nums=[1]+nums+[1]` . Now work with indices 0 to n+1.
Pseudocode


```
function maxCoins(nums)a :=[1]+nums+[1]
    m :=length of a
    dp :=2D array [m][m],all 0
    for gap from 2 to m - 1
        for i from 0 to m - gap - 1
            j :=i+gap
            for k from i+1 to j - 1
                coins :=a[i] * a[k] * a[j]+dp[i][k]+dp[k][j]
                dp[i][j] :=max(dp[i][j],coins)
return dp[0][m-1]

```

The answer is dp[0][n+1]. Balloon k is the last one burst in the interval(i,j),so a[i] and a[j] are still present as neighbors.
Time complexity: O(n3).
Space complexity: O(n2)for the DP table.

Burst Balloons shows that sometimes you need to think backwards. "Which first?" creates dependencies. "Which last?" often simplifies them. The open interval trick(dp[i][j] for strictly between)is common when boundaries interact with the answer.
When picking the locally best option fails and choices affect future options,consider interval DP with reverse thinking. Take time to work through examples.

units.unit_name: LeetCode 132 Palindrome Partitioning II - Problem Statement
units.text:
Given a string s,partition it so every substring is a palindrome. Return the minimum number of cuts needed. For "aab",one cut gives ["aa","b"],both palindromes. For "a",zero cuts needed.
This combines two smaller tasks: checking if substrings are palindromes,and finding minimum cuts. Precompute  `isPalin[i][j]`  in O(n2),then find minimum cuts with DP in O(n2).

units.unit_name: LeetCode 132 Palindrome Partitioning II - Two DPs
units.text:
First,precompute isPalin[i][j]=true if s[i..j] is a palindrome. This is itself an interval DP: isPalin[i][j]=(s[i]==s[j])&& isPalin[i+1][j−1].
Then,define cuts[i]=minimum cuts for s[0..i]. If s[0..i] is a palindrome,cuts[i]=0. Otherwise,try all j where s[j+1..i] is a palindrome: cuts[i]=min(cuts[j]+1). This is 1D(one-dimensional)DP using the 2D(two-dimensional)precomputation. Total time: O(n2).
Time complexity: O(n2).
Space complexity: O(n2)for the dp arrays.

Phase 1: Build isPalin table. Iterate by length. isPalin[i][i]=true. isPalin[i][i+1]=(s[i]==s[i+1]). For longer: check ends match and inner is palindrome.
Phase 2: Compute cuts[i] for each position. Check if whole prefix is palindrome(0 cuts). Otherwise try all valid last palindrome positions. Answer is cuts[n−1]. Time: O(n2),Space: O(n2)for isPalin table. Trace through a small example to verify your understanding before moving on.
Time complexity: O(n2).
Space complexity: O(n2).

Find minimum cuts to partition s="aab" into palindromes. First,precompute isPalin[i][j]=true if s[i..j] is a palindrome. dp[i]=min cuts for s[0..i].
If s[0..i] is palindrome,dp[i]=0. Else,try all cuts. dp[0]=0("a"). dp[1]=0("aa" is palindrome). dp[2]=1("aab" needs cut: "aa"+"b"). Pre-computing the palindrome table avoids redundant checks during the DP. This is a common preprocessing pattern.

The DP gives minimum cuts. What about finding all palindrome partitions? Use backtracking with the isPalin table. At each position,try all palindromic prefixes.
For "aab": start at 0. "a" is palindrome → recurse on "ab". "aa" is palindrome → recurse on "b". "aab" is not. Collect all valid partitions: [["a","a","b"],["aa","b"]]. Time: exponential in worst case. The time complexity is exponential because the number of valid partitions can be exponential.

Use interval DP when: the problem involves a contiguous range,the answer depends on how you split or process the range,and answers for smaller ranges help build larger ones.
Common signals: "minimum/maximum cost to process array","optimal way to merge/split","number of ways to parenthesize". The template: dp[i][j] for range,iterate by length,try all split points,combine answers from smaller ranges. Time is usually O(n3).

n piles of stones. Two players take turns removing a pile from either end. Find the largest your score. dp[i][j]=max score difference(my score. Opponent's)for piles i..j.
Positive means I win. Transition: dp[i][j]=max(piles[i]−dp[i+1][j],piles[j]−dp[i][j−1]). Take left or right,subtract opponent's best. The subtraction handles alternating turns. If dp[0][n−1]>0,first player wins. This is a classic game theory problem that can be solved with interval DP. The state captures optimal play.

Base case: dp[i][i]=piles[i](take the only pile). Fill by increasing length. For each interval,try taking left or right end. The "difference" formulation elegantly handles the zero-sum game. Both players play optimally. Time: O(n2),Space: O(n2). Can reduce space to O(n)since each length only depends on previous length. Fill the table by increasing interval length. Each cell depends on two smaller intervals. This keeps dependencies valid.
Time complexity: O(n2).
Space complexity: O(n2).

units.unit_name: LeetCode 1039 Minimum Score Triangulation of Polygon - Problem Statement
units.text:
Triangulate a convex polygon to find the lowest sum of triangle scores. Score of triangle=product of vertices. dp[i][j]=min cost to triangulate the polygon formed by vertices i to j.
Transition: pick a vertex k between i and j to form triangle(i,k,j). Cost=v[i]⋅v[k]⋅v[j]+dp[i][k]+dp[k][j]. This is Matrix Chain in disguise! The "matrices" are edges,and triangles are the "multiplications".

units.unit_name: LeetCode 1039 Minimum Score Triangulation of Polygon - Implementation
units.text:
Base case: dp[i][i+1]=0(an edge is not a polygon,no triangulation needed). Iterate by polygon size(number of vertices in the sub-polygon). For each interval [i,j] where j−i≥2,try all intermediate vertices k. Time: O(n3),Space: O(n2). Same complexity as Matrix Chain Multiplication. The base case is edges(two adjacent vertices),which need no triangulation. Build up from there.
Time complexity: O(n3).
Space complexity: O(n2).

Use interval DP when: the answer for a range depends on how you split it,and sub-ranges are independent after splitting. Common signals: "partition","cut","split","merge adjacent","remove from ends","parenthesize".
The loop structure is always: iterate by length,then by start position,then by split point. Complexity is usually O(n3): O(n2)intervals times O(n)split points. Some problems allow O(n2)with optimizations(Knuth's).

Interval DP works on linear structures: strings,arrays,sequences. But what about hierarchical structures? Trees have natural recursive structure. Each node's answer depends on its children's answers. Tree DP is the next step: instead of intervals,your subproblems are subtrees.
Instead of split points,you have child nodes. The core idea stays the same: break into subproblems,combine answers. The structure just changes from intervals to trees.

You learned interval DP: problems where dp[i][j] represents the answer for range [i,j]. The key is trying all split points and building from shorter to longer ranges.
Matrix Chain taught the basic pattern. Burst Balloons showed reverse thinking("which last?"). Palindrome Partitioning combined interval precomputation with 1D(one-dimensional)DP. When you see a problem about optimally processing a sequence,and choices affect the structure of remaining elements,think interval DP.

So far,your DP(dynamic programming)states lived on arrays or grids. Trees are different. States live at nodes,and the answer at each node depends on its children.
I'll show you how to think about DP on trees. You'll learn the post-order DFS pattern that makes tree DP work,and apply it to classic problems: finding the longest path,finding the largest node selection,and computing answers for every possible root. By the end,you'll know how to set up dp[node] and combine children's values to solve tree problems.

A subtree rooted at node v includes v and all nodes below it. When you compute dp[v],you only need information from v's subtree. This is why tree DP works: each subtree is independent.
Once you know the answer for every child's subtree,you can compute the answer for the parent. Leaves have trivial subtrees(just themselves),so they're your base cases. If you try to compute dp[v] before computing dp[child],you'll be missing the values you need. The order matters.

Here's the core pattern: dp[v]=some combination of dp[child] for all children of v. For leaves(nodes with no children),dp[leaf] comes directly from the node's properties.
For internal nodes,you combine children's dp values according to the problem's logic. The answer is usually dp[root],representing the solution for the entire tree. Sometimes you track a global maximum across all nodes instead.

Post-order DFS visits children before the parent. When you return from recursing on all children,their dp values are ready. You combine them to compute dp[parent].
Pseudocode


```
function dfs(v,parent)for each child in neighbors[v]
        if child≠parent then
            dfs(child,v)dp[v] :=combine children's dp values

```

The parent check prevents going back up the edge you came from. Without it,you'd treat your parent as a child and recurse forever.   Post-order keeps children are processed before their parent. You need child answers to compute the parent answer.

The diameter of a tree is the length of the longest path between any two nodes. The path can start and end anywhere. Example: In a tree with edges 1-2,2-3,2-4,4-5,the diameter is 3.
The longest path is 3→2→4→5(or 1→2→4→5). Before reading on,think about this: the longest path either stays entirely in one subtree,or it passes through some node connecting two subtrees. How would you use this?

Every path in a tree passes through some "highest" node,the node closest to the root that lies on the path. At this node,the path connects two downward branches.
So for each node,you can ask: what's the longest path that passes through me? That path goes down into one child's subtree,back up through me,and down into another child's subtree. The diameter is the maximum of these "path through node" values across all nodes.

dp[v]=the length of the longest path starting at v and going down into v's subtree. For a leaf,dp[leaf]=0(no edges going down).
For internal nodes,dp[v]=1+max(dp[child])over all children. At each node v,the longest path through v combines the two deepest downward paths: dp[child1​]+dp[child2​]+2. Track this in a global variable as you go.

Here's the full solution:
Pseudocode


```
function treeDiameter(adj,n)diameter :=0
    function dfs(v,parent)max1 :=0,max2 :=0
        // two longest paths down
        for each child in adj[v]
            if child≠parent then
                depth :=dfs(child,v)+1
                if depth > max1 then
                    max2 :=max1
                    max1 :=depth
                else if depth > max2 then
                    max2 :=depth
        diameter :=max(diameter,max1+max2)
return max1
    dfs(0,-1)
return diameter

```

Time: O(n). You visit each node once. The trick is tracking the two longest paths to compute the diameter through that node. Space: O(n)for adjacency list plus recursion stack.
Time complexity: O(n).
Space complexity: O(n).

Trace diameter on a tree: 1-2-3-4 with 5 attached to 2. The diameter is the path 4-3-2-5 or 4-3-2-1(length 3). At each node,compute depth1 and depth2=two longest paths going down.
The candidate diameter through this node is depth1+depth2. At node 2: paths down are to 1(length 1),to 5(length 1),and to 3-4(length 2). Two longest: 2 and 1. Candidate: 2+1=3. The global maximum of all candidates gives the diameter. Post-order traversal keeps children are processed before parents.

The diameter DP gives the length,but what about the actual path? Track which children give the two longest depths. At the node where the maximum occurs,you have two endpoints.
From each,trace back through the tree. Alternative: run BFS from any node to find the farthest node u. Then BFS from u to find the farthest node v. The path u→v is the diameter. The two-BFS approach is simpler to implement when you need the actual path,not just the length.

units.unit_name: LeetCode 337 House Robber III - Problem Statement
units.text:
Houses form a binary tree. Each house has money. You cannot rob two directly connected houses(parent and child). Find the maximum money you can rob. Example: Tree [3,2,3,null,3,null,1].
If you rob the root(3),you cannot rob its children(2 and 3). The best is 3+3+1=7: take the root and two grandchildren. This is House Robber on a tree. For each node,you decide: rob it or skip it?

units.unit_name: LeetCode 337 House Robber III - Key Observation
units.text:
If you rob node v,you get v's money but cannot rob v's children. You can still rob grandchildren. If you skip node v,you get no money from v,but you're free to rob or skip each child independently.
This creates a dependency: your choice at v constrains your choices at v's children. You need to track both possibilities. For each node,store 2 values: the best result when you rob it and the best when you skip it.

units.unit_name: LeetCode 337 House Robber III - State Design
units.text:
For each node,compute two values: rob[v]=max money if you rob v,and skip[v]=max money if you skip v.
Transitions: rob[v]=v.val+skip[left]+skip[right],and skip[v]=max(rob[left],skip[left])+max(rob[right],skip[right]).
Answer: max(rob[root],skip[root]).

Pseudocode


```
function rob(root)function dfs(node)
if node is null then
            return(0,0)//(rob,skip)left :=dfs(node.left)right :=dfs(node.right)rob_this :=node.val+left.skip+right.skip
        skip_this :=max(left.rob,left.skip)+max(right.rob,right.skip)return(rob_this,skip_this)result :=dfs(root)
return max(result.rob,result.skip)
```

Each node is visited once. Returning a pair avoids storing a separate memo array.
To verify your understanding,pick a small tree and trace through the code by hand. Write down the  `(rob,skip)`  pair at each node.
Time: O(n). Space: O(h)for the recursion stack,where h is the tree height.

Rob houses on a tree where adjacent nodes can't both be robbed. Each node returns(rob,skip): max money if robbed vs skipped. Example tree: root=3,left=2,right=3,left.right=3,right.right=1. Post-order builds answers bottom-up. At leaves:(value,0). At node 2 with child 3: rob=2+skipchild​,skip=max(robchild​,skipchild​). So(2+0,3)=(2,3). At root 3: left=(2,3),right=(3,1). Rob=3+3+1=7. Skip=max(2,3)+max(3,1)=3+3=6.
Answer: max(7,6)=7.

What if there's a cooldown: after robbing a node,you must skip at least one level before robbing again? Now you need three states:(rob,skip1,skip2+). skip1=just skipped one level,skip2+=skipped two or more.
Transition: rob=value+skip2+children​. skip1=robchildren​. skip2+=max(skip1,skip2+)children​. This pattern generalizes: more constraints=more states. The tree structure stays the same; only the state tuple grows.

Some problems ask: for each node,what's the answer if that node were the root? Naive approach: run tree DP n times,once per root. That's O(n2). Rerooting does it in O(n)using two passes.
First,compute answers with an arbitrary root. Then,"move" the root to each neighbor efficiently by adjusting the dp values. When does root choice matter? If dp[node] depends on the direction of parent-child relationships,different roots give different answers.

units.unit_name: LeetCode 834 Sum of Distances in Tree - Problem Statement
units.text:
Given a tree,for each node i,compute the sum of distances from i to all other nodes. Distance=number of edges. Example: Tree with edges [[0,1],[0,2],[2,3],[2,4],[2,5]].
Answer: [8,12,6,10,10,10]. Node 2 has the smallest sum(6)because it's most central. With naive BFS from each node,you'd get O(n2). Let's solve it in O(n)with rerooting.

questions.title: Sum of Distances in Tree - Read Statement

units.unit_name: LeetCode 834 Sum of Distances in Tree - Key Observation
units.text:
When you move the root from node p to its child c:
1. Nodes in c's subtree get 1 closer(they're now below the root instead of 2 edges away).
2. All other nodes get 1 farther(they have to go through c now). If c's subtree has count[c] nodes,then n−count[c] nodes get farther. So: answer[c]=answer[p]−count[c]+(n−count[c])=answer[p]+n−2⋅count[c].

units.unit_name: LeetCode 834 Sum of Distances in Tree - Two Passes
units.text:
Pass 1(down): Root at node 0. Compute count[v]=nodes in v's subtree,and sum[v]=sum of distances from v to all nodes in its subtree. count[v]=1+∑count[child] and sum[v]=∑(sum[child]+count[child]).
Pass 2(up): Starting from root,propagate answers to children using the formula from the previous unit.
The first pass collects information from leaves to root. The second pass distributes answers from root to leaves.

units.unit_name: LeetCode 834 Sum of Distances in Tree - Implementation
units.text:
Pseudocode


```
function sumOfDistances(n,edges)// Build adjacency list
    // Pass 1(down): compute count[v] and sum[v]
    dfs_down(0,-1)// Pass 2(up): propagate answers
    ans[0]=sum[0]
    dfs_up(0,-1)
return ans

```

Two passes using the rerooting technique. One pass down collects subtree info,one pass up distributes answers.
Time: O(n). Space: O(n).

questions.title: Sum of Distances in Tree - Implement Solution

units.unit_name: LeetCode 834 Sum of Distances in Tree - Walkthrough
units.text:
Compute sum of distances from each node to all others. Naive: O(n2). Rerooting: O(n). Pass 1(post-order): compute subtreeSize[v] and subtreeSum[v]=sum of distances within subtree rooted at v. Pass 2(pre-order): for each node,combine subtree answer with "rest of tree" answer from parent.
When moving root from u to child v: nodes in v's subtree get 1 closer(there are size[v]),nodes outside get 1 farther(there are n−size[v]). New answer=old ± difference.

units.unit_name: LeetCode 124 Binary Tree Maximum Path Sum - Problem Statement
units.text:
Find the maximum sum path in a binary tree. A path can start and end at any nodes,but must follow edges. observation: at each node,the path either passes through it(joining left and right subtrees)or is entirely in one subtree.
Return two values: maxDown=max sum path starting at this node and going down,maxPath=max path in subtree. At node v: maxDown=max(v.val,v.val+max(left.maxDown,right.maxDown)). maxPath=max(left.maxPath,right.maxPath,v.val+left.maxDown+right.maxDown).

units.unit_name: LeetCode 124 Binary Tree Maximum Path Sum - Implementation
units.text:
Negative nodes complicate things. We might want to exclude a subtree entirely if its contribution is negative. Trick: when computing maxDown,take max(0,childMaxDown). This lets us "cut off" negative subtrees. Base case: null node returns maxDown=0,maxPath=−∞. Leaf returns(val,val). Time: O(n)single post-order traversal. Space: O(h)for recursion stack where h is tree height.
Time complexity: O(n).
Space complexity: O(h).

units.unit_name: LeetCode 310 Minimum Height Trees - Problem Statement
units.text:
Find the root(s)that reduce tree height. There are at most 2 such roots,and they're the tree's centroid(s). Approach 1: Root at each node,compute height,take minimum. O(n2)naive,O(n)with rerooting.
Approach 2: Peel leaves layer by layer. Remove all degree-1 nodes,repeat. The last 1-2 nodes remaining are the answer. Both approaches give the centroid(s). The peeling approach is simpler and naturally handles the "at most 2" property.

units.unit_name: LeetCode 1448 Count Good Nodes in Binary Tree - Problem Statement
units.text:
A node is "good" if no node on the path from root to it has a greater value. Count good nodes. This is a top-down tree DP(pre-order).
Carry the maximum value seen on the path so far. At each node: if val≥pathMax,it's good. Update pathMax=max(pathMax,val)for children. Unlike most tree DP which is bottom-up,this uses parent information,so it's top-down. The state flows from root to leaves.

units.unit_name: LeetCode 1448 Count Good Nodes in Binary Tree - Implementation
units.text:
dfs(node,pathMax): returns count of good nodes in subtree. Add 1 if node.val≥pathMax. Recurse on children with newMax=max(pathMax,node.val). Sum the results. Base case: null returns 0. Leaf returns 1 if good,0 otherwise. Time: O(n),Space: O(h). This is the template for top-down tree DP: carry state down,aggregate results up. This is one of the simpler tree DP problems. It demonstrates the top-down pattern clearly.
Time complexity: O(n).
Space complexity: O(h).

units.unit_name: LeetCode 968 Binary Tree Cameras - Problem Statement
units.text:
Place minimum cameras to monitor all nodes. A camera monitors itself,its parent,and its children. Three states per node: covered(no camera here but covered),hasCamera,notCovered(valid only if parent will cover).
Greedy intuition: place cameras at parents of leaves,not at leaves. Leaves are covered by their parents. DP captures this: leaves return "not covered",parents are forced to place cameras or be covered by their parents.

units.unit_name: LeetCode 968 Binary Tree Cameras - State Transitions
units.text:
state[v]∈{0,1,2}: 0=has camera,1=covered(no camera),2=not covered(needs parent's camera). If any child is not covered(state 2),v must have a camera(state 0).
If any child has camera(state 0),v is covered(state 1). Otherwise,all children are covered(state 1),so v is not covered(state 2)and relies on its parent. The three states capture all possibilities. The transitions make sure every node gets covered without redundant cameras.

Post-order DFS. Return the state of each node. Count cameras(state 0 nodes). Handle root specially: if root returns "not covered",we must add a camera at root. Null children: treat as "covered"(state 1)so they don't force unnecessary cameras. Time: O(n),Space: O(h). Each node decides based on children's states in constant time. Start from leaves and work up. Leaves are never optimal camera locations because their parents cover more.
Time complexity: O(n).
Space complexity: O(h).

The key question: what information from a subtree do we need to make decisions at the parent? Common patterns: single value(subtree sum/max),pair(include/exclude),tuple(multiple mutually exclusive states).
If constraints involve parent-child relationships(no adjacent,must cover),you likely need multiple states per node. Test your state design: can you compute the parent's state from just the children's states? If yes,it's valid. If you need grandchildren info,add more states.

Tree DP works because trees have no cycles. What about DAGs(directed acyclic graphs)? Same principle: process nodes in topological order.
A node's answer depends only on its descendants,which are processed first. Key difference: a node might have multiple parents,so you can't just use post-order. Use explicit topological sort. Examples: longest path in DAG,counting paths,minimum cost paths. All solvable with DP on topological order.

What if you need to answer many queries like "what's the answer if we root at node q"? Naive: O(n)per query=O(nq)total. With rerooting: O(n)preprocessing,O(1)per query. Precompute answers for all nodes with rerooting. Store in an array. Answer queries by lookup.
This pattern works for any associative operation on subtrees. The key is making the "reroot" operation O(1). This is a useful technique for turning O(nq)into O(n+q). Precomputation pays off with many queries.

You've learned DP on explicit tree structures. But what if the "tree" is implicit,defined by the problem's structure? Digit DP treats a number's decimal representation as a kind of tree: each digit is a node,and valid numbers are paths.
The constraint is usually "count numbers \leq N with property X". The DP state tracks position and whether we're still at the limit. Make sure you're comfortable with tree DP before moving on. Digit DP uses similar ideas but in a less visual context.

You learned DP on trees. States live at nodes and flow from children to parents via post-order DFS.
1. The pattern: dp[v] combines dp[child] values. Leaves are base cases.
2. For path problems(diameter),track a global answer for paths passing through each node.
3. For selection problems(House Robber III),track multiple states per node.
4. For "answer at each node" problems,use rerooting: one pass down,one pass up. Tree DP shows up often. The recursive structure of trees makes these problems natural once you see the pattern.

Some problems ask you to visit all cities,assign all workers,or select from all elements. With n items,there are 2n possible subsets. Tracking each subset as a state sounds expensive,but for small n,it's practical.
I'll show you how bitmasks represent subsets as integers. You'll learn to read,write,and iterate over subsets using bit operations. Then you'll apply this to classic problems: Traveling Salesman,Assignment,and Subset Partitioning. By the end,you'll know when bitmask DP(dynamic programming)applies and how to implement it.

Here are the bit operations you'll use constantly. Memorize these patterns:
Pseudocode


```
Check if bit i is set:(mask >> i)& 1
Set bit i: mask |(1 << i)Clear bit i: mask & ~(1 << i)Toggle bit i: mask ^(1 << i)Count set bits: popcount(mask)Lowest set bit: mask &(-mask)Clear lowest set bit: mask &(mask - 1)
```

Each operation runs in O(1). The expression 1<<i creates a number with only bit i set. OR sets bits,AND with complement clears them,XOR toggles them.
Practice until these feel automatic. In contests,you will write them dozens of times. Fumbling with bit manipulation wastes precious minutes and invites subtle bugs.

Let's ground those bit operations with a concrete example. Suppose you have n=4 elements labeled 0,1,2,3. The mask 13 in decimal equals 1101 in binary.
Reading right to left: bit 0 is 1(element 0 included),bit 1 is 0(element 1 excluded),bit 2 is 1(element 2 included),bit 3 is 1(element 3 included).
So mask 13 represents the subset {0,2,3}. Check if element 2 is in the subset:(13>>2)&1=(1101>>2)&1=11&1=1. Yes,it's included. Check element 1:(13>>1)&1=110&1=0. Not included. These operations become automatic with practice.

The core idea: dp[mask]=answer for the subset represented by mask. Your DP(dynamic programming)table has 2n entries,one per subset. Transitions typically add or remove one element,connecting dp[mask] to dp[mask with one bit changed].
You build larger subsets from smaller ones,or vice versa. Time complexity is typically O(n⋅2n)if each state checks n transitions,or O(n2⋅2n)if transitions involve pairs. For n=20,that's 20 million or 400 million operations,both feasible.

Use bitmask DP(dynamic programming)when:
1. You need to track which elements from a small set(n≤20)have been used.
2. The problem involves permutations,matchings,or visiting all items.
3. Brute force is O(n!)but O(2n)is acceptable. Signals: "visit all cities exactly once","assign workers to tasks","partition into subsets". If n>20,2n is too large. 220≈1 million is fine. 225≈33 million is borderline.

Given an array of n integers and a target sum S,determine if any subset of the array sums to exactly S. For example,with array [3,1,4,2] and target 5,the answer is yes because {3,2} and {1,4} both sum to 5. Constraints: n≤20. With n this small,2n≈1 million subsets is feasible to enumerate.
This is the simplest bitmask problem: iterate all subsets,check their sums. Before reading on,try to write the brute-force solution. How would you represent each subset? How would you compute its sum?

Each integer from 0 to 2n−1 represents exactly one subset. The mask tells you which elements to include. Iterate through all masks,and for each one,compute the sum of included elements. For mask=5(binary 101)with array [3,1,4]: bits 0 and 2 are set,so include arr[0]=3 and arr[2]=4. Sum=7.
This is brute-force,but 220 operations is fast enough. No clever DP(dynamic programming)needed here. The bitmask just gives you a clean way to enumerate all subsets without recursion.

Here's the full solution:
Pseudocode


```
function subsetSumExists(arr,target)n :=length of arr
    for mask :=0 to(1 << n)- 1
        sum :=0
        for i :=0 to n - 1
            if(mask >> i)& 1=1 then
                sum :=sum+arr[i]
        if sum=target then
            return true
    return false

```

Time: O(2n⋅n). For each of 2n masks,you check n bits. Space: O(1)extra. This is your first complete bitmask solution. The pattern is simple: iterate masks,extract bits,compute answer.

units.unit_name: LeetCode 1879 Minimum XOR Sum of Two Arrays - Problem Statement
units.text:
Given two arrays nums1 and nums2 of length n,rearrange nums2 to find the smallest XOR sum: ∑i=0n−1​(nums1[i]⊕nums2[p[i]])where p is a permutation. Example: nums1=[1,2],nums2=[2,3]. If we pair 1 with 3 and 2 with 2:(1⊕3)+(2⊕2)=2+0=2.
This is minimum. This is the assignment problem: match each element of nums1 to a unique element of nums2. The bitmask tracks which elements of nums2 have been used.

Here's the trick: process nums1 in order,from index 0 to n−1. When assigning nums1[k],you've already assigned indices 0 to k−1. The mask tells you which elements of nums2 are taken. The number of set bits equals how many nums1 elements have been assigned.
So popcount(mask)tells you which nums1 index comes next. This eliminates the need for a second dimension. You don't need dp[mask][i] because i=popcount(mask).

dp[mask]=minimum cost when the first popcount(mask)elements of nums1 have been assigned to the nums2 elements indicated by mask.
Base case: dp[0]=0. No assignments made,zero cost. Transition: Let k=popcount(mask). For each unset bit j,try assigning nums1[k] to nums2[j]. dp[mask∣(1<<j)]=min(dp[mask∣(1<<j)],dp[mask]+nums1[k]⊕nums2[j])

Here is the full solution for minimum XOR sum:
Pseudocode


```
function minXorSum(nums1,nums2)n :=length of nums1
    INF :=large number
    dp :=array of size 2^n,filled with INF
    dp[0] :=0
    for mask :=0 to 2^n - 1
        if dp[mask]=INF then
            continue
        k :=popcount(mask)
if k >=n then
            continue
        for j :=0 to n - 1
            if(mask >> j)& 1=1 then
                continue
            newMask :=mask |(1 << j)cost :=nums1[k] XOR nums2[j]
            dp[newMask] :=min(dp[newMask],dp[mask]+cost)
return dp[2^n - 1]

```

The trick: popcount(mask)tells you how many elements of nums1 have been assigned. If the mask has k bits set,you are assigning nums1[k] next. This eliminates the need for a second dimension in the DP.
Time: O(n⋅2n). Space: O(2n).

You will solve CSES 1690 - Hamiltonian Flights. This is NOT the Traveling Salesman Problem(TSP).
The difference: TSP finds the minimum cost Hamiltonian cycle(visit all cities,return to start). CSES 1690 counts Hamiltonian paths from city 1 to city n(visit all cities exactly once,fixed endpoints).
You'll use bitmask DP where dp[mask][i]=number of ways to visit the cities in 'mask' and end at city i. The final answer is dp[(1<<n)−1][n−1] modulo 109+7.

When planning the next move,you only care about which cities you've visited and where you are now. The exact path taken doesn't matter. If you've visited {1,2,4} and are at city 4,the remaining problem is the same whether you came via 1→2→4 or 2→1→4.
This overlap is what DP exploits. The bitmask tracks visited cities. Combined with current city,this fully describes the state. That's n⋅2n states instead of n! paths.

dp[mask][last]=minimum cost to visit exactly the cities in mask,ending at city last. Base case: dp[1][0]=0. You start at city 0,having visited only city 0,with zero travel cost.
The mask=1 means only bit 0 is set. Goal: Visit all cities(mask=2n−1),then return to city 0. The answer is minlast​(dp[2n−1][last]+cost[last][0]). You check all possible final cities and add the return flight cost.

From state(mask,last),try going to any unvisited city next. Check if next is unvisited:(mask>>next)∧1=0.
If so,transition to(mask∣(1<<next),next)with added cost cost[last][next]. Process masks in increasing order. Smaller subsets(fewer bits set)must be computed before larger ones,since you build up by adding cities.
This topological order keeps dependencies are satisfied. For each mask,try all n possible last cities and all n possible next cities. That's O(n2)work per mask,giving O(n2⋅2n)total.

Here's the full solution:
Pseudocode


```
function tsp(cost,n)INF :=large number
    dp :=2D(two-dimensional)array [2^n][n],filled with INF
    dp[1][0] :=0
    // start at city 0
    for mask :=1 to 2^n - 1
        for last :=0 to n - 1
            if dp[mask][last]=INF then
                continue
            if(mask >> last)& 1=0 then
                continue
            for next :=0 to n - 1
                if(mask >> next)& 1=1 then
                    continue
                newMask :=mask |(1 << next)dp[newMask][next] :=min(dp[newMask][next],dp[mask][last]+cost[last][next])ans :=INF
    for last :=0 to n - 1
        ans :=min(ans,dp[2^n - 1][last]+cost[last][0])
return ans if ans < INF else "IMPOSSIBLE"

```

Time: O(n2⋅2n). Space: O(n⋅2n). For n=20,this runs in a few seconds.
Time: O(n2⋅2n). Space: O(n⋅2n).

units.unit_name: LeetCode 698 Partition to K Equal Sum Subsets - Problem Statement
units.text:
Given an array nums and integer k,determine if the array can be divided into k non-empty subsets with equal sum. Example: nums=[4,3,2,3,5,2,1],k=4. Total=20,target per subset=5. Partition: {5},{4,1},{3,2},{3,2}. Return true.
This needs bitmask DP because you must track exactly which elements have been used. Standard sum-based DP can't distinguish different groupings that reach the same partial sum.

questions.title: Partition to K Equal Sum Subsets - Read Statement

units.text:
Build subsets one at a time. For the subset being built,track how much sum you've accumulated. When it reaches target,start a new subset.
The mask tells you which elements are used. The partial sum(mod target)tells you how close the current subset is to complete. observation: dp[mask] can store the current partial sum. When dp[mask]=0,you've just completed a subset and are starting fresh.

Here: full solution for partitioning into K equal subsets:
Pseudocode


```
function canPartitionKSubsets(nums,k)total :=sum of nums
    if total%k≠0 then
        return false
    target :=total / k
    n :=length of nums
    dp :=array of size 2^n,filled with -1
    dp[0] :=0
    for mask :=0 to 2^n - 1
        if dp[mask]=-1 then
            continue
        for i :=0 to n - 1
            if(mask >> i)& 1=1 then
                continue
            if dp[mask]+nums[i] > target then
                continue
            newMask :=mask |(1 << i)dp[newMask] :=(dp[mask]+nums[i])%target
    return dp[2^n - 1]=0

```

When the sum hits target,the modulo operation resets it to 0,starting a fresh subset.
Time: O(n⋅2n). Space: O(2n).
Time: O(n⋅2n). Space: O(2n).

questions.title: Partition to K Equal Sum Subsets - Implement Solution

units.text:
You've learned to use bitmasks as DP states. Now here's a different problem: given values f[0],f[1],…,f[2n−1],compute for each mask the sum of f[submask] over all submasks of that mask. Example: if n=2 and mask=3(binary 11),its submasks are {0,1,2,3}(binary 00,01,10,11).
You need the sum f[0]+f[1]+f[2]+f[3]. The naive approach iterates all submasks for each mask. That's O(3n). With n=20,that's 3.5 billion operations. Too slow. SOS DP does it in O(n⋅2n),about 20 million operations for n=20.

Instead of iterating subsets directly,build them incrementally. Process one bit position at a time. For each bit,either keep it or turn it off(if it was on).
Define dp[mask][i]=sum of f[submask] where submask matches mask in bits i+1 through n−1,and can differ in bits 0 through i. Base case: dp[mask][−1]=f[mask]. No bits are flexible yet,so the only "submask" is mask itself. Final answer: dp[mask][n−1] gives the sum over all submasks.

To compute dp[mask][i],consider bit i of mask:
1. If bit i is 0 in mask: submasks must also have bit i=0. So dp[mask][i]=dp[mask][i−1]. Nothing new to add.
2. If bit i is 1 in mask: submasks can have bit i as 0 or 1. Those with bit i=1 contribute dp[mask][i−1]. Those with bit i=0 contribute dp[mask⊕2i][i−1](flip bit i off). Combined: dp[mask][i]=dp[mask][i−1]+dp[mask⊕2i][i−1] when bit i is set.

You don't need to store all dp[mask][i] values. Process bits from 0 to n−1. When computing layer i,you only need layer i−1. Better: update in-place.
Start with dp[mask]=f[mask]. For each bit i from 0 to n−1,update: if bit i is set in mask,add dp[mask⊕2i] to dp[mask]. The order matters: process masks with bit i set after masks without it,or iterate masks in increasing order. This keeps dp[mask⊕2i] at its layer i−1 value when you read it.

Here is the SOS DP implementation:
Pseudocode


```
// dp[mask] starts as f[mask]
for mask from 0 to(1 << n)- 1
    dp[mask] :=f[mask]

// Process one bit at a time
for i from 0 to n - 1
    for mask from 0 to(1 << n)- 1
        if(mask >> i)& 1=1 then
            dp[mask] :=dp[mask]+dp[mask xor(1 << i)]

```

Three loops,and you're done. The outer loop picks a bit position. The inner loop checks every mask. If bit i is set in that mask,you add the contribution from the mask with bit i turned off. After all n rounds,`dp[mask]`  holds the sum of  `f[submask]`  over every submask of mask.
Why does iterating masks in increasing order work? When you read  `dp[mask xor(1 << i)]`,that mask has bit i off. It was not updated in this round(the  `if`  skipped it),so it still holds the value from the previous round. This is exactly what the recurrence needs.
Time complexity: O(n⋅2n).
Space complexity: O(2n).

Given n integers,compute three counts for each element x:
1. How many elements y satisfy x∣y=x?(OR equals x)2. How many elements y satisfy x∧y=x?(AND equals x)3. How many elements y satisfy x∧y=0?(AND is non-zero)Constraints: n≤2×105,values up to 106. The brute force O(n2)is too slow. Each query type needs a different SOS DP formulation.

Let's decode what each query asks as subsets:
1. x∣y=x means every bit of y is also in x. So y is a subset of x. Count subsets of x in the array.
2. x∧y=x means every bit of x is also in y. So x is a subset of y,meaning y is a superset of x. Count supersets of x.
3. x∧y=0 means x and y share at least one bit. The complement: x∧y=0 means y is a subset of ∼x(the bitwise complement). So count n minus subsets of ∼x.

Build a frequency array f[mask] counting occurrences of each value. Then run two SOS passes:
Pseudocode


```
function solveBitProblem(nums,maxVal)n :=bits needed for maxVal
    f :=frequency array of size 2^n
    sub :=copy of f
    for i :=0 to n - 1
        for mask :=0 to 2^n - 1
            if(mask >> i)& 1 then
                sub[mask]+=sub[mask ^(1 << i)]
    sup :=copy of f
    for i :=0 to n - 1
        for mask :=0 to 2^n - 1
            if not((mask >> i)& 1)then
                sup[mask]+=sup[mask |(1 << i)]
    ALL :=2^n - 1
    for x in nums
        print(sub[x],sup[x],len(nums)- sub[ALL ^ x])
```

Time: O(n⋅2n)Each SOS pass,where n=log(max). Space: O(2n).
Time: O(n⋅2n). Space: O(2n).

Sometimes you need sum over supersets instead of subsets: Each mask,compute ∑f[supermask] where supermask contains all bits of mask. This is the dual problem to what we just solved. The idea is symmetric.
Flip the update direction: instead of "if bit is set,add the version with bit off," do "if bit is off,add the version with bit on."  `pseudocode for i :=0 to n - 1 for mask :=0 to 2^n - 1 if(mask &(1 << i))=0 then dp[mask] :=dp[mask]+dp[mask |(1 << i)]` 
Now dp[mask] holds the sum over all supersets of mask. Same complexity: O(n⋅2n). This version is useful When want to aggregate over all masks that include a required set of bits.

Use SOS DP when you see these patterns:
1. "For each subset,aggregate over all its subsets(or supersets)." Direct SOS application.
2. "Count pairs where one bitmask is a subset of another." Precompute subset sums,then query.
3. "Find if there exists a subset with property X contained in mask." Store boolean flags,SOS computes OR. The constraint n≤20(or values up to 220)signals bitmask techniques. If the problem involves subset/superset relationships,think SOS.

Four patterns you've seen:
1. State compression: When tracking "which elements used," represent it as a bitmask. The state space becomes O(2n)subsets.
2. Transitions via bit operations: Set bits,clear bits,check bits. These run in O(1)and keep your code clean.
3. Assignment problems: When matching n items to n slots in order,dp[mask] alone suffices. Process items one by one.
4. SOS DP: When you need sums over all subsets(or supersets),don't iterate submasks naively. Use the O(n⋅2n)technique. The constraint n≤20 is your signal. Anything larger and 2n won't fit in time or memory.

You learned Bitmask DP for subset-based states.
1. A bitmask represents a subset as an integer. Bit i set means element i is included. This compresses exponential state spaces into manageable O(2n).
2. TSP(Traveling Salesman Problem)-style problems need dp[mask][last]: which cities visited,which city you're at. Assignment problems often need just dp[mask].
3. SOS DP handles subset aggregation in O(n⋅2n). Process one bit at a time,building from smaller flexibility to full subset sums.
When n≤20 and track subsets,think bitmask. The exponential state space is tractable for small n.

How many integers from 1 to 1018 have all distinct digits? You can't loop through them. There are too many. But you can count them by building numbers digit by digit. Digit DP(dynamic programming)solves counting problems over huge ranges of numbers.
Instead of checking each number,you construct valid numbers one digit at a time,tracking constraints as you go. I'll show you how to count numbers satisfying digit-based properties. By the end,you'll handle problems that would otherwise be impossible to brute force.

Digit DP(dynamic programming)processes numbers as sequences of digits. To count integers from 1 to N,you represent N as a string and build valid numbers position by position.
For example,if N=325,you process three positions. At each position,you decide which digit to place,subject to constraints. Some digits might violate your condition(like repeating a digit when you want all distinct).
The observation: instead of iterating over all numbers,you iterate over all ways to fill digit positions. This turns O(N)into O(digits×states),which is tiny even for N=1018.

The tight bound tracks whether you're still constrained by the upper limit N. If your first two digits match N's first two digits exactly,you're "tight" and can't exceed N's third digit. Example: N=325. If you've placed 32,you're tight. Your next digit can only be 0-5.
But if you placed 31,you're no longer tight. Your next digit can be 0-9. Once you place a digit smaller than N's corresponding digit,you become "loose" and stay loose forever. You can never exceed N anymore,so all future digits are free.

When counting up to N=325,you also count 1,42,99,numbers with fewer digits. How do you handle this in digit DP(dynamic programming)? Track a "started" flag. Before placing the first non-zero digit,you haven't started yet. A leading zero means you're still choosing whether to use this position.
If started is false and you place 0,you're skipping this position(like choosing a 2-digit number instead of 3-digit). If you place non-zero,started becomes true and stays true.

Most digit DP problems use this state: dp(pos,tight,started,...extra)pos: current digit position(0 to length-1,left to right). tight: are we still bounded by N? started: have we placed a non-zero digit yet? extra: problem-specific state(used digits,digit sum,remainder,etc.).
To compute dp,try each valid digit at pos,updates the state,and recurses. Base case: when pos equals the length,return 1 if valid,0 otherwise.

Digit DP naturally counts from 0 to N. But problems often ask for a range [L,R]. Use inclusion-exclusion: count(L,R)=count(0,R)−count(0,L−1)Compute the count up to R,subtract the count up to L−1.
You run digit DP twice with different limits. Watch for edge cases: if L=0,you might need to handle it specially since L−1=−1 is invalid. Some problems exclude 0 from the count.

units.unit_name: LeetCode 902 Numbers At Most N Given Digit Set - Problem Statement
units.text:
Given an array of digits(like [1,3,5,7])and an integer n,return the count of positive integers that can be formed using only those digits and are at most n. For digits=[1,3,5,7] and n=100,you can form: 1,3,5,7(one-digit),11,13,15,...,77(two-digit),but nothing three-digit since 111>100.
Answer: 20. This is a clean introduction to digit DP. The only constraint is which digits you can use.

questions.title: Numbers At Most N Given Digit Set - Read Statement

units.text:
You can only use digits from the given set. At each position,instead of trying 0-9,you try only the allowed digits. observation: numbers shorter than n are always valid(if they use allowed digits).
A 2-digit number made from [1,3,5,7] is≤100. For numbers with the same length as n,you need the tight bound logic. Can you place digit d at position pos without exceeding n? Transform the problem: count valid numbers by building them digit by digit from left to right.

Define dp(pos,tight)=count of valid numbers using positions pos to end,given whether we're tight. No "started" flag needed here because all digits in the set are non-zero(the problem guarantees digits are from 1-9).
Every number we form is positive. The answer is the sum of:(1)all numbers shorter than n,plus(2)dp(0,true)for numbers with the same length as n. This state captures all the information needed to count valid numbers from position pos onward.

At position pos with tight bound: If tight: you can only place digits≤n[pos]. For each allowed digit d≤n[pos],recurse with tight=(d==n[pos]).
If not tight: you can place any allowed digit. Recurse with tight=false. To compute dp(pos,tight),loop through the digit set and checks if each digit is valid given the current tight status. This formula tells you how to compute dp(pos,tight)from dp values at later positions. Getting this right is the core of DP.

Count numbers≤321 using digits {1,3,5}. Build digit by digit,tracking tightness. Position 0(hundreds): if tight,try 1 and 3(but not 5,since 5>3). If 3,stay tight. If 1,become loose. Position 1(tens): if tight(started with 3),try 1(becomes loose,since 1<2). If loose,try all: 1,3,5. Position 2(units): if tight,try 1(since 1≤1). If loose,try all. Count all valid paths.
Answer: numbers like 111,113,115,131,...,311,313,315 that are≤321.

1. Shorter numbers need separate handling. Count them with simple math: ∣digits∣k for each length k<len.
2. Tight bound controls which digits are valid. When tight,you can't exceed n[pos].
3. Once you place a smaller digit,tight becomes false forever. You have full freedom for remaining positions. This is the simplest digit DP. Next,you'll add more state to track used digits.

What if the digit set doesn't include 0? We can't form numbers like 101. The DP handles this naturally by only trying available digits. What if N=0? The answer is 0(or 1 if we count 0 as valid). Check your base case. What if the digit set is {0}? Only 0 is valid.
But leading zeros mean we only count actual 0,not "00" or "000". What if N< smallest digit? Answer is 0. The DP will find no valid paths at the first position.

units.unit_name: LeetCode 2376 Count Special Integers - Problem Statement
units.text:
Given a positive integer n,return the number of positive integers in [1,n] that have all distinct digits. For n=20: integers 1-10 are all special(single digit or "10"). Integers 11-20: only 11 has repeated digits.
Answer: 19. For n=135: you need to count all numbers up to 135 where no digit repeats. This requires tracking which digits you've used. Before reading the solution,try to identify the base case and recursive relationship yourself.

to make sure all digits are distinct,you must remember which digits you've already placed. A digit can only appear once. How do you track "used digits"? A bitmask.
If bit d is set,digit d has been used. Ten digits means a 10-bit mask. When placing digit d,check if bit d is already set. If yes,skip. If no,set bit d and continue. Take time to understand the problem structure. The recursive relationship often becomes clear once you see how smaller instances relate to larger ones.

Define dp(pos,tight,started,mask)=count of valid special integers from position pos onward. mask: bitmask of used digits. Bit d is 1 if digit d has been placed.
Why "started"? Leading zeros don't count as using digit 0. The number 042 is 42,and 0 isn't "used." You only start tracking the mask once you place a real digit. Choosing what dp tracks is often the hardest part. Each dp value must have enough information to count valid numbers from that position onward.

At each position,try digits 0 to limit(where limit is 9 if not tight,else n[pos]). For each digit d:
1. If started and bit d is set in mask: skip(digit already used).
2. If not started and d=0: recurse with started still false,mask unchanged.
3. Otherwise: recurse with started=true,new mask=mask |(1<<d). The mask grows as you place digits,blocking repeats. This formula shows how dp at position pos depends on dp at position pos+1.

Count numbers≤135 with all distinct digits. Use a bitmask to track used digits. Start: mask=0,tight=true,started=false. Position 0: try 0(leading zero,don't add to mask),1(mask |=2).
If we chose 1(mask=0b10,tight=true): position 1 can be 0,2,3(not 1,it's used). If we choose 3(> limit's 3),invalid. Continue to position 2. Count paths where each digit appears at most once. Bitmask tracks usage in O(1)per transition.

1. Use bitmask to track used elements. Ten digits fit in a 10-bit integer.
2. Leading zeros need special handling. Don't mark 0 as used until you've started.
3. The base case checks "started." An empty number(all leading zeros)doesn't count. This pattern(digit DP+bitmask)appears whenever you need to track which digits or elements you've used. Apply these patterns when you encounter similar problems.

units.unit_name: LeetCode 600 Non-negative Integers without Consecutive Ones - Problem Statement
units.text:
Given a positive integer n,return the number of non-negative integers≤n whose binary representation does not contain consecutive ones. For n=5(binary 101): valid numbers are 0,1,2,4,5(binary 0,1,10,100,101). Invalid: 3(binary 11).
Answer: 5. This is digit DP on binary representation. Instead of base 10,you work in base 2. Before reading the solution,try to identify the base case and recursive relationship yourself.

questions.title: Non-negative Integers without Consecutive Ones - Read Statement

units.text:
Convert n to binary. Now each "digit" is 0 or 1. At each position,you choose 0 or 1(subject to tight bound). The constraint: no two consecutive 1s. You need to track the previous bit.
If the previous bit was 1,you can't place 1 again. State:(pos,tight,prevBit). The prevBit tells you whether placing 1 at this position would create 11. Understanding the problem structure is half the battle.

Define dp(pos,tight,prev)=count of valid binary numbers from position pos onward,given the previous bit was prev. prev=1: the last placed bit was 1.
You can only place 0 now. prev=0: the last placed bit was 0(or no bit yet). You can place 0 or 1. Base case: when pos reaches the end,return 1. You've built a valid number. This state captures all the information needed to count valid binary numbers from position pos onward.

At position pos:
1. You can always place 0. Recurse with prev=0.
2. You can place 1 only if prev =1 and(not tight or n[pos]=1). If prev was 1 and you try to place 1,you'd create 11. Skip that branch. The tight logic works the same as base 10: if tight and n[pos]=0,you can't place 1. This formula tells you how to compute dp(pos,tight,prev)from dp values at later positions. Getting this right is the core of DP.

Pseudocode


```
function findIntegers(n)binary :=binary representation of n as string
len :=length of binary
dp :=3D array [len+1][2][3],all -1
return solve(0,true,-1,binary)function solve(pos,tight,prev,binary)
if pos=length of binary then return 1
t :=1 if tight else 0
p :=prev+1
if dp[pos][t][p] .=-1 then return dp[pos][t][p]
limit :=binary[pos] if tight else 1
count :=0
count :=count+solve(pos+1,tight and binary[pos]=0,0,binary)
if prev .=1 and limit >=1 then
count :=count+solve(pos+1,tight and binary[pos]=1,1,binary)dp[pos][t][p] :=count
return count

```

Work in binary. Track the previous bit to avoid consecutive 1s. The tight constraint limits choices based on n's bits.
Time: O(logn). Space: O(logn).

questions.title: Non-negative Integers without Consecutive Ones - Implement Solution

units.text:
Count binary numbers≤101(5 in decimal)with no consecutive 1s. Track the previous digit. N=101 in binary. Position 0: try 0(loose)or 1(tight).
If 1,prevDigit=1. Position 1: if prev=1 and tight,can only try 0(since 1 would make consecutive 1s). If loose,try both but skip 1 if prev=1. Position 2: continue. Valid numbers: 000,001,010,100,101. That's 5 numbers. The DP counts all valid paths.

1. Digit DP works in any base. Binary is just base 2 with digits 0 and 1.
2. Track the previous digit when constraints involve adjacent positions.
3. The dp table is tiny in binary: O(logn×2×2)states. Binary digit DP appears in problems about binary representations,XOR constraints,and bit manipulation combined with counting. Apply these patterns when you encounter similar problems.

What if we allow up to k−1 consecutive 1s,but not k or more? Track the last k−1 digits,not just the last one. State:(pos,tight,lastKDigits)where lastKDigits is a bitmask of the last k−1 positions. Transition: shift the mask left,add new digit,check if all k digits are 1(invalid). For k=3 in base 10: track last 2 digits. State space: O(logN⋅2⋅10k−1). Gets expensive for large k.
Time complexity: O(logN⋅2⋅10k−1). Space complexity: O(logN⋅2⋅10k−1)for the dp table.

When you see a problem counting numbers in a range with digit-based constraints,think digit DP. The pattern:
1. Convert the limit to a string(base 10)or binary.
2. State:(pos,tight,started,...extra)where extra depends on the constraint.
3. Transition: try each valid digit,update state,recurse.
4. Base case: return 1 when you've placed all digits(and satisfied constraints). Common extra states: bitmask(distinct digits),running sum(digit sum),remainder(divisibility),previous digit(adjacency constraints).

Count numbers in [L,R] whose digit sum equals S. Classic digit DP with sum tracking. State:(pos,tight,currentSum).
Transition: try each digit,add to currentSum,check against S at the end. improvement: if currentSum>S,prune. At position pos,max remaining sum is 9⋅(n−pos). If currentSum+maxRemaining<S,prune. Answer=count(R)−count(L−1). Handle L=0 carefully.

dp[pos][tight][sum]=count of valid completions. Base case: pos=n returns(sum==S)?1:0. Memoize only when tight=false. When tight,the valid digit range depends on the specific limit,breaking reuse. Alternative: memoize all states with limit baked in. Use dp[pos][tight][sum] where tight is binary. Trade space for simplicity. Time: O(n⋅S⋅10)where n is digit count. Space: O(n⋅S)with memoization.
Time complexity: O(logN⋅S).
Space complexity: O(logN⋅S).

A stepping number has adjacent digits differing by exactly 1. Count stepping numbers in [L,R]. State:(pos,tight,lastDigit,started).
Transition: if ∣newDigit−lastDigit∣=1,valid. Handle leading zeros: before placing the first non-zero digit,any digit is valid. Track started flag. Examples: 123,321,12321 are stepping. 135,100,122 are not. The stepping constraint only involves adjacent digits. Track the last digit placed to check the constraint.

When started=false: placing 0 keeps started=false. Placing 1-9 sets started=true and lastDigit. When started=true: only place digits within 1 of lastDigit. Update lastDigit for next position. Edge case: what's the first digit of 0? We define single-digit numbers as valid(no adjacent pair to check). Time: O(n⋅2⋅10⋅2)=O(n)where n=log10​N. The constants are small. Space: O(logN⋅10)for memoization table.
Time complexity: O(logN⋅10).
Space complexity: O(logN).

units.unit_name: Challenge: Digit DP on Multiple Numbers
units.text:
Count pairs(a,b)where a≤X,b≤Y,and a+b has some property. This is 2D digit DP. Build a and b simultaneously,digit by digit.
State:(pos,tightA,tightB,carry,...). The carry matters: when checking if a+b has no digit > 5,you need to know if there's a carry from previous positions. Complexity explodes: O(n⋅2⋅2⋅2⋅additionalStates). Use only when necessary.

units.unit_name: Pattern - When to Use Digit DP
units.text:
Use digit DP when: counting integers in a range with digit-based constraints,and the range is too large to enumerate. Signals: "count numbers in [L,R]","digit sum","no repeated digits","digits in increasing order","divisible by K".
Won't work when: the constraint involves the number's value(not digits),like "count primes". Primality doesn't decompose by digits. Divisibility is a special case: track nummodK as you build. Since(10⋅x+d)modK=(10xmodK⋅10+d)modK,it works.

Digit DP counts over a constraint space defined by digits. Next,we'll count over a constraint space defined by game states. Game Theory DP models two-player games. Instead of "how many numbers",you'll answer "who wins with optimal play". The idea is the same: define states,compute transitions,propagate answers.
