But the transition is adversarial: your opponent picks the worst option for you. Make sure you understand digit DP's state design. Game theory DP adds complexity with min-max alternation.

You've learned digit DP. You can now:
1. Count numbers in huge ranges without brute force.
2. Handle tight bounds to stay within limits.
3. Track used digits with bitmasks.
4. Apply digit DP in binary for bit-based constraints. The observation: build numbers digit by digit,tracking just enough state to enforce your constraints. This turns impossible O(N)problems into easy O(logN×states)solutions. For training,solve the problems here's problemset. Then move on to Game Theory DP where you'll handle two-player games.

Two players. They take turns. Both play perfectly. Who wins? I'll show you how to model games as DP(dynamic programming)problems. You'll learn the minimax principle: on my turn I find the largest,on your turn you reduce.
The DP state captures the game position,and transitions represent possible moves. By the end,you'll solve problems where two players compete over arrays,stones,or numbers,and you'll know how to compute the winner when both sides play optimally.

"Optimal play" means both players make the best possible move at every turn. No mistakes. No random choices. Each player assumes the opponent will also play perfectly.
This changes everything. In real games,you might hope your opponent blunders. In Game Theory DP,you assume they won't. Your strategy must work against a perfect opponent. If a problem says "both players play optimally," that's your signal: this is Game Theory DP.

When the game involves scores(not just win/lose),use minimax: I find the largest my score,you find the largest yours. Since your gain is my loss,you reducing my outcome is the same as you finding the largest yours. The trick: track the score difference. Let dp[state]=best(my score−opponent's score)from this state.
When I move,I pick the max. When opponent moves,they pick the min(which flips signs). Result: dp[state]>0 means I win,dp[state]<0 means I lose,dp[state]=0 means tie.

units.unit_name: LeetCode 486 Predict the Winner - Problem Statement
units.text:
Two players take turns picking numbers from either end of an array. Each player adds the picked number to their score. Both play optimally. Can Player 1 win(or tie)? Example: nums=[1,5,2]. Player 1 picks 2,Player 2 picks 5,Player 1 picks 1. Scores: 3 vs 5. Player 1 loses.
But if Player 1 picks 1 first,then 5... try it yourself. This is the classic Game Theory DP problem. The array shrinks from both ends,and we need to track who's winning at each subarray.

units.unit_name: LeetCode 486 Predict the Winner - Key Idea
units.text:
Don't track two separate scores. Track the difference: dp[i][j]=maximum(current player's score−opponent's score)for subarray [i,j].
Why difference? When I pick,I gain points. Then it's opponent's turn,and their "best difference" becomes my negative. The signs flip automatically. Player 1 wins if dp[0][n−1]≥0: their advantage over the full array is non-negative. This single-number state avoids tracking both players' scores separately.

units.unit_name: LeetCode 486 Predict the Winner - State Design
units.text:
dp[i][j]=the maximum score difference the current player can achieve on subarray nums[i..j]. Positive means current player wins on this subarray,negative means they lose. Base case: dp[i][i]=nums[i]. Only one element left. Current player takes it,and the difference equals that value since opponent gets nothing from this subarray.
This is interval DP: we compute answers for smaller ranges first,then build up to larger ranges. Process subarrays of length 1,then 2,then 3,and so on. The final answer is dp[0][n−1].

Current player has two choices: take nums[i](left)or take nums[j](right). If I take left: I gain nums[i],then opponent plays optimally on [i+1,j]. My net: nums[i]−dp[i+1][j]. The subtraction is because opponent's best difference becomes my disadvantage.
If I take right: I gain nums[j],opponent plays on [i,j−1]. My net: nums[j]−dp[i][j−1]. I pick the better option: dp[i][j]=max(nums[i]−dp[i+1][j],nums[j]−dp[i][j−1]).

Here's the full solution:
Pseudocode


```
function predictTheWinner(nums)n :=length of nums
    dp :=2D(two-dimensional)array of size n×n
    // Base case: single elements
    for i :=0 to n - 1
        dp[i][i] :=nums[i]
    // Fill for increasing lengths
    for length :=2 to n
        for i :=0 to n - length
            j :=i+length - 1
            takeLeft :=nums[i] - dp[i+1][j]
            takeRight :=nums[j] - dp[i][j - 1]
            dp[i][j] :=max(takeLeft,takeRight)
return dp[0][n - 1] >=0

```

The outer loop iterates by subarray length. Each length,we try all starting positions i,compute the ending position j=i+length−1,and fill dp[i][j] using already-computed smaller subarrays. This keeps dependencies are satisfied.
Time: O(n2). Space: O(n2).
Time: O(n2). Space: O(n2).

Two players pick from ends of [1,5,2]. Each tries to find the largest their score. Who wins? dp[i][j]=net advantage(my score. Opponent's)for interval [i,j].
Player 1 wants positive,Player 2 wants negative(or zero). dp[0][0]=1,dp[1][1]=5,dp[2][2]=2. For dp[0][1]: take 1 and face−dp[1][1]=−5,or take 5 and face−dp[0][0]=−1. Best: 5−1=4. For dp[0][2]: take 1 and face−dp[1][2],or take 2 and face−dp[0][1]. After computing: Player 1 wins if dp[0][n−1]≥0.

In Predict the Winner with an even-length array,the first player can always at least tie. Why? Split the array into even and odd indices.
First player can always take all even-indexed elements(or all odd-indexed). Strategy: compute sums of even-indexed and odd-indexed elements. Take from whichever set has larger sum. This proves first player \geq second player. But the DP finds the exact margin,not just win/lose.

units.unit_name: LeetCode 1140 Stone Game II - Problem Statement
units.text:
Piles of stones in a row. Players alternate taking X piles from the front,where 1≤X≤2M. After taking,M becomes max(M,X). Initially M=1. Both play optimally.
What's the max stones Player 1 can get? Example: piles=[2,7,9,4,4],M=1. Player 1 can take 1 or 2 piles. Taking 2 gives 2+7=9 stones and sets M=2. Now Player 2 can take 1 to 4 piles... The variable M adds a new dimension to the state. This problem combines interval-style thinking with an extra parameter.

units.unit_name: LeetCode 1140 Stone Game II - Key Idea
units.text:
Unlike Predict the Winner,we can't just track indices. The value of M affects what moves are legal. Two positions with the same remaining piles but different M are different states.
State: dp[i][M]=max stones current player can get starting from pile i with parameter M. Also useful: precompute suffix sums. If I know total stones from pile i onward,and I know what opponent gets,my stones=total−opponent's stones.

units.unit_name: LeetCode 1140 Stone Game II - State Design
units.text:
dp[i][M]=maximum stones the current player can collect from piles [i,n−1] when the parameter is M. Base case: if i≥n,no piles left,return 0.
If i+2M≥n,current player takes all remaining piles. Transition: try taking X piles for X=1 to 2M. Stones I get=suffix[i]−suffix[i+X]. Then opponent plays from i+X with M′=max(M,X). My total=stones taken+(remaining−opponent's best).

Here's the full solution:
Pseudocode


```
function stoneGameII(piles)n :=length of piles
    suffix :=array of size n+1
    suffix[n] :=0
    for i :=n - 1 down to 0
        suffix[i] :=suffix[i+1]+piles[i]
    dp :=2D(two-dimensional)array [n+1][n+1],all -1
    return solve(0,1)function solve(i,M)
if i >=n then
        return 0
    if i+2 * M >=n then
        return suffix[i]
    if dp[i][M] !=-1 then
        return dp[i][M]
    best :=0
    for X :=1 to 2 * M
        opponent :=solve(i+X,max(M,X))myStones :=suffix[i] - opponent
        best :=max(best,myStones)dp[i][M] :=best
    return best

```

Time: O(n3). Space: O(n2).
Time complexity: O(n3).
Space complexity: O(n2).

Piles: [2,7,9,4,4]. Player can take first 1 to 2M piles. M starts at 1,updates to max(M,X)after taking X piles. dp[i][M]=max stones current player can get from piles [i:] with parameter M. From dp[0][1]: can take 1 pile(get 2)or 2 piles(get 9).
After taking X,opponent gets dp[i+X][max(M,X)],I get the rest. observation: I get suffixSum[i]−dp[i+X][max(M,X)]. The opponent's best becomes my loss. Find the largest over valid X.

What if you must take exactly M piles(not up to 2M)? The state and transition change slightly. What if M doesn't update? Then M is constant,and we have a simpler interval DP. What if piles can be taken from either end? Combine with Predict the Winner logic.
Each variant changes the transition but keeps the core idea: track what the current player can guarantee. Each variant teaches a different aspect of game theory DP. Practice all three to build intuition.

units.unit_name: LeetCode 464 Can I Win - Problem Statement
units.text:
Two players pick numbers from 1 to n(each number used at most once). The first player to reach a running total≥target wins. Can the first player guarantee a win? Example: n=10,target=11. Player 1 picks 10. Player 2 picks any number. If Player 2 picks 1,total is 11 and Player 2 wins.
So Player 1 should pick differently... can they force a win? Here's where Bitmask DP meets Game Theory. The set of used numbers is a subset,perfect for bitmask. The game state is which numbers remain.

units.unit_name: LeetCode 464 Can I Win - Key Idea
units.text:
State: which numbers have been used. With n≤20,a bitmask works perfectly. Bit i set means number i+1 is used.
Winning condition: current player can pick a number that either wins immediately(total≥target)or leaves opponent in a losing state. We don't need to track whose turn it is. The current player is always "me" in the recursion. If I can win from this state,the state is winning.

units.unit_name: LeetCode 464 Can I Win - State Design

units.text:
You're building a recursive function with memoization. The state is a bitmask representing which numbers have been picked. The base case checks if the player can win immediately by picking any remaining number.
For the recursive case,you try each unpicked number. If picking that number makes the opponent lose,you win. Otherwise,keep trying other numbers. The memoization key is the bitmask itself.
Since there are 220 possible states and you visit each once,the solution runs in O(2n⋅n)time. You'll cache results in a map where the key is the integer value of the bitmask. This is a classic minimax game theory problem: you win if there exists a move that makes your opponent lose.
Time: O(2n⋅n). Space complexity: O(2n)with bitmask memoization.

Pool of integers 1 to n. Players pick unused numbers. First to reach sum≥target wins. Can Player 1 guarantee a win? State: bitmask of used numbers. dp[mask]=true if current player can win from this state. Example: n=4,target=6. From empty mask,try 1,2,3,or 4.
If taking 4 doesn't win immediately,check if opponent loses from resulting mask. Current sum is computed from mask. If sum+pick≥target,current player wins. Else,recurse and check if any pick leads to opponent's loss.

Three patterns you've seen:
1. Score difference: Track dp[state]=my score−opponent's score. Opponent's gain is my loss(subtraction).
2. Extra parameters: Some games have changing rules(like M in Stone Game II). Include them in the state.
3. Bitmask+game theory: When tracking used elements from a small set,combine bitmask DP with minimax thinking. The core stays the same: I win if I have any move that puts opponent in a losing state. I lose if all my moves put opponent in a winning state.

n stones in a pile. Each turn,remove 1,2,or 3 stones. Whoever takes the last stone wins. dp[i]=true if the player facing i stones can win. Base: dp[0]=false(no stones=you lose). Transition: dp[i]=¬dp[i−1]∨¬dp[i−2]∨¬dp[i−3].
If any move leads to opponent losing,you win. Pattern emerges: dp[i]=(imod4=0). Multiples of 4 are losing positions. This is the classic Nim result.

The DP reveals a pattern: you win if n≡0(mod4). Can we prove this directly? From n≡0(mod4): any move(1,2,or 3)leaves n′≡0(mod4). Opponent wins. From n≡0(mod4): take nmod4 stones. Leave n′≡0(mod4). Opponent loses.
This is the power of game theory DP: compute the pattern with DP,then prove and simplify to O(1). This pattern appears in many game theory problems. Look for cyclic structures in the win/lose sequence.

Start with n. Each turn,pick divisor x of current number where 0<x<n,subtract it. Can't move=lose. dp[1]=false(no valid divisor). dp[2]=true(pick 1,opponent gets 1,loses).
Transition: dp[n]=⋁x∣n,x<n​¬dp[n−x]. Check all proper divisors. Pattern: dp[n]=(nmod2==0). Even numbers win,odd numbers lose. Proof: even can always subtract 1(a divisor),odd - 1=even,opponent wins.

n stones. Each turn,remove a perfect square number of stones(1,4,9,...). Can't move=lose. dp[0]=false. dp[k2]=true for all k(take all,opponent gets 0). Transition: dp[n]=⋁k2≤n​¬dp[n−k2]. Try all valid square moves. Time: O(nn​).
Time complexity: O(nn​). For each state,try O(n​)moves. No simple closed form like Nim or Divisor Game. Unlike Nim,this has no simple closed form. The DP is necessary to determine winning positions.
Space complexity: O(n)for the dp array.

Precompute perfect squares up to n: [1,4,9,16,...]. For each state,iterate through valid squares. Early termination: if any move leads to a losing state,we win. Stop as soon as we find one. Memoization: use array dp[0..n]. Fill bottom-up since dp[n−k2] is always smaller than dp[n]. Space: O(n). Time: O(nn​)worst case,but early termination helps in practice. Precompute all perfect squares up to n. This avoids repeated square root computations in the inner loop.
Time complexity: O(nn​).
Space complexity: O(n).

String of '+' and '-'. Each turn,flip "++" to "--". Can't move=lose. Can first player guarantee a win? State: the string itself(or a hash of it).
Too many states? Use memoization with string keys. Transition: for each "++" substring,flip it,check if opponent loses from the new state. improvement: canonical form. The game decomposes into independent subgames. Use Sprague-Grundy theorem for large inputs.

When a game splits into independent subgames,we can solve each separately and combine with XOR. Each position has a Grundy number(nimber): 0=losing,positive=winning. G(pos)=mex({G(next)})where mex=minimum excludant. For combined games: G(game1+game2)=G(game1)⊕G(game2).
If XOR is 0,current player loses. This is advanced. For most LeetCode problems,simple win/lose DP suffices. Sprague-Grundy helps with complex game structures.

Graph game: cat starts at node 2,mouse at node 1. Mouse moves,then cat. Mouse wins by reaching node 0,cat wins by catching mouse.
State:(mouse,cat,turn). Outcomes: 1=mouse wins,2=cat wins,0=draw(cycle). Transition: on mouse's turn,mouse picks a neighbor. On cat's turn,cat picks a neighbor(except 0). Determine outcome recursively. This is complex: we need to detect draws(cycles in the game graph). Use BFS/DFS with visited states.

States: O(n2⋅2)for n nodes. Each state has an outcome: MOUSE_WIN,CAT_WIN,or UNKNOWN. Base cases: mouse at 0=MOUSE_WIN. Mouse and cat at same node=CAT_WIN. Work backwards: if all of cat's moves lead to MOUSE_WIN,then cat loses. If any of mouse's moves leads to MOUSE_WIN,mouse wins. Remaining UNKNOWN states after propagation=DRAW. Both players can force a cycle. Time: O(n2⋅moves),Space: O(n2)for storing state outcomes.
Time complexity: O(n3).
Space complexity: O(n2).

Signals: two players,alternating turns,both play optimally,determine winner or optimal score. State design: represent the game position. Include whose turn it is if not inferrable.
Track any parameters that affect valid moves. Transition: current player picks the move that maximizes their outcome. Opponent's outcome becomes your loss(in zero-sum games). Common patterns: interval games(pick from ends),pile games(remove stones),graph games(move pieces). The DP structure adapts to each.

What if players don't have opposite goals? Example: both want to find the largest their own score,but scores aren't complementary.
Now you can't use the "my gain=your loss" simplification. Track both players' scores explicitly. State:(position,score1,score2). Transition: current player picks action finding the largest their score. This can lead to non-intuitive equilibria. Players might cooperate on some moves. Game theory gets complex fast.

Three-player games? The turn order matters. Track(position,currentPlayer). Each player maximizes their own outcome.
But alliances can form: two weak players might gang up on the leader. For simple games,treat it as a sequence: each player picks optimally assuming future players also pick optimally. This is rarely tested. Most problems stick to two-player zero-sum games where minimax applies cleanly.

Game Theory DP handles adversarial opponents who play optimally. But what if outcomes are random? Probability DP computes expected values when transitions are stochastic.
Instead of min/max,you take weighted averages. The state design is similar,but transitions sum over possibilities weighted by probabilities. Make sure you understand game theory DP's minimax logic. Probability DP replaces adversarial choices with probabilistic outcomes.

You learned Game Theory DP for two-player games with optimal play.
1. A state is winning if any move leads to a losing state for the opponent. A state is losing if all moves lead to winning states.
2. For score games,track the score difference. The recurrence subtracts the opponent's best result because their gain is your loss.
3. Interval games(pick from ends)use dp[i][j]. Games with extra rules add parameters. Games with used elements use bitmasks. When a problem says "both players play optimally," think minimax. Model the game state,define what winning means,and build the DP from base cases.

So far,every DP(dynamic programming)problem had a clear answer: the minimum cost,the maximum profit,the number of ways. You always knew exactly what would happen at each step.
But what if outcomes are random? A knight moves to one of 8 squares with equal chance. A ball drops left or right with 50%probability each. How do you compute the expected outcome? I'll show you how to handle randomness in DP. You'll learn to compute probabilities and expected values. By the end,you'll handle problems where transitions are probabilistic.

Probability DP(dynamic programming)computes expected values or probabilities instead of counts or extrema. Each dp value represents the probability or expected value at a position,and transitions have probabilities attached.
In standard DP,you might write dp[i]=min(dp[i−1]+cost1​,dp[i−2]+cost2​). In probability DP,you write dp[i]=p1​⋅dp[i−1]+p2​⋅dp[i−2] where p1​ and p2​ are probabilities. The observation: instead of taking min or max,you take a weighted sum. Each transition contributes proportionally to how likely it is.

Expected value is the average outcome if you repeated an experiment infinitely many times. For a dice roll,the expected value is(1+2+3+4+5+6)/6=3.5. You'll never roll 3.5,but it's the long-run average. Formula: E[X]=∑i​pi​⋅xi​ where pi​ is the probability of outcome xi​.
If you flip a coin where heads gives $10 and tails gives $0,the expected value is 0.5⋅10+0.5⋅0=5. Expected value lets you compare random outcomes. "On average,which choice is better?" That's what probability DP answers.

A transition probability is the chance of moving from one position to another. If a knight can make 8 legal moves and picks uniformly at random,each transition has probability 1/8.
In a graph view: nodes are states,edges are transitions,and edge weights are probabilities. All outgoing edges from a state must sum to 1(something must happen). When transitions have different probabilities,you weight them . Rolling a loaded dice where 6 appears 50%of the time changes everything.

Here's the formula you'll use in every probability DP problem: dp[state]=∑next​P(state→next)⋅dp[next] In words: the value at dp[pos] equals the sum over all possible next states of(probability of going there)times(value from there).
This is just expected value applied to DP. For probability problems,dp[state] is the probability of some event. For expected value problems,dp[state] is the expected outcome. The formula stays the same.

units.unit_name: LeetCode 688 Knight Probability in Chessboard - Problem Statement
units.text:
A knight starts at position(row,col)on an n×n chessboard. It makes exactly k moves,where each move is chosen uniformly at random from the 8 possible knight moves. The knight might move off the board.
Once off,it stays off. What's the probability that the knight remains on the board after all k moves? Before reading on,think: what should dp track? What's the base case? How do transitions work?

questions.title: Knight Probability in Chessboard - Read Statement

units.text:
The knight's position matters. A knight in the center has all 8 moves available. A knight in a corner might have only 2 legal moves(the rest go off-board). The number of remaining moves matters too.
With k=0 moves left,if you're on the board,probability is 1. With k=1,it depends on how many of your 8 moves stay on-board. State:(row,col,moves_left). Value: probability of staying on-board from this state to the end.

Define dp[r][c][k]=the probability that a knight starting at(r,c)with k moves remaining stays on the board for all k moves. The answer we want is dp[row][col][K] where(row,col)is the starting position and K is the total number of moves.
Why this state? It captures everything that matters: where you are and how many moves are left. Given your current position and remaining moves,the future probability depends only on these values,not on the path you took to get here(Markov property).

From position(r,c),the knight can move to 8 positions:(r±1,c±2)and(r±2,c±1). Each move is chosen with probability 1/8.
The formula to compute dp[r][c][k]: dp[r][c][k]=81​∑(r′,c′)∈moves​dp[r′][c′][k−1] But wait: what if(r′,c′)is off the board? Then that move contributes 0 to the sum. The knight fell off,so the probability of staying on-board from that path is 0.

1. If(r,c)is off the board: return 0. The knight has fallen off.
2. If k=0 and(r,c)is on the board: return 1. No more moves needed,and you're still on the board. These two cases handle all boundaries. Off-board positions contribute nothing. On-board positions with no moves left contribute certainty. This is where recursion stops. Without it,you get infinite recursion and a stack overflow. Every recursive solution needs at least one base case.

Here's the solution with caching(to avoid recomputing the same dp values):
Pseudocode


```
function knightProbability(n,k,row,col)dp :=3D array [n][n][k+1],all -1
    return solve(row,col,k,n)function solve(r,c,k,n)
if r < 0 or r >=n or c < 0 or c >=n then
        return 0
    if k=0 then
        return 1
    if dp[r][c][k]≠-1 then
        return dp[r][c][k]
    moves :=[(-2,-1),(-2,1),(-1,-2),(-1,2),(1,-2),(1,2),(2,-1),(2,1)]
    prob :=0
    for(dr,dc)in moves
        prob :=prob+solve(r+dr,c+dc,k - 1,n)/ 8
    dp[r][c][k] :=prob
    return prob

```

Time: O(n2⋅k). Space: O(n2⋅k)for the 3D memoization table.

questions.title: Knight Probability in Chessboard - Implement Solution

units.text:
A knight starts at(r,c)on an n×n board. After k moves,what's the probability it's still on the board? dp[step][r][c]=probability of being at(r,c)after step moves,starting from the initial position.
Initial: dp[0][r0​][c0​]=1. Transition: each cell distributes its probability equally to 8 knight-move destinations. After k steps,sum all dp[k][r][c] for valid(r,c). Moves that go off-board contribute to the "off-board" probability.

1. State includes position and moves remaining. Both affect the probability.
2. Transition sums weighted contributions. Each of 8 moves contributes 1/8 of its probability.
3. Off-board states return 0. They're absorbing states where the process "fails." This pattern repeats in almost all probability DP: state captures position and progress,transitions are weighted sums,and boundaries return 0 or 1.

units.unit_name: LeetCode 576 Out of Boundary Paths - Problem Statement
units.text:
You have an m×n grid and a ball at position(startRow,startCol). You can move the ball in 4 directions(up,down,left,right). Find the number of paths to move the ball out of the grid boundary in exactly maxMove moves or fewer. This is counting paths,not computing probability.
But the structure is identical: state is(position,moves remaining),transitions go to 4 neighbors. The twist: paths that go off-grid count toward the answer instead of returning 0.

questions.title: Out of Boundary Paths - Read Statement

units.text:
Define dp[r][c][k]=number of paths starting at(r,c)with exactly k moves remaining that eventually leave the grid.
The difference from knight: we're counting paths,not computing probability. And leaving the grid is success,not failure. We need to sum over k from 1 to maxMove. A path can leave on move 1,move 2,...,or move maxMove. This dp definition has all the information needed to count paths from(r,c)with k moves.

From(r,c)with k moves,you can go up,down,left,or right. The formula: dp[r][c][k]=dp[r−1][c][k−1]+dp[r+1][c][k−1]+dp[r][c−1][k−1]+dp[r][c+1][k−1] Base case: if(r,c)is outside the grid,return 1. That path successfully left.
If k=0 and you're still inside,return 0. No moves left,didn't escape. Don't forget: take results modulo 109+7 since counts can get huge. Each cell receives probability from its four neighbors. The grid acts like a probability distribution over positions.

Here's the memoized solution:
Pseudocode


```
function findPaths
    MOD :=10^9+7
    dp :=3D(three-dimensional)array [m][n][maxMove+1],all -1
    return solve
function solve(r,c,k,m,n,MOD)
if r < 0 or r >=m or c < 0 or c >=n then
        return 1
    if k=0 then
        return 0
    if dp[r][c][k]≠-1 then
        return dp[r][c][k]
    result :=0
    result :=(result+solve(r - 1,c,k - 1,m,n,MOD))%MOD
    result :=(result+solve(r+1,c,k - 1,m,n,MOD))%MOD
    result :=(result+solve(r,c - 1,k - 1,m,n,MOD))%MOD
    result :=(result+solve(r,c+1,k - 1,m,n,MOD))%MOD
    dp[r][c][k] :=result
    return result

```

Leaving the grid returns 1. Staying with no moves returns 0. Sum over 4 directions,modulo 109+7.
Time: O(m⋅n⋅k). Space: O(m⋅n⋅k)for the 3D memoization table.

questions.title: Out of Boundary Paths - Implement Solution

units.text:
Count paths that exit an m×n grid in exactly k moves. Each move goes up/down/left/right. dp[step][r][c]=number of paths at(r,c)after step moves. Transitions: each cell receives from 4 neighbors.
When a move goes out of bounds,it counts as an exit. Track exits at each step. Total exits=sum of paths that exit at each step from 1 to k. Take modulo as counts get large. Use modular arithmetic for counting paths. The mod operation distributes over addition correctly.

1. Counting paths uses the same structure as probability. Sum over transitions instead of weighted sum.
2. What counts as "success" changes the base case. Here,leaving the grid returns 1.
3. Modular arithmetic is needed when counting. Probabilities stay between 0 and 1,but counts explode. Notice how similar the code is to Knight Probability? State and transition structure are identical. Only the base cases and arithmetic differ.

You have two soups,A and B,each with n ml. Each turn,you pick one of four operations with equal probability: serve(100,0),(75,25),(50,50),or(25,75)ml from(A,B).
The process stops when either soup is empty(or both). Return the probability that A empties first,plus half the probability that both empty at the same time. This looks complex. What should dp track? What's tricky about large n?

State is(a,b)=remaining amounts of soup A and B. But if n=109,that's way too many states. observation: all serving amounts are multiples of 25. Divide everything by 25.
State becomes(a/25,b/25). Another observation: soup A is served more on average(100+75+50+25)/4=62.5 vs(0+25+50+75)/4=37.5. For large n,A almost certainly empties first. When n>4800,the probability is so close to 1 that the difference is below 10−5(the required precision). Return 1.0 directly for large n.

After scaling by 25,define dp[a][b]=probability that A empties first(plus half of both empty)given a units of A and b units of B remain. Base cases:
1. If a≤0 and b≤0: both empty simultaneously. Return 0.5.
2. If a≤0: A emptied first. Return 1.
3. If b≤0: B emptied first. Return 0. The answer is dp[n/25][n/25],rounded up if not divisible. This dp definition has all the information needed to compute the probability from(a,b).

Four operations with equal probability 0.25:
1. Serve(4,0): go to state(a−4,b)2. Serve(3,1): go to state(a−3,b−1)3. Serve(2,2): go to state(a−2,b−2)4. Serve(1,3): go to state(a−1,b−3)Transition: dp[a][b]=0.25⋅(dp[a−4][b]+dp[a−3][b−1]+dp[a−2][b−2]+dp[a−1][b−3])This formula tells you how to compute dp[a][b] from dp values at smaller amounts. Getting this right is the core of DP.

Here's the solution with the large-n improvement:
Pseudocode


```
function soupServings(n)
if n > 4800 then
        return 1.0
    n :=(n+24)/ 25
    size :=n+1
    dp :=2D(two-dimensional)array [size][size],all -1
    return solve(n,n)function solve(a,b)
if a≤0 and b≤0 then
        return 0.5
    if a≤0 then
        return 1.0
    if b≤0 then
        return 0.0
    if dp[a][b]≠-1 then
        return dp[a][b]
    result :=0.25×(solve(a-4,b)+solve(a-3,b-1)+solve(a-2,b-2)+solve(a-1,b-3))dp[a][b] :=result
    return result

```

Time: O((n/25)2)for small n. Space: O((n/25)2).
Time complexity: O(N2).
Space complexity: O(N2).

Serve soup from two pots A and B. Each operation reduces(A,B)by one of 4 choices. Find probability A empties first(or tie counts as 0.5).
For small N,use DP: dp[a][b]=probability A empties first from state(a,b). observation: on average,A decreases faster than B(operations favor reducing A). For large N,probability approaches 1. improvement: for N>4800,return 1.0 directly. The error is negligible. This bounds the DP state space.

1. Scale down to reduce state space. Dividing by 25 turned 109 states into 200.
2. Use mathematical observation to shortcut. For large n,one outcome dominates,so return early.
3. Multiple stopping conditions need careful base cases. Both empty vs A empty vs B empty. This problem shows that probability DP often needs problem-specific tricks. Understanding the math helps. Apply these patterns when you encounter similar problems.

When you see a problem with random transitions,think probability DP. The pattern:
1. State: what you need to know(position,resources,moves left).
2. Transition: dp[state]=∑pi​⋅dp[nexti​] where pi​ is the probability of transition i.
3. Base cases: terminal states where probability is 0 or 1(or 0.5 for ties). For counting paths,replace probability weights with counts. For expected values,weight by outcomes instead of just probabilities.

Draw cards 1 to maxPts uniformly. Stop when score≥k. Find probability score is≤n. dp[i]=probability of reaching exactly score i. Start: dp[0]=1. Transition: dp[i]=maxPts1​∑j=1maxPts​dp[i−j],but only for scores <k(we don't draw after reaching k).
Answer: ∑i=kn​dp[i]. Scores from k to n are winning. Use sliding window for efficient sum computation.

Naive: for each dp[i],sum last maxPts values. O(n⋅maxPts)total. improvement: maintain a running sum S of the last maxPts probabilities. dp[i]=S/maxPts. Update S: add dp[i](if i<k,since we keep drawing),subtract dp[i−maxPts](if valid). Final complexity: O(n)time,O(n)space. The sliding window avoids recomputing the sum. The sliding window sum avoids recomputing the last maxPts probabilities. This is a common DP improvement.
Time complexity: O(n).
Space complexity: O(n).

units.unit_name: Random Point in Circle - Problem Statement
units.text:
Generate a random point uniformly in a circle of radius r. This isn't DP,but illustrates probability concepts.
Wrong approach: random angle+random radius. Points cluster near center because area grows with r2. Correct: random angle,radius=r⋅random​. The square root compensates for area growth. Alternatively: rejection sampling. Pick random(x,y)in square,accept if x2+y2≤r2.

n coins with probabilities pi​ of heads. Find probability of exactly k heads. dp[i][j]=probability of j heads in first i coins. Transition: dp[i][j]=dp[i−1][j−1]⋅pi​+dp[i−1][j]⋅(1−pi​). Base: dp[0][0]=1(no coins,0 heads).
Answer: dp[n][k]. This is the classic probability DP pattern. Each coin contributes independently,and we track the cumulative count. Each coin contributes independently. The product of individual probabilities gives the combined probability.

Full table: O(nk)space. But each row only depends on the previous row. Use two arrays: prev and curr. Swap after each coin. Space: O(k). Or use single array with reverse iteration: dp[j]=dp[j−1]⋅pi​+dp[j]⋅(1−pi​),iterating j from k down to 1. Time: O(nk),Space: O(k). The pattern is identical to 0/1 Knapsack space reduction. Space reduction works because each row only depends on the previous row. You can use two arrays or reverse iteration.

Some probability processes have infinite expected value. Example: flip coin until heads. Expected flips=2. But: pay 2n if first heads is on flip n. Expected payout=∑n=1∞​2n⋅2−n=∞.
This is the St. Petersburg paradox. In DP,watch for recurrences like E=1+E with probability 1(infinite loop). The expected value is undefined. make sure your probability DP has absorbing states(termination)with positive probability from every state.

units.unit_name: Pattern - When to Use Probability DP
units.text:
Use probability DP when: you need probability of an outcome after a sequence of random events. Signals: "probability of","expected value","random choice","uniform distribution","Monte Carlo"(but DP is exact).
State: typically(step,current_position)or(step,accumulated_value). Transitions: weighted by probabilities. The difference from counting DP: transitions multiply by probability instead of adding counts. But linearity of expectation often lets us sum contributions.

You've learned DP with various state spaces: sequences,grids,trees,digits,games,probabilities. Now we'll improve DP transitions.
Many DPs have O(n2)or O(n3)transitions that can be sped up. Divide & Conquer improvement uses the fact that optimal split points are monotonic. Knuth's improvement is similar. These techniques don't change what you compute,just how fast. Learn the DP concepts first; improvement is the polish.

You've learned probability DP. You can now:
1. Compute probabilities using weighted sums over transitions.
2. Handle expected values by weighting outcomes.
3. Recognize when to count paths vs compute probabilities.
4. Apply tricks like scaling and early termination for large inputs. The observation: probability DP is just regular DP with weighted transitions. Instead of min or max,you sum with weights. That's it. For training,solve the problems here's problemset. Then move on to DP Optimizations where you'll learn to make DP faster.

I teach you two optimizations that skip unnecessary work. Both rely on a property called the quadrangle inequality. When it holds,the optimal split point is monotonic: opt[i]≤opt[i+1] for all i.
As i increases,the best split never moves backward,so you can predict where to look. Knuth's improvement turns O(n3)interval DP(dynamic programming)into O(n2). D&C improvement turns O(kn2)partitioning DP into O(knlogn). By the end,you'll understand why these optimizations work,when to apply them,and how to implement them.

Consider dp[i]=minj<i​(dp[j]+cost(j,i)). For each of n states,you check O(n)previous states. That's O(n2). For interval DP like dp[i][j]=mink​(dp[i][k]+dp[k+1][j])+cost(i,j),you have O(n2)states and O(n)choices per state. That's O(n3).
With n=105 or even n=4000,these are too slow. But what if you didn't need to check every j or every k?

I'll prove: if cost satisfies QI,then opt[i]≤opt[i+1]. This means opt is monotonic(non-decreasing): as the state index grows,the optimal split never moves backward.
Assume for contradiction that opt[i+1]=j<k=opt[i]. Since k is optimal for i: dp[k]+cost(k,i)≤dp[j]+cost(j,i). Since j is optimal for i+1: dp[j]+cost(j,i+1)≤dp[k]+cost(k,i+1). Add these inequalities.
After canceling dp[j] and dp[k]: cost(k,i)+cost(j,i+1)≤cost(j,i)+cost(k,i+1). But QI with a=j<b=k<c=i<d=i+1 says the opposite! Contradiction. So opt[i]≤opt[i+1].

D&C improvement: For layered DP like dp[g][i]=minj<i​(dp[g−1][j]+cost(j,i)). Uses divide and conquer to exploit monotonicity. O(n2)→O(nlogn)per layer. Knuth's improvement: For interval DP like dp[i][j]=mink​(dp[i][k]+dp[k+1][j])+cost.
Uses a double bound on k. O(n3)→O(n2)total. Both require QI. The difference is the DP structure: layers vs intervals.

You want to compute dp[1],dp[2],…,dp[n] where Base case: dp[0]=0. For i≥1: dp[i]=minj<i​(dp[j]+cost(j,i)). Naively,each takes O(n)time.
D&C improvement computes the middle element first: dp[mid]. Finding opt[mid] takes O(n)time.
But now you know: for all i<mid,search only j≤opt[mid]. For all i>mid,search only j≥opt[mid]. Recurse on both halves.
Each level of recursion does O(n)total work. Picture a binary tree: at the root Compute dp[n/2],its children compute dp[n/4] and dp[3n/4]. Each level handles O(n)work total across all nodes. With O(logn)levels,total is O(nlogn).

Define solve(l,r,optL,optR): compute dp[l..r] knowing optimal splits are in [optL,optR].
1. Base case: if l>r,return.
2. Compute mid=(l+r)/2.
3. Find dp[mid] by trying all j∈[optL,min(optR,mid−1)]. Track which j was best as opt[mid].
4. Recurse: solve(l,mid−1,optL,opt[mid])and solve(mid+1,r,opt[mid],optR).

Compute dp[i] for i∈[0,7]. Optimal splits are monotonic. Start with range [0,7]. Compute dp[3](midpoint). Find opt[3] by trying all valid splits. Say opt[3]=5. Recurse on [0,2] with constraint opt∈[0,5]. Recurse on [4,7] with constraint opt∈[5,n].
Each level does O(n)work total(all ranges at that level partition the search space). O(logn)levels gives O(nlogn).

units.unit_name: Why D&C Is O(n log n)
units.text:
The recursion depth is O(logn)because you halve the state range each time. At each depth,the search ranges [optL,optR] across all calls are nearly disjoint. When you find opt[mid]=k,the left child gets range [optL,k] and the right child gets [k,optR]. These don't overlap except at k.
So all calls at the same depth search a total of O(n)values. With O(logn)depths,total work is O(nlogn). For layered DP with g layers,you run D&C once per layer: O(g⋅nlogn)total.

Imagine the states 1,2,…,n on a line. You solve the middle one first. Its optimal split divides the search space.
Now you have two subproblems: left half of states with left portion of search space,right half with right portion. Each subproblem is independent. The tree of recursive calls has depth logn. At each level,the search ranges don't overlap. That's why total work is linear per level.

units.unit_name: Codeforces 321E Ciel and Gondola - Problem Statement
units.text:
There are n people in a queue for k gondolas. Some pairs know each other. If two people who know each other share a gondola,they talk(cost 1). Assign people to gondolas in queue order to reduce total talking. Each gondola needs at least one person.
This is partitioning: split n people into k contiguous groups,reducing sum of pair-costs within groups. Before reading the solution,try to identify the base case and recursive relationship yourself.

units.unit_name: Codeforces 321E Ciel and Gondola - The DP
units.text:
dp[g][i]=minimum cost to assign first i people to first g gondolas. Transition: dp[g][i]=minj<i​(dp[g−1][j]+cost(j+1,i))Here cost(l,r)=number of knowing pairs in [l,r]. Precompute with 2D(two-dimensional)prefix sums. Naive: O(k⋅n2).
With D&C: O(k⋅nlogn). Work through examples by hand before coding. Understanding the pattern makes implementation simple. Practice with similar problems to reinforce your understanding.

units.unit_name: Codeforces 321E Ciel and Gondola - Why QI Holds
units.text:
Let cost(l,r)count pairs of people in [l,r] who know each other. I need to verify QI: cost(a,c)+cost(b,d)≤cost(a,d)+cost(b,c)for a≤b≤c≤d. Consider any pair(x,y)with x<y. Count how many times it appears on each side:
1. Both in [b,c]: counted twice on both sides. Equal.
2. One in [a,b),one in [b,c]: left has 1(via [a,c]),right has 2(via both). Right wins.
3. One in [a,b),one in(c,d]: left has 0,right has 1(via [a,d]). Right wins. Every pair contributes at least as much to the right side. QI holds.

Here's the D&C solution:
Pseudocode


```
function solve(g,l,r,optL,optR,dp,cost)
if l > r then
        return
    mid :=(l+r)/ 2
    opt :=optL
    dp[g][mid] :=infinity
    for j from optL to min(optR,mid - 1)val :=dp[g-1][j]+cost[j+1][mid]
        if val < dp[g][mid] then
            dp[g][mid] :=val
            opt :=j
    solve(g,l,mid-1,optL,opt,dp,cost)solve(g,mid+1,r,opt,optR,dp,cost)// Each layer g: solve(g,1,n,0,n-1,dp,cost)
```

Time: O(k⋅nlogn). Precompute cost in O(n2). The QI property guarantees that optimal splits are monotonic,enabling the Knuth improvement.
Space: O(n⋅k)for the DP table.
Time: O(n⋅klogn). Space: O(n⋅k).

Partition n people into k groups to reduce total cost. Cost of group [l,r] depends on prefix sums. dp[j][i]=min cost to partition first i people into j groups.
Transition: try all splits. QI holds for the cost function. Apply D&C: for layer j,compute all dp[j][i] in O(nlogn)using the recursion. Total: O(knlogn)instead of O(kn2). For k=100,n=105,this is the difference between TLE and AC.

1. D&C improvement works for layered DP: dp[g][i]=minj<i​(dp[g−1][j]+cost(j,i)).
2. Prove QI holds,which implies monotonicity of optimal splits.
3. Solve middle first,then recurse with bounded search ranges.
4. Each recursion level does O(n)work. Total: O(nlogn)per layer. The pattern: partition problems,layer-by-layer DP,cost satisfying QI. Apply these patterns when you encounter similar problems.

How do you prove QI for a given cost function? Sometimes it's obvious,sometimes it requires algebra. Common technique: show C(a,d)−C(a,c)≥C(b,d)−C(b,c)(equivalent to QI).
This is the 'difference condition'. For sums of squares,products,and many combinatorial costs,QI often holds. For non-standard costs,verify carefully. If unsure,test numerically on small cases. But be warned: numeric tests can't prove QI,only disprove it.

Interval DP has form: dp[i][j]=mini≤k≤j​(dp[i][k−1]+dp[k+1][j])+cost(i,j). Some variants use k<j depending on the problem.
Naive: O(n2)states,O(n)choices per state=O(n3). Knuth's observation: if cost satisfies QI,then opt[i][j−1]≤opt[i][j]≤opt[i+1][j]. This double bound limits where to search for k. Summed over all states,total work becomes O(n2).

Knuth's improvement uses: opt[i][j−1]≤opt[i][j]≤opt[i+1][j]. The left bound says: if k was optimal for [i,j−1],then for [i,j] you won't do better with any k′<k.
Expanding the interval rightward makes later splits more attractive,not earlier ones. The right bound says: if k was optimal for [i+1,j],then for [i,j] you won't do better with any k′>k. Adding an element on the left makes earlier splits more attractive. Both follow from QI by the same logic as the monotonicity proof. The bounds shrink your search range dramatically.

To use the bound opt[i][j−1]≤opt[i][j]≤opt[i+1][j],you need both opt[i][j−1] and opt[i+1][j] computed before opt[i][j]. Interval [i,j−1] has length(j−1)−i+1=j−i. Interval [i+1,j] has length j−(i+1)+1=j−i. Both are one shorter than [i,j]'s length of j−i+1.
So process intervals by increasing length: first all length-1(base cases),then length-2,up to length-n. Each cell's bounds come from the previous length level,which you've already computed.

Interval DP: dp[i][j]=mink​(dp[i][k]+dp[k+1][j])+cost[i][j]. With QI,opt[i][j−1]≤opt[i][j]≤opt[i+1][j].
For interval [0,4]: opt[0][3]≤opt[0][4]≤opt[1][4]. The search range for opt[0][4] is bounded. Fill by diagonal(increasing interval length). For each interval,the bounded search guarantees amortized O(1)per interval. Total: O(n2)for all intervals,down from O(n3). Critical for n=5000 problems.

For fixed length len,you compute dp[i][j] for all i where j=i+len−1. Each cell searches k from opt[i][j−1] to opt[i+1][j]. As i increases,these ranges chain together: the right endpoint of one is the left endpoint of the next.
Example with n=5,len=3: cell(1,3)searches [opt[1][2],opt[2][3]]. Cell(2,4)searches [opt[2][3],opt[3][4]]. Cell(3,5)searches [opt[3][4],opt[4][5]]. The ranges tile [1,n] without overlap. Total k checks per length: O(n). Summing over n lengths: O(n2).

Imagine a 2D(two-dimensional)grid where(i,j)is a cell. You fill the grid diagonal by diagonal(by length). For cell(i,j),you look at(i,j−1)(one left)and(i+1,j)(one down).
Their opt values bound where to search. The search range for each cell is small. Across a diagonal,ranges tile the space without much overlap. That's why the total is O(n)per diagonal,O(n2)overall.

Given n keys with access frequencies f1​,…,fn​,build a BST to reduce expected search cost. If key i is at depth di​,cost is ∑fi​⋅(di​+1). Deeper keys cost more.
This is the problem Knuth designed his improvement for. The interval DP structure comes from the recursive BST definition. Read the problem statement carefully,noting the constraints. Try to identify the recursive structure before looking at the solution.

dp[i][j]=minimum cost for keys i to j. Choose key k as root. Left subtree: [i,k−1]. Right subtree: [k+1,j]. All keys go one level deeper. dp[i][j]=mini≤k≤j​(dp[i][k−1]+dp[k+1][j])+∑l=ij​fl​ The sum adds depth cost for all keys in the range.
Work through examples by hand before coding. Understanding the pattern makes implementation simple. Practice with similar problems to reinforce your understanding.

For Optimal BST,cost(i,j)=∑l=ij​fl​(sum of frequencies). I'll verify QI: cost(a,c)+cost(b,d)≤cost(a,d)+cost(b,c). Left side: ∑l=ac​fl​+∑l=bd​fl​.
The middle part [b,c] is counted twice. Right side: ∑l=ad​fl​+∑l=bc​fl​. The full span [a,d] plus the middle [b,c] again. Breaking into pieces: left=∑ab−1​+2∑bc​+∑c+1d​. Right=same expression. They're equal! Since left=right,we have left≤right. QI holds.

Here's the Knuth-improved solution:
Pseudocode


```
function optimalBST(n,freq)dp,opt :=2D(two-dimensional)arrays
    prefix :=[0]+prefix_sums(freq)// Base cases
    for i from 1 to n:
        dp[i][i] :=freq[i]
        opt[i][i] :=i
    for i from 1 to n+1:
        dp[i][i-1] :=0
    // Fill by length
    for len from 2 to n
        for i from 1 to n - len+1
            j :=i+len - 1
            lo :=opt[i][j-1] if j > i else i
            hi :=opt[i+1][j] if i < n else j
            for k from lo to hi
                val :=dp[i][k-1]+dp[k+1][j]+prefix[j] - prefix[i-1]
                if val < dp[i][j]:
                    dp[i][j] :=val
                    opt[i][j] :=k
    return dp[1][n]

```

Edge cases: when j=i(single element),lo=i. When i=n,hi=j. The prefix sum avoids recomputing ∑fl​ each time.
Time complexity: O(n2)with Knuth optimization.
Space complexity: O(n2)for the  `dp`  and  `opt`  tables.

Build BST with minimum expected search cost. Keys have access frequencies fi​. dp[i][j]=min cost for keys i to j.
Transition: pick root k∈[i,j]. Cost=dp[i][k−1]+dp[k+1][j]+∑l=ij​fl​(depth increases by 1 for all nodes). QI holds for this cost function. Apply Knuth: opt[i][j−1]≤opt[i][j]≤opt[i+1][j]. Fill by increasing interval length. Each dp[i][j] searches bounded range. Total: O(n2).

1. Knuth works for interval DP: dp[i][j]=mini≤k≤j​(dp[i][k−1]+dp[k+1][j])+cost.
2. QI gives you the double bound on opt[i][j].
3. Process by length so bounds are available when needed.
4. Store opt[i][j] as you compute it. The pattern: combining subproblems(like BST,matrix chain,stone merge). This improvement avoids redundant computation. The time saved can be dramatic,turning an exponential solution into polynomial time.

A string of length n with m break points. Each break costs the current string length. Find minimum total cost. dp[i][j]=min cost to break segment between break points i and j.
Cost of one break=segment length. Transition: dp[i][j]=mink​(dp[i][k]+dp[k][j])+(pos[j]−pos[i]). The length adds to all splits. QI holds. Apply Knuth: O(m2)total. Without improvement,O(m3). This is one of the cleanest examples of Knuth improvement. The cost function satisfies the quadrangle inequality.

Add 0 and n as dummy break points. Now we have m+2 points,and we need to fully break the string. Base case: dp[i][i+1]=0(no break needed between adjacent points). Fill by increasing gap: gap=2,3,...,m+1. For each(i,j)with gap j−i,search k∈[opt[i][j−1],opt[i+1][j]]. Time: O(m2),Space: O(m2). Track opt[i][j] alongside dp[i][j]. Add sentinel break points at 0 and n to handle boundaries cleanly. The actual breaks are between sentinels.
Time complexity: O(n2).
Space complexity: O(n2).

Allocate n tasks to k servers. Server cost=sum of(task weights)^2 for assigned tasks. Find the lowest total. dp[j][i]=min cost to assign first i tasks to j servers.
Transition: split at some point for the j-th server. Cost of assigning [l,r] to one server=(∑t=lr​wt​)2. Use prefix sums. QI holds for this squared-sum cost. Apply D&C: O(knlogn). The squared sum cost encourages even distribution. Putting all tasks on one server gives high cost.

Precompute prefix sums S[i]=∑t=0i−1​wt​. Cost of [l,r]=(S[r+1]−S[l])2. D&C recursion: for layer j,compute all dp[j][i] using the monotonicity of optimal splits. Base: dp[1][i]=(S[i])2(all tasks on one server). Fill layers 2 to k. Final answer: dp[k][n]. Time: O(knlogn). Space: O(n)if we only keep two layers. Keep only two layers in memory since each layer only depends on the previous. This saves space.
Time complexity: O(knlogn).
Space complexity: O(n)using two-layer rolling array.

Use D&C when: DP has layers: dp[g][i]=minj<i​(dp[g−1][j]+cost(j,i)). Partitioning into k groups. Use Knuth when: DP is interval-based: dp[i][j]=mini≤k≤j​(dp[i][k−1]+dp[k+1][j])+cost. Combining subproblems. Both need: Cost satisfies the quadrangle inequality.
If unsure which,check the recurrence structure. Layers=D&C. Intervals=Knuth.

1. Forgetting to prove QI: Don't assume it holds. Prove it for your cost function.
2. Wrong iteration order(Knuth): Process by length,not by i then j.
3. Off-by-one errors: Bounds are [opt[i][j−1],opt[i+1][j]]. Check edge cases.
4. Not storing opt: You need opt values to bound future searches. These optimizations are tricky. Test on small inputs first.

Not all DP problems satisfy QI. If optimal splits aren't monotonic,these optimizations don't apply. Example: cost function with alternating behavior,or costs that depend on absolute values in complex ways.
Signs of non-QI: optimal splits jump around non-monotonically in test cases. The recurrence structure doesn't fit. Alternative optimizations: segment trees,Li Chao trees,or different algorithmic approaches entirely.

D&C and Knuth can combine with other techniques. Example: D&C improvement inside a segment tree for online queries.
Or: Knuth improvement for the core DP,then binary search for the answer over a parameter. These combinations are rare in contests but effective. Understand each technique deeply before combining. Learn single optimizations first. Combinations are advanced competitive programming territory.

When cost satisfies QI,optimal splits are monotonic. This lets you prune the search space.
1. Identify the DP type: Layered(partitioning)→ D&C. Interval(combining)→ Knuth.
2. Prove QI: cost(a,c)+cost(b,d)≤cost(a,d)+cost(b,c).
3. Apply the improvement: Use divide-and-conquer or double bounds.
4. Track opt values: They bound future computations. This improvement reduces redundant work largely.

D&C checklist: pass opt_l and opt_r bounds,compute mid correctly,update opt[mid],recurse with updated bounds. Knuth checklist: iterate by increasing interval length,track opt[i][j],make sure opt[i][j-1] and opt[i+1][j] are computed first. Boundary conditions: what is opt for base cases? Usually opt[i][i]=i or similar. Off-by-one errors are common. Verification: print optimal splits for small inputs. Check they're monotonic. If not,QI doesn't hold for your cost function.
Time complexity: O(n2)with Knuth optimization,O(n2logn)with D&C optimization.
Space complexity: O(n2)for the DP and opt tables.

Given a DP problem,ask:(1)Is it interval DP or layered DP?(2)Does the cost function satisfy QI?(3)Are optimal splits bounded? Interval DP with QI → Knuth.
Layered DP with QI → D&C. Neither → standard DP or different improvement. Practice problems: Matrix Chain(Knuth),Ciel and Gondola(D&C),Optimal BST(Knuth),Printing Neatly(D&C). Build intuition by solving 5-10 problems. The pattern recognition becomes automatic.

D&C and Knuth improve by exploiting monotonicity of optimal splits. But what if the structure is different? Convex Hull Trick optimizes DP transitions that have a linear form: dp[i]=min(mj​⋅xi​+bj​). It's geometry-based.
Instead of bounding split points,CHT maintains a hull of lines. Each transition is a point-line query. These are all tools in the DP improvement toolkit. Each applies to a specific structural pattern.

You've learned two DP optimizations based on the quadrangle inequality:
1. D&C improvement: For layered DP. Solve middle first,recurse with bounded ranges. O(kn2)→O(kn \log n)$$.
2. Knuth's improvement: For interval DP. Use opt[i][j−1]≤opt[i][j]≤opt[i+1][j]. O(n3)→O(n^2)$$. The observation: monotonicity of optimal splits means you can skip most of the search space. Practice these on the problemset. They're advanced techniques,but once you understand them,they become mechanical to apply.

Your DP(dynamic programming)recurrence(the formula relating states,introduced in Section 1)looks like dp[i]=minj​(dp[j]+aj​⋅bi​+cj​). For each i,you check all j. That's O(n2).
But notice something: the expression aj​⋅bi​+cj​ is linear in bi​. Each j defines a line. You're finding the minimum among n lines at point bi​. Most of those lines will never be optimal. I'll teach you to keep only the useful ones. By the end,you'll reduce O(n2)to O(n)using the Convex Hull Trick(I'll explain why it's called "convex" in Unit 5).

In the previous section,you learned that Quadrangle Inequality(QI)improvement works when optimal splits are monotonic. But what if your cost function doesn't satisfy QI? Consider dp[i]=minj<i​(dp[j]+(si​−sj​)2)where s is a prefix sum.
Expanding: dp[i]=minj​(dp[j]+si2​−2si​sj​+sj2​). The term−2si​sj​ mixes i and j. This isn't QI-friendly. But look closer: for fixed i,this is linear in sj​. That's the pattern CHT exploits.

A linear function has the form f(x)=mx+c. Given slope m and intercept c,for any query x,you get f(x)in O(1). In CHT,each previous DP(dynamic programming)state(the subproblem index)j contributes a line.
The slope mj​ and intercept cj​ depend on values computed at j. The query point xi​ depends on the current state i. Your DP becomes: evaluate all lines at xi​,take the minimum. That's still O(n)per state. But most lines are worthless.

Plot all lines y=mj​x+cj​ in the xy-plane. For a query point xi​,you want the line giving minimum y. Different lines win at different x values.
A steep negative slope wins for small x. A gentle slope wins for large x. At any x,exactly one line(or a tie)gives the minimum. The set of points where each line is optimal forms the lower envelope. Only lines on this envelope ever contribute to answers. Lines strictly above it are useless.

The lower envelope is the boundary you see when looking at all lines from below. It's a piecewise linear function: each segment belongs to 1 line. The lower envelope is concave because you're taking the pointwise minimum of linear functions. At each breakpoint,the slope decreases.
You'll hear this called the 'Convex Hull Trick' because the line segments trace a convex hull in the dual space. Lines on the envelope are sorted by slope,high to low from left to right. Adjacent lines intersect at breakpoints. For query x,find which segment contains x. That segment's line gives the minimum. The structure has at most n segments.

Suppose line L2​ is always above lines L1​ and L3​ in the range where queries happen. Then L2​ never wins. It's dominated. More precisely: if adding L2​ to the envelope would create a segment of zero width(the intersection with L1​ is to the right of intersection with L3​),then L2​ is useless.
This is the pruning rule. When adding a new line,remove any line that becomes dominated. Only O(n)lines survive across the entire algorithm.

units.unit_name: What Is the Convex Hull Trick?
units.text:
The Convex Hull Trick maintains the lower envelope as you add lines. For each query,it finds the optimal line on the envelope. Two cases:
1. Sorted queries: If xi​ values are monotonic(say,increasing),the optimal line also moves monotonically. Use a deque(double-ended queue that supports push/pop from both ends): O(1)amortized per query.
2. Arbitrary queries: Use binary search on the envelope or a Li Chao tree. O(logn)per query. I'll focus on sorted queries first. That's the cleanest case.

Assume x1​≤x2​≤…≤xn​. As x increases,the optimal line can only move right on the envelope(or stay the same). Why? Lines are sorted by slope on the envelope. A line with smaller slope beats others for small x. As x grows,lines with larger slopes take over.
Once a line stops being optimal,it never comes back. This monotonicity means you never revisit old lines. A simple pointer suffices. Even simpler: use a deque.

Store lines in a deque(double-ended queue),ordered by slope. The front has the smallest slope(optimal for small x). The back has the largest slope(optimal for large x). For queries: check if the second line beats the first at current x.
If yes,pop the front. Repeat until the front is optimal. For insertions: add new lines at the back. But first,remove lines that become dominated. Both operations are amortized O(1)because each line enters and leaves at most once.

Lines: y=3x+10,y=2x+15,y=x+22. Slopes are decreasing(sorted). Build the lower envelope. Add line 1(3x+10): deque=[L1]. Add line 2(2x+15): check if L1 is still useful.
Intersection at x=5. L2 better for x>5. Deque=[L1,L2]. Add line 3(x+22): L3 intersects L2 at x=7. L3 intersects L1 at x=6. L2 is not dominated(6>5). Final deque=[L1,L2,L3]. Query x=4: L1 gives 22,L3 gives 26. Min=22 from L1. Deque structure gives O(1)amortized query.

When adding line Lnew​ with slope mnew​:
1. While the deque has≥2 lines,check if the last line is now dominated.
2. Let L1​ be second-to-last,L2​ be last. If L2​ loses to both L1​ and Lnew​ everywhere between their intersections,pop L2​.
3. Formally: if intersect(L1​,Lnew​)≤intersect(L1​,L2​),then L2​ is useless. Pop it.
4. Push Lnew​. This maintains the lower envelope invariant.

To query at xi​:
1. While the deque has≥2 lines,check if the second line is better than the first at xi​.
2. If L2​(xi​)<L1​(xi​),pop the front. The first line will never be optimal again because queries are increasing.
3. Repeat until the front is optimal.
4. Return L1​(xi​). Because queries are sorted,once you pop a line,you never need it again. Each line leaves the deque at most once.

Deque has lines with intersections at x=3,7,12. Query for x=10. Binary search: 10>3,10>7,10<12. The optimal line is between intersections 7 and 12.
With sorted queries: if last query was x=8,pointer was at line after intersection 7. For x=10,pointer stays(still before intersection 12). Amortized O(1)per query: pointer only moves right. Total movement across all queries is O(n).

Each line is added exactly once and removed at most once. That's O(n)add operations,O(n)remove operations. Adding: You might pop multiple lines when adding one,but total pops across all adds is bounded by total lines ever added. That's O(n). Querying: Same logic.
Each front-pop happens at most once per line. Total query overhead: O(n). Combined: O(n)time for n states. Your O(n2)DP becomes O(n)because we do O(n)work total,not O(n)work per state. That's the power of CHT.

Picture lines in a plane. The lower envelope zigzags through them. You maintain this envelope in a deque. As you process states left-to-right:
1. Query: Your x pointer slides right. Pop lines from the front that are no longer optimal.
2. Insert: Add the new line at the back. Pop any lines it dominates. Think of it as a sliding window on the envelope. The window only moves forward. That's why it's O(n).

units.unit_name: Codeforces 319C Kalila and Dimna - Problem Statement
units.text:
There are n trees with heights a1​,…,an​ and costs b1​,…,bn​. You start at tree 1 and want to reach tree n. From tree i,jumping to tree j>i costs bi​⋅aj​. Find the minimum total cost. The cost mixes bi​(source)and aj​(destination).
This product structure is the CHT signal. The problem statement specifies that heights ai​ are strictly increasing and costs bi​ are strictly decreasing. This ordering is exactly what CHT needs. Let me show you how to set up the DP.

questions.title: Kalila and Dimna in the Logging Industry - Read Statement

units.unit_name: Codeforces 319C Kalila and Dimna - The DP
units.text:
Let dp[i]=minimum cost to reach tree i. Base: dp[1]=0(start here,no cost). Transition: dp[j]=mini<j​(dp[i]+bi​⋅aj​). Naive: O(n2). For each j,try all i.
But look at the transition: dp[i]+bi​⋅aj​. For fixed j,rewrite as dp[j]=mini​(bi​⋅aj​+dp[i]). This is mini​(mi​⋅xj​+ci​)where mi​=bi​,ci​=dp[i],xj​=aj​. That's CHT form!

units.unit_name: Codeforces 319C Kalila and Dimna - CHT Form
units.text:
Now I'll show you how to rewrite the DP recurrence into CHT form. Here's the idea: each transition can be expressed as a line equation: y=mx+b. Start with the recurrence: dp[i]=min(dp[j]+cost[j][i]).
Expand the cost function and rearrange terms so that variables depending on j are separated from variables depending on i. You'll get: dp[i]=min(slope[j]×x[i]+intercept[j]). The slope and intercept depend only on j,while x[i] depends only on i.
This separation is what makes CHT applicable. Once in this form,you maintain a convex hull of lines. Each line represents a previous state j. To compute dp[i],you query the minimum value at point x[i] on the hull. This reduces the transition cost per state significantly.

units.unit_name: Codeforces 319C Kalila and Dimna - Why It Works
units.text:
Slopes bi​ are strictly decreasing. When we add lines in order i=1,2,…,each new line has a smaller slope than all previous. Lines are added to the deque in decreasing slope order. For queries: we need aj​ values. The problem states a1​<a2​<…<an​(increasing).
So queries are sorted too. Both conditions met: sorted slopes(decreasing)and sorted queries(increasing). The simple O(n)deque CHT applies directly.

Here's the full solution:
Pseudocode


```
function solve(n,a,b)dp :=array of size n+1
    dp[1] :=0
    deque :=empty
    for i from 1 to n
        while deque.size≥2 and dominated(deque[-2],deque[-1],(b[i],dp[i]))deque.pop_back()deque.push_back((b[i],dp[i]))
if i < n
            while deque.size≥2 and eval(deque[0],a[i+1])≥eval(deque[1],a[i+1])deque.pop_front()dp[i+1] :=eval(deque[0],a[i+1])
return dp[n]

```

The dominated check uses cross-multiplication to avoid floating point. Each line enters and leaves the deque once.
Time complexity: O(n)amortized,since each line enters and leaves the deque at most once.
Space complexity: O(n)for the  `dp`  array and deque.

questions.title: Kalila and Dimna in the Logging Industry - Implement Solution

units.text:
1. CHT applies when the DP has form dp[i]=minj​(mj​⋅xi​+cj​)+extra.
2. Rewrite your recurrence to extract the linear form. Identify mj​,cj​,xi​.
3. Check if slopes or queries are sorted. If yes,use the simple deque. If not,use binary search or Li Chao tree.
4. Time: O(n)Time complexity: O(n)for sorted case,O(nlogn)for general case. CHT is effective because it doesn't require QI. It exploits linearity instead.
Space complexity: O(n)for the hull.

The deque approach assumes slopes are sorted(decreasing for lower envelope). What if they're not? Option 1: Binary search on the hull. Insert each line,but use binary search to find its position. O(logn)per operation. Option 2: Li Chao tree. A segment tree over x-coordinates.
Each node stores the optimal line for its range. O(logn)insert and query. Option 3: Process offline. Sort all operations,then use deque. Works when you can reorder.

What if xi​ values come in arbitrary order? The front-popping trick fails because you might need a "popped" line later. Two solutions:
1. Binary search: Keep all lines on the envelope. For query x,binary search for the segment containing x. O(logn)per query.
2. Li Chao tree: A segment tree where each node stores a line. Insert and query both take O(logn). The deque still works for insertions(if slopes are sorted). Only queries change.

You store the envelope as a list of lines sorted by slope. You also store breakpoints: x values where adjacent lines intersect. To query at x,binary search for the largest breakpoint leqx. The line that becomes optimal past that breakpoint gives you the minimum.
This works because when you sort lines by slope,their pairwise intersections fall at increasing x positions. Your breakpoints are sorted,so binary search finds the correct segment in O(logn). Building takes O(n)if slopes are pre-sorted; n queries take O(nlogn)total. That beats the O(n2)naive approach.

A Li Chao tree is a segment tree over the range of possible x values. Each node stores one line. Insert: Compare new line with stored line at the midpoint.
Keep the one that wins at midpoint. Recurse with the other line to the half where it might win. Query: Walk from root to leaf at x. Return the best value seen. Both operations: O(logn). Works for arbitrary slopes and arbitrary queries. It's the most general CHT data structure.

Li Chao tree: segment tree over x-values. Each node stores the line that's best at the midpoint of its range. Insert line L: compare with current line at midpoint.
The winner stays. The loser might still be optimal in some child. Recurse into the half where the loser could win. At most O(logn)nodes visited per insert. Query x: traverse from root to leaf for x. At each node,evaluate the stored line. Return the minimum across all nodes on the path.

1. Sign errors: If you want minimum and slopes are increasing,you need the upper envelope,not lower. Double-check your setup.
2. Wrong domination check: The intersection formula can have division issues. Use cross-multiplication to avoid floating point.
3. Forgetting the extra term: Remember to add back si2​ or whatever you factored out.
4. Overflow: m⋅x can overflow 64-bit integers. Use 128-bit or __int128 if needed.
5. Off-by-one: Process lines before querying. Don't query with a line you haven't added yet.

Split n soldiers into contiguous groups. Group value=a⋅x2+b⋅x+c where x is sum of strengths in group. dp[i]=max value for first i soldiers.
Transition: dp[i]=maxj<i​(dp[j]+f(Si​−Sj​))where S is prefix sum. Expand f(Si​−Sj​)=a(Si​−Sj​)2+b(Si​−Sj​)+c. Separate terms by i and j. Result: linear in Si​ with slope depending on j. Apply CHT. Handle max instead of min by negating.

For maximization: negate all values,find minimum,negate result. Or use upper envelope instead of lower. Upper envelope: the piecewise function giving maximum y at each x. Maintain with the same deque logic,but flip comparisons. Alternatively: insert lines(−m,−c)into a lower envelope CHT,then negate the query result. Watch for integer overflow with large prefix sums. Use long long or 128-bit integers if needed.
Time complexity: O(n).
Space complexity: O(n)with monotonic CHT.

Cover n points with roofs. Roof [l,r] costs c+(r−l)2. Find minimum total cost. dp[i]=min cost to cover first i points. Transition: dp[i]=minj<i​(dp[j]+c+(xi​−xj+1​)2). Expand the square.
After algebra: dp[i]=min((−2xj+1​)⋅xi​+(dp[j]+c+xj+12​))+xi2​. Lines have slope−2xj+1​ and intercept dp[j]+c+xj+12​. Apply CHT. Add xi2​ to the query result.

What if the linear term has two variables? min(aj​⋅xi​+bj​⋅yi​+cj​)for 2D(two-dimensional)queries. This becomes a 2D lower envelope: a convex polyhedron.
Much harder to maintain dynamically. Practical approach: if one dimension is small,enumerate it. Or use divide and conquer over one variable. Full 2D CHT is rarely needed in contests. Most problems can be reduced to 1D(one-dimensional)with clever problem reformulation.

Recognize CHT when:
1. Your DP has dp[i]=minj​(f(j)⋅g(i)+h(j))where f,g,h depend only on their argument.
2. The cost "mixes" i and j through a product term. Look for terms like ai​⋅bj​ in the recurrence.
3. You can rewrite as minj​(mj​⋅xi​+cj​). Then check: Slopes mj​ monotonic? Use deque for inserts. Queries xi​ monotonic? Use deque for queries. Neither sorted? Use Li Chao tree instead.

Signal: dp[i]=minj​(dp[j]+f(i,j))where f(i,j)can be written as mj​⋅g(i)+cj​+h(i). The mj​⋅g(i)is the linear term. cj​ is the intercept. h(i)is added after the query.
Common forms: aj​⋅bi​,(a−b)2 expanded,prefix sum products. Look for products of i-term and j-term. If you see a quadratic that expands into separable terms,CHT likely applies. Practice recognizing the pattern.

Tip 1: Handle edge cases. Empty deque,single line,parallel lines(same slope). Check before accessing deque elements. Tip 2: Use exact arithmetic for intersection comparisons. Avoid floating point. Cross-multiply instead of dividing. Tip 3: For debugging,print the hull after each insertion. Verify that intersection x-coordinates are increasing. Tip 4: Template your CHT. Once debugged,reuse. The logic is tricky to get right from scratch under time pressure.
Time complexity: O(n)amortized for inserting n lines.
Space complexity: O(n)for storing lines in the deque.

CHT optimizes DP with linear cost functions. But some DPs have range constraints: j must be within a sliding window of i. Monotonic Queue improvement handles dp[i]=minj∈[i−k,i−1]​(dp[j])+cost(i). The valid j range slides with i.
Instead of a hull of lines,we maintain a deque of values. The front is always the best in the current window. Both are deque-based optimizations,but for different DP structures. Learn both to cover more problem types.

You've learned the Convex Hull Trick:
1. When DP has linear terms mixing indices,rewrite as minj​(mj​⋅xi​+cj​).
2. Each j defines a line. You query the lower envelope at xi​.
3. For sorted queries: O(n)total using a deque.
4. For arbitrary queries: O(nlogn)using binary search or Li Chao tree. CHT doesn't need QI. It works on a completely different structure: linearity. You now have another tool for breaking through TLE. For practice,solve the problems in the problemset.

You know how to improve DP(dynamic programming)with Convex Hull Trick when transitions involve line queries. But what if your DP needs the minimum or maximum over a sliding window of previous states? I'll show you monotonic queues.
This data structure keeps candidates in sorted order while elements enter and leave a window. You'll use it to turn O(nk)DP into O(n). By the end,you'll recognize sliding window patterns in DP and implement monotonic queue optimizations. We'll solve four classic problems together.

Some DP transitions look like this: dp[i]=minj=i−ki−1​(dp[j])+cost[i]. You need the minimum over the last k states. The naive approach checks all k candidates for each i,giving you O(nk)time.
When n=105 and k=104,that's a billion operations. Your code times out. You need a way to track the minimum without rechecking all candidates every time. What if you could maintain a "shortlist" of candidates that might become optimal?

Here's the breakthrough: if dp[j1​]≥dp[j2​] and j1​<j2​,you never need j1​ as a candidate for minimum. Why? Because j2​ gives a better(or equal)value AND stays valid longer in the sliding window. When j1​ expires,j2​ is still there with a better(or equal)answer.
This means you can discard candidates that will never be optimal. You only keep the ones that might matter. The monotonic queue does this automatically by removing dominated elements.

The deque stores indices in increasing order of their dp values(for finding minimum). When you process position i:
1. Remove indices from the front if they're outside the window [i−k,i−1].
2. The front index gives you the minimum dp value in the window.
3. Remove indices from the back while their dp values are≥dp[i].
4. Add i to the back. Each index enters once and leaves once. Total time: O(n)Time complexity: O(n)amortized for all operations combined.
Space complexity: O(k)for the deque.

Find sliding window maximum for [1,3,−1,−3,5,3] with k=3. Window 1: indices [0,1,2]. Insert 1,deque=[1].
Insert 3,pop 1(smaller),deque=[3]. Insert -1,deque=[3,-1]. Max=3. Window 2: indices [1,2,3]. Index 0 was already popped when value 3 was inserted. Deque starts as [3,-1]. Insert -3,deque=[3,-1,-3]. Max=3. Continue: window 3 inserts 5,clears everything smaller. Window 4 adds 3 after 5. Final maxes: [3,3,5,5].

units.unit_name: LeetCode 239 Sliding Window Maximum - Problem Statement
units.text:
Given an array of integers and a window size k,return the maximum value in each sliding window as it moves from left to right through the array. Example: nums=[1,3,-1,-3,5,3,6,7],k=3. The windows are [1,3,-1],[3,-1,-3],[-1,-3,5],[-3,5,3],[5,3,6],[3,6,7]. The answer is [3,3,5,5,6,7].
This is THE foundational problem for monotonic queues. Every other problem builds on this pattern. Before reading on,try to think: how would you maintain the maximum efficiently?

Your first instinct might be to use a heap. Push elements as they enter,pop as they leave. But heaps don't support efficient removal of arbitrary elements. When an element leaves the window,finding it in the heap takes O(k)time. What about a balanced BST like a TreeMap? That works in O(nlogk),which is decent.
But we can do better. The monotonic queue gives O(n)total time with O(k)space for the deque. The observation: in a sliding window,you only care about candidates that could become the maximum. Smaller elements behind a larger one will never matter.

Use a monotonic decreasing deque. Store indices,not values. The front always holds the index of the current maximum. When processing index i: First,remove indices from the front that are outside the window(index <i−k+1).
Second,remove indices from the back while nums[back]≤nums[i]. Third,add i to the back. Why remove smaller elements? Because nums[i] is both larger AND enters later. Those smaller elements will never be the maximum while nums[i] is in the window.

Here's the approach in pseudo-code:
Pseudocode


```
function maxSlidingWindow(nums,k)deque :=empty deque of indices
    result :=[]
    for i from 0 to n-1
        // Remove indices outside window
        while deque not empty and deque.front()< i - k+1
            deque.popFront()// Remove smaller elements from back
        while deque not empty and nums[deque.back()] <=nums[i]
            deque.popBack()deque.pushBack(i)
if i >=k - 1 then
            result.append(nums[deque.front()])
return result

```

Time: O(n)because each index is pushed and popped at most once. Space: O(k)for the deque.
Time complexity: O(n).
Space complexity: O(k).

Array [8,5,10,7,9,4,15,12],window k=4. Maintain deque of indices with decreasing values. i=0: deque=[0]. i=1: 5<8,deque=[0,1]. i=2: 10>5>8,pop all,deque=[2]. i=3: 7<10,deque=[2,3].
Window full,max=10. i=4: 9>7,pop 3. 9<10,deque=[2,4]. Index 2 still valid. Max=10. i=5: 4<9,deque=[2,4,5]. Index 2 still valid. Max=10. i=6: 15>all,clear deque. deque=[6]. Index 2 out of window anyway. Max=15. i=7: 12<15,deque=[6,7]. Max=15.

units.unit_name: LeetCode 1696 Jump Game VI - Problem Statement
units.text:
You're at index 0 of an array nums. From index i,you can jump to any index j where i<j≤i+k. Your score is the sum of all visited elements. Find the maximum score to reach the last index.
Example: nums=[1,-1,-2,4,-7,3],k=2. One optimal path: 0 → 2 → 4 → 5 with score 1+(−2)+(−7)+3=−5. Wait,that's not right. Let me reconsider: 0 → 1 → 3 → 5 with score 1+(−1)+4+3=7. This is where DP meets monotonic queues. The naive DP is O(nk). Can you see the sliding window structure?

units.unit_name: LeetCode 1696 Jump Game VI - DP Formulation
units.text:
Let dp[i]=maximum score to reach index i. The base case is dp[0]=nums[0]. The transition: dp[i]=nums[i]+maxj=max(0,i−k)i−1​(dp[j]).
You take the best score from any reachable previous position and add the current element. Naive implementation: for each i,loop through the last k positions. That's O(nk)time. With n=105 and k=105,you're looking at 1010 operations. Time to improve.

Look at the transition: maxj=i−ki−1​(dp[j]). This is exactly the sliding window maximum problem! The window contains the last k DP values.
You already know how to solve that in O(1)per query using a monotonic decreasing deque. Apply it directly to the DP values. As you compute dp[i],maintain a deque of indices sorted by decreasing dp values. The front gives you the maximum dp value in the valid range.

Here's the faster approach:
Pseudocode


```
function maxResult(nums,k)n :=length of nums
    dp :=array of size n
    dp[0] :=nums[0]
    deque :=empty deque
    deque.pushBack(0)for i from 1 to n-1
        // Remove indices outside window
        while deque not empty and deque.front()< i - k
            deque.popFront()// dp[i]=best previous+current value
        dp[i] :=dp[deque.front()]+nums[i]
        // Maintain decreasing order
        while deque not empty and dp[deque.back()] <=dp[i]
            deque.popBack()deque.pushBack(i)
return dp[n-1]

```

Time: O(n). Space: O(n)for DP array,O(k)for deque. This is the template for monotonic queue DP improvement.

dp[i]=max score to reach index i. From i,can jump to [i+1,i+k]. Transition: dp[i]=nums[i]+maxj∈[i−k,i−1]​dp[j]. Array [1,−1,−2,4,−7,3],k=2. dp[0]=1. dp[1]=−1+dp[0]=0. dp[2]=−2+max(dp[0],dp[1])=−1. dp[3]=4+max(dp[1],dp[2])=4+0=4. dp[4]=−7+max(dp[2],dp[3])=−7+4=−3. dp[5]=3+max(dp[3],dp[4])=3+4=7.
Answer: 7. Monotonic queue gives O(n)instead of O(nk).

What if the window size varies? At position i,valid j∈[li​,ri​] where bounds depend on i. If li​ is non-decreasing,the left side still slides right. Pop expired elements from front as usual.
If ri​ is non-decreasing,new elements can be added in order. Standard monotonic queue works. If bounds are arbitrary,monotonic queue doesn't directly apply. Consider segment trees or other structures.

units.unit_name: LeetCode 1425 Constrained Subsequence Sum - Problem Statement
units.text:
Given an integer array nums and integer k,find the maximum sum of a non-empty subsequence where consecutive elements in the subsequence have indices at most k apart.
Example: nums=[10,2,-10,5,20],k=2. One valid subsequence: indices 0,1,3,4 with values [10,2,5,20]. The gap between consecutive indices is at most 2. Sum=37. This looks similar to Jump Game VI,but there's a twist. You don't have to include every position you "visit." You're choosing a subsequence,not a path.

Let dp[i]=maximum sum of a valid subsequence ending at index i(must include nums[i]). The transition: dp[i]=nums[i]+max(0,maxj=i−ki−1​(dp[j])). You take the best previous subsequence sum if it's positive,otherwise start fresh. The max(0,...)is what separates this from Jump Game VI.
If all previous subsequences ending in the window have negative sum,you're better off starting a new subsequence at i.
