Find maximum sum of subsequence where adjacent elements are at most k apart. dp[i]=max sum ending at i. Transition: dp[i]=nums[i]+max(0,maxj∈[i−k,i−1]​dp[j]). The max(0,...)handles starting fresh. Example: [10,2,−10,5,20],k=2. dp=[10,12,2,17,37]. At each step,query window max and add current.
Answer: max(dp)=37(subsequence 10,2,5,20). Monotonic queue handles the window max in O(1)amortized.

units.unit_name: LeetCode 862 Shortest Subarray with Sum at Least K - Problem Statement
units.text:
Given an integer array nums(can have negative numbers)and integer k,find the length of the shortest non-empty subarray with sum at least k. Return -1 if no such subarray exists. Example: nums=[2,-1,2],k=3. The subarray [2,-1,2] has sum 3 and length 3. That's the shortest.
Answer: 3. This problem is tricky because negative numbers break the sliding window approach. You can't just shrink from the left when the sum exceeds k. This requires a different kind of monotonic queue.

questions.title: Shortest Subarray with Sum at Least K - Read Statement

units.text:
Let P[i]=sum of first i elements,with P[0]=0. The sum of subarray [i,j)is P[j]−P[i]. You want P[j]−P[i]≥k with j>i,reducing j−i. Rearranging: P[j]−k≥P[i]. For each j,you're looking for the largest i<j such that P[i]≤P[j]−k.
But you also want j−i as small as possible,which means you want i as large as possible while still satisfying the constraint.

Use a monotonic increasing queue of indices by prefix sum value. Two observations:
1. If P[i1​]≥P[i2​] and i1​<i2​,you never need i1​. Why? Index i2​ is closer to future j values AND has equal or smaller prefix sum,so it gives a shorter valid subarray.
2. Once index i forms a valid subarray with j(meaning P[j]−P[i]≥k),you can remove i from the deque. Future positions j′>j would give longer subarrays with i.

You'll use a deque to maintain candidate starting positions. The core idea: if prefix[j]≥prefix[i] and j>i,position i is never useful.
For each position j,you do two things. First,remove positions from the back of the deque whose prefix sum is≥prefix[j]. They will never be optimal starting points because j is closer and has a smaller or equal prefix sum. Second,check positions at the front of the deque: while prefix[j]−prefix[front]≥k,you have a valid subarray. Update your answer with j−front,then pop the front(no future j will do better with that index).
Each position enters the deque once and leaves once. This gives O(n)total work across all iterations.
Time complexity: O(n).
Space complexity: O(n)for the prefix array and deque.

questions.title: Shortest Subarray with Sum at Least K - Implement Solution

units.text:
Find shortest subarray with sum≥K. Use prefix sums: sum[l..r]=prefix[r+1]−prefix[l]≥K. We want prefix[r+1]−prefix[l]≥K,i.e.,prefix[l]≤prefix[r+1]−K.
For each r,find smallest valid l. Maintain monotonic increasing deque of prefix indices. For each r,pop front while prefix[front]≤prefix[r]−K. After popping,if queue non-empty,the popped indices give valid subarrays. Track minimum length. Also pop back if prefix[r]≤prefix[back].

With all non-negative values,sliding window(two pointers)finds shortest subarray with sum≥K in O(n). Negative values break monotonicity of cumulative sums.
Expanding the window can decrease the sum. Prefix sums+monotonic queue handle this. We maintain increasing prefixes because larger prefixes are better left bounds. observation: if prefix[i]≥prefix[j] for i<j,then i is never a better left bound than j for any future right bound.

Look for these patterns to recognize monotonic queue improvement:
1. DP transition needs min/max over a sliding window: dp[i]=minj=i−ki−1​(f(j))+g(i).
2. Fixed or bounded window size k.
3. You're iterating through positions sequentially.
4. No complex dependencies between the function f and g. The improvement works when candidates can be "dominated." If a newer candidate is as good or better than an older one,the older one will never be chosen.

units.unit_name: Max Sum of Rectangle - Problem Statement
units.text:
Find maximum sum rectangle in a 2D(two-dimensional)grid. This combines 1D(one-dimensional)max subarray with column enumeration. Fix top and bottom rows. Compress columns to 1D array(sum of elements between rows). Apply Kadane's algorithm. With constraint that rectangle width≤k: use sliding window maximum instead of Kadane. Time: O(rows2⋅cols)Time complexity: O(n2⋅m)for basic. With width constraint: O(rows2⋅cols)using monotonic queue for columns.
Space complexity: O(n)for the prefix sums.

units.unit_name: Max Consecutive Ones III - Problem Statement
units.text:
Find longest subarray of 1s after flipping at most k zeros. Classic two-pointer / sliding window. Not directly monotonic queue,but related.
The window expands right,contracts left when zeros exceed k. Connection: monotonic queue generalizes sliding window for min/max queries. Here we track zero count,not min/max. The principle is the same: maintain a valid window by removing expired or invalid elements from the left.

Use monotonic decreasing queue when you need the maximum in a window. Larger elements dominate smaller ones because they'll always be preferred while both are valid.
Use monotonic increasing queue when you need the minimum in a window. Smaller elements dominate larger ones for the same reason. The Shortest Subarray problem is a special case: you need increasing queue because smaller prefix sums give more "room" for the subarray sum to exceed k. The structure of your improvement problem determines the monotonicity.

For sliding MAXIMUM: maintain decreasing queue. Larger elements dominate smaller ones. For sliding MINIMUM: maintain increasing queue. Smaller elements dominate larger ones. For "earliest" queries: maintain strictly monotonic. For "latest" queries: allow equal values.
When in doubt: think about what makes an element "dominated". If A is worse AND older,discard it. The choice depends on whether you want max(decreasing queue)or min(increasing queue).

Find longest subarray where max. Min≤limit. Need to track both max AND min in the window. Use two monotonic queues: one decreasing(for max),one increasing(for min). Expand window right. If max. Min > limit,shrink from left until valid. Update both queues on shrink. Time: O(n).
Time complexity: O(n). Each element enters and leaves each queue once. This is a classic two-queue pattern. Two queues work independently. The max queue and min queue together bound the range of values in the window.
Space complexity: O(k)for the two deques.

Maintain maxDeque(decreasing)and minDeque(increasing). Both store indices. On adding index r: pop maxDeque back while arr[back]<arr[r]. Pop minDeque back while arr[back]>arr[r]. On shrinking from l: if maxDeque front=l,pop front. Same for minDeque. Check: arr[maxDeque.front()]−arr[minDeque.front()]≤limit. If not,increment l and repeat. Both queues shrink from the front when elements leave the window. Track indices,not values.
Time complexity: O(n)since each element enters and leaves each deque at most once.
Space complexity: O(n)for the two deques.

Monotonic queue works online: process elements left to right,answer queries as they arrive. Some problems allow offline: collect all queries,sort cleverly,answer in better order.
Example: if queries come with variable k values,sort queries by k,adjust window sizes as needed. Online requires O(n)per query. Offline might allow O(n+q)total with careful ordering. Online algorithms process elements one at a time with no future knowledge. Offline can reorder for efficiency.

Monotonic queue: window min/max with fixed window size. O(n)time. Segment tree with lazy propagation: arbitrary range queries,supports updates. O(nlogn)time. Sparse table: static range min/max queries. O(nlogn)preprocessing,O(1)query.
When you have a sliding window(fixed size,sequential iteration),monotonic queue is the cleanest and fastest. Use the others for more general range query problems.

Signal 1: DP transition with min/max over a sliding window [i−k,i−1]. Signal 2: "Find shortest/longest subarray with some property" where property involves sums or min/max.
Signal 3: Two-pointer problems where you need efficient access to window's extreme values. Not applicable: arbitrary range(use segment tree),range sum(use prefix sums),or when window bounds are non-monotonic. The sliding window with min/max is the telltale sign. If you see a bounded range of indices,think monotonic queue.

Monotonic queue optimizes DP with window constraints. But some DPs have a different constraint: "use exactly k elements". Aliens Trick(WQS Binary Search)handles this.
Instead of fixing k,we add a penalty λ per element and binary search on λ. The idea: with the right penalty,the unconstrained optimum naturally uses exactly k elements. This is a effective meta-technique. It transforms constrained problems into simpler unconstrained ones.

You learned monotonic queue improvement for DP:
1. Recognize sliding window min/max in DP transitions.
2. Use decreasing deque for max,increasing deque for min.
3. Remove expired indices from front,dominated indices from back.
4. Each element enters and exits once,giving O(n)total time. You solved four problems: Sliding Window Maximum(foundation),Jump Game VI(basic DP),Constrained Subsequence Sum(optional inclusion),and Shortest Subarray(prefix sums). You now have a effective O(nk)→O(n)improvement technique.

Now I'll show you one of the cleverest DP(dynamic programming)tricks: the Aliens Trick,also called WQS Binary Search(binary search on a penalty parameter)or Lagrangian Relaxation. You'll face problems where you must pick exactly K items. The obvious DP has a dimension for count: dp[i][k].
When K is large,this is too slow. The Aliens Trick eliminates the count dimension entirely. By the end,you'll solve "exactly K" problems without tracking K in your DP state(the subproblem parameters). We'll work through four classic problems together.

Here's the challenge: find the minimum cost(total penalty from all segments)to partition an array into exactly K segments,where each segment has a cost(for example,the square of its element sum)and the total cost is the sum of all segment costs.
Without the "exactly K" constraint,simple DP suffices: dp[i]=best cost to partition positions 1 to i.
But "exactly K" forces you to track count: dp[i][k]=best cost using exactly k segments ending at position i. With n=50,000 and K=5,000,that's 250 million states. The extra dimension comes from the constraint. What If could remove the constraint and get the right answer?

What if you stopped caring about exactly K? Instead,add a penalty λ(lambda,a number you choose)for each segment you create. New goal: reduce(total cost+λ×segment count). Solve this for different λ values.
When λ=0,you use as many segments as possible(no penalty). When λ is huge,you use few segments(they're expensive). Somewhere in between,you'll naturally use exactly K segments. That's the trick.

Original DP: dp[i][k]=best cost to partition positions 1 to i into exactly k segments. This is O(n⋅K)states. Penalized DP: dpλ​[i]=best(cost+λ×count)to partition positions 1 to i into any number of segments.
This is O(n)states. The penalized DP is much faster. You compute it for different λ values until you find the one that produces exactly K segments.

We want exactly K elements. Define f(λ)=optimal count when each element costs+λ. f is monotonically non-increasing in λ.
More penalty=fewer elements used. Binary search: find smallest λ where f(λ)≤K. Or find λ where f(λ)=K exactly. Subtlety: f might jump(not hit every value). Handle ties carefully. Often we binary search and check both boundaries.

The Aliens Trick requires convexity(a shape that curves in one direction,like the bottom of a bowl). Let g(k)=minimum cost using exactly k segments. The function g must be convex,meaning: the marginal benefit of adding one more segment decreases as k grows. Mathematically: g(k−1)−g(k)≥g(k)−g(k+1).
Each additional segment helps less than the previous one. Most partitioning problems satisfy this naturally. If yours doesn't,the trick fails. Always verify convexity before applying.

When computing dpλ​[i],you need to track two things: the optimal cost and how many segments(or items)produced that cost. Store pairs:(cost,count). During DP transitions(moving from one state to the next),when you have a tie in cost,you need a tie-breaking rule.
If you're binary searching for the λ where count equals K,prefer fewer segments when searching upward and more segments when searching downward. The count tells you f(λ),which drives your binary search. Without accurate counts,you can't find the right penalty.

The penalized DP gives optimal cost. But we also need to know how many elements were used. Modify DP to track pairs:(cost,count). When adding an element,increment count. Comparison: first by cost,then by count(prefer fewer elements if costs are equal,or vice versa depending on problem). After DP,check if count equals K.
If not,adjust λ and repeat. The count tells you whether to increase or decrease the penalty. Binary search converges to the right penalty.

units.unit_name: Building a Tall Barn - Problem Statement
units.text:
You're building an N-story barn with K cows. Each cow is assigned to exactly one floor,each floor has at least one cow. Floor i requires ai​ work,and with c cows it takes ai​/c time. Find the lowest total time. Example: 3 floors with work [10,20,30],5 cows.
If you assign 1,2,2 cows to floors 1,2,3,total time=10/1+20/2+30/2=10+10+15=35. This problem beautifully illustrates why convexity holds. Adding more cows gives diminishing returns. Think about it: the first cow on a floor cuts time by half,the second cuts it by a third,etc.

questions.title: Building a Tall Barn - Read Statement

units.text:
Let g(k)=minimum total time using k cows. Claim: g is convex,meaning each additional cow helps less than the previous one. Intuition: with few cows,adding one makes a big difference. One cow per floor means total time equals ∑ai​. Adding a second cow to the busiest floor cuts that floor's time in half. Huge improvement.
With many cows,they're already well-distributed. Adding one more barely changes anything. This diminishing returns property,g(k−1)−g(k)≥g(k)−g(k+1),is exactly convexity(each additional unit helps less than the previous). The Aliens Trick applies.

Add penalty λ per cow. New goal: reduce ∑i=1N​ai​/ci​+λ⋅Kused​. For each floor i with work ai​,compute the optimal number of cows to assign if each cow costs λ.
This is a separate improvement per floor! For floor i: reduce ai​/c+λ⋅c over positive integers c. The optimal c is around ai​/λ​. Try c=⌊ai​/λ​⌋ and c+1,take the better one.

Here's
Pseudocode


```
function solve(a,K)// Binary search on lambda
    lo :=0,hi :=max(a)while hi - lo > epsilon
        mid :=(lo+hi)/ 2(cost,count):=computeWithPenalty(a,mid)
if count > K
            lo :=mid
        else
            hi :=mid(cost,count):=computeWithPenalty(a,lo)
return cost - lo * K

function computeWithPenalty(a,lambda)totalCost :=0,totalCount :=0
    for each floor i
        c :=optimal cows for floor i with penalty lambda
        totalCost+=a[i] / c+lambda * c
        totalCount+=c
    return(totalCost,totalCount)
```

Time: O(NlogC)where C is the range of costs. Space: O(n)for the DP array.
Time: O(nlogC). Space: O(n).

questions.title: Building a Tall Barn - Implement Solution

units.unit_name: Codeforces 674C Levels and Regions - Problem Statement
units.text:
You have n game levels to split into k regions of consecutive levels. Each region has an expected completion time based on level difficulties. find the smallest total expected time.
The exact formula involves replay probabilities,but the structure is: you're partitioning an array into exactly k segments,reducing a cost function that's convex in k. This is the classic setup for Aliens Trick: partition into exactly k parts with convex cost.

units.unit_name: Codeforces 674C Levels and Regions - Standard DP
units.text:
Standard DP: dp[i][j]=minimum expected time to complete first i levels using exactly j regions. Transition(the formula to compute each state): dp[i][j]=minp<i​(dp[p][j−1]+cost(p+1,i))where cost(l,r)is the expected time to complete levels l through r as a single region.
Time complexity: O(n2k).
Space complexity: O(n)for the dp array. With n=200,000 and k=50,that's 2×1012 operations. Way too slow. Even O(n2)transitions are borderline at 4×1010 operations.

units.unit_name: Codeforces 674C Levels and Regions - Aliens Trick
units.text:
Add penalty λ per region. Penalized DP: dpλ​[i]=min cost to partition first i levels into any number of regions,with penalty. Transition: dpλ​[i]=minp<i​(dpλ​[p]+cost(p+1,i)+λ). The+λ is the per-region penalty.
Now you have O(n)states(one per position). If cost(l,r)can be computed in O(1)with preprocessing,total time is O(n2)per binary search iteration,or O(n2logC)overall.

units.unit_name: Codeforces 674C Levels and Regions - CHT Combination
units.text:
The transition dpλ​[i]=minp​(dpλ​[p]+cost(p+1,i)+λ)often has structure that allows further improvement with Convex Hull Trick(covered in Section 16).
If cost(p+1,i)can be written as A[p]⋅B[i]+C[p]+D[i](separable into terms depending only on p and terms depending only on i),CHT applies. Each transition becomes a line query.
This problem achieves O(nlognlogC)by combining Aliens Trick with CHT. First Aliens removes the k dimension,then CHT speeds up the O(n2)transitions to O(nlogn). The techniques compose beautifully.

Partition N elements into exactly K groups. Each group has a cost. Find the lowest total. Direct DP: O(NK)states,O(N)transition=O(N2K). Apply Aliens to remove K dimension.
With penalty,we get O(N2)DP per binary search. Still slow. Apply CHT to reduce transition to O(N). Combined: O(NlogN)per binary search. Total: O(NlogNlog(answer)). Multiple optimizations stack.

units.unit_name: Codeforces 739E Gosha is Hunting - Problem Statement
units.text:
You have n Pokemon,a Poke Balls,and b Ultra Balls. Pokemon i is caught with probability pi​ by Poke Ball,ui​ by Ultra Ball. You can use at most one of each ball type per Pokemon. Find the largest expected catches.
If you throw both balls at Pokemon i,the catch probability is pi​+ui​−pi​⋅ui​(at least one succeeds). The constraint is two-dimensional: exactly a Poke Balls AND exactly b Ultra Balls. Standard DP is O(n⋅a⋅b),which is O(n3). Too slow.

Apply Aliens Trick twice. Add penalty λ1​ per Poke Ball and λ2​ per Ultra Ball. For each Pokemon i,compute the best action among: use neither,use Poke Ball only(+λ1​),use Ultra Ball only(+λ2​),use both(+λ1​+λ2​).
Binary search on λ1​ to use exactly a Poke Balls. Inside that,binary search on λ2​ to use exactly b Ultra Balls. Total: O(nlog2C).

Here's the structure:
Pseudocode


```
function solve(n,a,b,p[],u[])// Binary search on lambda1 for Poke Balls
    lo1 :=-1,hi1 :=1
    while hi1 - lo1 > epsilon
        mid1 :=(lo1+hi1)/ 2(exp,cnt1,cnt2):=bestWithPenalties(mid1,optimal_lambda2)
if cnt1 > a then
            lo1 :=mid1
        else
            hi1 :=mid1
    return final answer adjusted by penalties
function bestWithPenalties(lambda1,lambda2)// Each Pokemon,pick best action
    total :=0,cnt1 :=0,cnt2 :=0
    for i from 1 to n
        options :=compute all 4 options with penalties
        pick best,update totals
    return(total,cnt1,cnt2)
```

The inner binary search on λ2​ is nested inside.
Time: O(nlog2C),Space: O(n).
Time: O(nlogC). Space: O(n).

Find the largest expected catches with at most A Poke balls and B Ultra balls. Two constraints=two penalties. Outer binary search on λA​. Inner: either binary search on λB​ or solve directly. With both penalties,each Pokemon's optimal allocation is independent.
But we need the count for both ball types. Total: O(Nlog2(range))if nested binary search. Complexity grows with constraint dimensions.

A satellite photographs n points on an m×m grid using square photos taken along the diagonal. Use at most k photos to cover all points,reducing total cells photographed(overlapping cells count multiple times).
This problem gives the technique its name. It combines Aliens Trick with Convex Hull Trick for full improvement. The structure: partition points into groups,each covered by one photo. Exactly k groups(at most k photos). Find the lowest area with overlap penalties.

First,sort points by x-coordinate. Then remove dominated points: if point A's required square completely contains point B's required square,remove B. Point B will be covered whenever A is. After cleanup,the remaining points have a special structure: their required squares are nested in a specific way.
If i<j in sorted order,point i's required square starts at a smaller x-coordinate. The problem becomes: partition the cleaned points into segments,where each segment is covered by one square photo. Find the lowest total area,accounting for overlap between adjacent photos.

Let dp[i][j]=minimum total area to cover the first i points using exactly j photos. Transition: dp[i][j]=minp<i​(dp[p][j−1]+squareArea(p+1,i)−overlap(p,p+1)). You cover points p+1 through i with one photo,add its area,and subtract the overlap with the previous photo. The overlap term handles double-counting when adjacent photos share cells.
This is critical for correctness. Without it,overlapping cells would be counted twice. Complexity: O(n2k). With n=100,000 and k=100,000,that's 1015 operations. Completely infeasible.

Step 1: Apply Aliens Trick. Add penalty λ per photo to remove the k dimension. Now you have O(n)states instead of O(nk). Step 2: The transition has form dpλ​[i]=minp​(dpλ​[p]+f(p,i))where f(p,i)involves quadratic terms.
Rewrite f(p,i)in the form mp​⋅i+bp​ and apply Convex Hull Trick(from Section 16). Final complexity: O(nlognlogC)where C is the coordinate range. The Aliens Trick removes one dimension,and CHT makes the remaining transitions fast. This combination is common in advanced DP.

Pseudocode


```
function solve(points,k)preprocess points(sort,remove dominated)binary search on lambda
    for each lambda,compute dp with CHT
    count photos used
    adjust search range
    return optimal cost - k * lambda

function dpWithCHT(lambda)hull :=empty convex hull structure
    for i from 1 to n
        dp[i] :=query hull at position x[i]+lambda
        add line for point i to hull
    return(dp[n],count)
```

The CHT handles the quadratic cost function. Combined with Aliens Trick. Time: O(nlognlogC),Space: O(n).

Cover points with at most K squares. Find the lowest total area. The iconic problem that named the trick. Preprocess: reduce to covering intervals.
Some intervals dominate others. Reduce to O(N)needed intervals. DP: dp[i]=min cost to cover first i intervals. Transition: pick where last square starts. Apply CHT. Aliens trick: penalize each square by λ. Binary search to use exactly K. Total: O(NlogNlog(cost)).

Look for these signs that Aliens Trick applies:
1. "Exactly K" constraint in the problem statement(not "at most K").
2. Standard DP would require dp[i][k] with large k dimension,making it too slow.
3. It's a partition,grouping,or selection problem where you're choosing how to divide things.
4. Convex cost structure: adding one more item/segment gives diminishing returns. If you see all four patterns,try the Aliens Trick. It's rare but transforms problems from O(n⋅K)to O(nlogC),making otherwise impossible problems solvable.

What if g(k)is not convex? The simple binary search doesn't work. Check convexity: if adding one element always has diminishing(or constant)returns,function is convex.
Non-convex: sometimes adding more elements can be 'cheaper per unit'. The marginal cost isn't monotonic. For non-convex,try: randomization,different DP formulation,or accept that Aliens doesn't apply. Test convexity by computing the optimal values for different k. If the differences are non-increasing,it is convex.

1. Forgetting to verify convexity. If g(k)isn't convex,binary search won't find the right λ.
2. Not handling ties properly. When multiple k values give the same cost,track counts carefully.
3. Wrong binary search bounds. Start with reasonable bounds on λ: typically [0,max_cost].
4. Precision issues with floating-point λ. Sometimes you need integer binary search with careful rounding.

Checklist 1: Verify convexity. Test small cases manually. If g(k)isn't convex,the trick fails. Checklist 2: Handle lambda precision. Use integer arithmetic if possible. Binary search on integers is cleaner. Checklist 3: Track count correctly. Pair(cost,count)comparisons must be consistent. Checklist 4: Final answer. After finding optimal lambda,compute actual answer: cost−K⋅λ. Test on small inputs where you can verify the answer manually. Check that the count varies correctly with penalty.
Time complexity: O(nlogC)where C is the penalty search range,times the cost of the inner DP.
Space complexity: O(n)for the DP arrays.

Aliens Trick uses penalties and binary search. Slope Trick uses a completely different representation: the function itself. When DP functions are piecewise linear(from absolute values),we can represent them by their breakpoints.
Updates(like adding ∣x−a∣)shift and add breakpoints. Heaps efficiently maintain this representation. Slope Trick is another advanced technique for specific problem structures. Learn Aliens first,then add Slope Trick to your toolkit.

You learned the Aliens Trick for "exactly K" constraints:
1. Add penalty λ per item/segment to remove the K dimension.
2. Binary search on λ to hit exactly K items.
3. Verify convexity: marginal benefit must decrease.
4. Track counts during DP to guide binary search. You solved four problems: Building a Tall Barn(clearest convexity),Levels and Regions(standard partition),Gosha is Hunting(two constraints),and IOI 2016 Aliens(combined with CHT). This technique is rare but effective when it applies.

Some DP(dynamic programming)problems have cost functions with absolute values: ∣x−a1​∣+∣x−a2​∣+…. The naive solution checks every possible value.
With large ranges,this takes too long. I'll show you how to represent these functions by their slope changes. You'll use priority queues to track breakpoints where slopes shift,avoiding computation of all values. By the end,you'll solve problems with ranges up to 109 in O(nlogn)time instead of timing out.

A slope-change point is where the slope of the function changes. For f(x)=∑∣x−ai​∣,the slope changes at each ai​. These are the corners. Example: f(x)=∣x−2∣+∣x−5∣ has slope-change points at 2 and 5. Between them,f(x)=(x−2)+(5−x)=3,constant with slope=0.
Instead of storing f(x)everywhere,you store breakpoints and compute slopes on the fly. Space drops from O(V)to O(n).

You maintain two heaps: max-heap for left slopes,min-heap for right slopes. The max-heap top gives the rightmost point with negative slope. The min-heap top gives the leftmost point with positive slope.
When you add ∣x−a∣,push a into both heaps. Then balance: if max-heap.top()> min-heap.top(),you've created an invalid setup. Pop from both until ordered correctly. This keeps the function convex and lets you query the minimum location. The heaps store all slope-change points without computing function values.

A piecewise linear function is defined by its slope changes. If slope goes from 0 to 1 at x=5,we store 5. observation: we only need the breakpoints,not the full function.
The slopes between breakpoints are implicit. Two heaps: L(max-heap)for left breakpoints,R(min-heap)for right breakpoints. The minimum is in the 'valley'. To add ∣x−a∣: insert a into both L and R. Then balance: if L.top>R.top,swap and adjust the base value.

units.unit_name: Codeforces 713C Sonya and Problem Without a Legend - Problem Statement
units.text:
You have n numbers. Change each to any value. The cost of changing ai​ to x is ∣x−ai​∣. Make the array strictly increasing with minimum total cost. Naive DP(dynamic programming): dp[i][v]=min cost for first i elements with ai​=v. Transition: dp[i][v]=minw<v​(dp[i−1][w]+∣v−ai​∣).
This is O(n⋅V2). With V=109,impossible. Think about the shape of dp[i] as a function of the last value. It's piecewise linear and convex. You'll track slope changes instead of all values.

questions.title: Sonya and Problem Without a Legend - Read Statement

units.text:
Strictly increasing means a1​<a2​<…<an​. This is hard to work with. Transform it: let bi​=ai​−i. Now you want b to be non-decreasing instead.
Why? If b1​≤b2​≤…≤bn​,then a1​<a2​<…<an​ automatically. The cost stays the same: changing ai​ to x is the same as changing bi​ to x−i. This transform converts strict inequality to non-strict. Now you can use the classic non-decreasing array technique with heaps.

dp[i] as a function of the i-th value is convex and piecewise linear. Initially,dp[0](v)=∣v−b0​∣,a V-shape centered at b0​. Adding ∣v−bi​∣ preserves convexity. The constraint "v≥previous value" truncates the function on the left,keeping only where v is at least the previous minimum.
After each step,the minimum of dp[i] is at some breakpoint. You track breakpoints with a max-heap. The heap top is the best last value.

Process elements left-to-right. For each bi​,push it to the heap. If bi​<heap.top(),the array isn't non-decreasing yet.
Pop the largest value,pay the cost to reduce it to bi​,then push bi​ again. Why twice? The first push represents bi​ itself. The second accounts for the slope change from merging the popped value. Each element is pushed once initially. Extra pushes balance slope changes. Total operations: O(nlogn).

Pseudocode


```
function solve(a)b[i]=a[i] - i for all i
    cost=0
    maxHeap=empty

    for i from 1 to n:
        push b[i] to maxHeap
        if maxHeap.top()> b[i]:
            cost+=maxHeap.top()- b[i]
            pop from maxHeap
            push b[i] to maxHeap

    return cost

```

Transform a to b with bi​=ai​−i,then process with a heap. The heap tracks slope-change points.
Time: O(nlogn). Space: O(n).

questions.title: Sonya and Problem Without a Legend - Implement Solution

units.text:
Make array non-decreasing with minimum cost(sum of changes). Transform: bi​=ai​−i. Now make b non-decreasing but with the same values allowed. dp[i][x]=min cost for first i elements,last value is x.
Transition: dp[i][x]=miny≤x​(dp[i−1][y])+∣x−bi​∣. The miny≤x​ is a prefix min. The ∣x−bi​∣ adds a V-shape centered at bi​. Heap operations: prefix min=remove all right breakpoints beyond the min point. Add V=add bi​ to both heaps,balance.

What if we need strictly increasing instead of non-decreasing? Transform differently. Set bi​=ai​−i. Then a strictly increasing iff b non-decreasing.
But we want non-decreasing. Alternative: bi​=ai​−i⋅ϵ for tiny ϵ. Conceptually,offset each position slightly. In practice: bi​=ai​−i works for non-decreasing. For strictly increasing,use bi​=ai​−i and require the transformed sequence to be strictly increasing(same cost structure).

units.unit_name: Codeforces 865D Buy Low Sell High - Problem Statement
Codeforces 865D Buy Low Sell High - The Idea
units.text:
Use a min-heap to track all previous prices. When you see price pi​,you have two choices: buy at pi​ or sell shares bought earlier.
If pi​>heap.top(),you can profit by selling. Pop the smallest price from the heap(the buy price),add pi​−heap.top()to profit. Then push pi​ twice: once as the sell price just used,once as a potential buy for the future. This "undoes" the sell if a better opportunity comes.

units.unit_name: Codeforces 865D Buy Low Sell High - Why It Works
units.text:
Think of profit as a function of the last action. Each buy/sell pair contributes to the piecewise linear profit function. The heap tracks slope-change points. Pushing pi​ twice after a sell lets you retroactively change the decision. If a higher price appears later,the second pi​ can be popped as a "buy" price.
This is Slope Trick in disguise: you're maintaining a convex function of decisions without storing all states. The heap gives you O(logn)updates.

units.unit_name: Codeforces 865D Buy Low Sell High - Implementation
units.text:
Here is the solution. Use a min-heap to track all prices you might buy at,then greedily sell when profitable:
Pseudocode


```
function maxProfit(prices)minHeap :=empty min-heap
    profit :=0
    Each price p in prices
        push p to minHeap
        if p > minHeap.top()profit :=profit+(p - minHeap.top())pop from minHeap
        push p to minHeap
    // Double push for retroactive adjustment
    return profit

```

Each price,push it to the heap. If the price exceeds the minimum in the heap,you can profit by selling. Pop the minimum,add the profit difference,then push the current price twice.
The double-push allows retroactive adjustment if a better opportunity comes later.
Time: O(nlogn). Space: O(n)for the heap.
Time: O(nlogn). Space: O(n).

questions.title: Buy Low Sell High - Implement Solution

units.unit_name: Codeforces 865D Buy Low Sell High - Walkthrough
units.text:
Buy and sell stocks,at most one held at a time,unlimited transactions. get the highest profit. Insight: treat each price as an opportunity. Either buy(add to heap),sell(pop min and profit),or hold. Greedy: when seeing price p,compare to heap min m.
If p>m,sell(profit p−m)and add p back(can 'regret' and re-buy). Adding p back allows changing past decisions. This is the 'regret' trick. Total profit is sum of all positive trades.

units.unit_name: CSES Increasing Array II - Problem Statement
units.text:
You have n numbers. You can change each to any value. The cost of changing ai​ to x is ∣x−ai​∣. Make the array non-decreasing with minimum total cost.
This is the classic Slope Trick problem. Naive DP: dp[i][v]=min cost for first i elements with ai​=v. With V=109,you can't store all values. You'll track the function's shape through its slope-change points. The solution is similar to Sonya,but without the strictly-increasing transform.

questions.title: CSES Increasing Array II - Read Statement

units.text:
dp[i] as a function of v(the value of ai​)is convex and piecewise linear. Initially,dp[0](v)=∣v−a0​∣,a V-shape centered at a0​.
Adding ∣v−ai​∣ keeps convexity. The constraint "v≥previous value" truncates the function on the left,preserving only the non-decreasing part. The minimum of dp[i] is at the largest breakpoint less than or equal to the current choice. A max-heap tracks these breakpoints.

Process elements left-to-right. For each ai​,push it to the max-heap. If ai​<heap.top(),the array violates non-decreasing.
Pop the largest previous value,pay the cost heap.top()−ai​,then push ai​ again. The double-push maintains the correct number of slope-change points. Why does this work? The heap represents the piecewise linear function. Popping and pushing adjust the slope at ai​ without computing all values.

Here is the solution. A max-heap tracks the largest values seen,and we adjust when the current element violates non-decreasing order:
Pseudocode


```
function minCost(a)n :=length of a
    maxHeap :=empty max-heap
    cost :=0
    for i from 0 to n-1
        push a[i] to maxHeap
        if maxHeap.top()> a[i] then
            cost :=cost+(maxHeap.top()- a[i])pop from maxHeap
            push a[i] to maxHeap
    return cost

```

Each element is pushed once. If it is smaller than the heap top,pop the top,add the cost difference to total,and push the current element again to balance slopes. The heap size grows by at most one per element.
Time: O(nlogn),Space: O(n). This handles value ranges up to 109 that would break standard DP.
Time: O(nlogn). Space: O(n).

questions.title: CSES Increasing Array II - Implement Solution

units.text:
Make array non-decreasing by only increasing elements. find the lowest sum of increases. Simpler than Sonya: we can only increase,not decrease. The DP is asymmetric. dp[i][x]=min cost for first i elements,last value≤x.
But we only pay for increases: cost=max(0,x−ai​). Slope trick: track where the optimal x lies. It's always at a breakpoint(one of the ai​ values). Use heap to maintain.

Some problems require "prefix min": replace f(x)with miny≤x​f(y). This truncates the function,keeping only the minimum value for all y up to x.
In heap terms: after computing f(x)for all x,you want the function to be non-increasing. Pop from the min-heap until it's empty,leaving only the max-heap. This operation changes the function shape but preserves the slope-change representation. Use it when constraints force monotonicity.

Computing miny≤x​f(y)transforms the function. The result is a non-increasing prefix of the original. Graphically: walk from left to right,keep the minimum seen so far.
The function flattens into a non-increasing staircase. On the heap: prefix min removes all right breakpoints. The function becomes flat from its minimum onwards. This is why right heap matters: prefix min empties it. The slope to the right of minimum becomes 0.

Make array k-increasing: a[i]≤a[i+k] for all valid i. Minimum changes. Split into k independent subarrays: indices {0,k,2k,...},{1,k+1,2k+1,...},etc. Each subarray must be non-decreasing. Apply Slope Trick to each independently. Total cost=sum of costs for each subarray. Time: O(nlogn)Time complexity: O(nlogn)overall. Split into k independent subproblems. Each subproblem is a standard non-decreasing array problem.
Space complexity: O(n)for the dp arrays.

n jobs with processing times and deadlines. Schedule to reduce total tardiness(lateness past deadline). Sort jobs by deadline(EDD rule).
Process in this order. dp[i][t]=min tardiness for first i jobs finishing at time t. Transition: dp[i][t]=dp[i−1][t−pi​]+max(0,t−di​). The max term is piecewise linear. Slope Trick applies: maintain the DP function as breakpoints. Each job adds a new breakpoint at its deadline.

Use Slope Trick when:
1. DP has absolute value costs ∣x−ai​∣.
2. The range of values is too large for standard DP.
3. The DP function is convex. Other signals: reducing costs for scheduling with penalties,selecting values from ranges with deviation costs,partition problems with balance constraints. If you see min∑∣xi​−ai​∣ with constraints linking consecutive xi​,think Slope Trick. The piecewise linear shape is the giveaway.

1. Forgetting the double-push. When you pop from the heap,you must push the current element again to maintain slope counts. Without this,your function breaks.
2. Using the wrong heap type. Non-decreasing arrays need max-heap. Non-increasing arrays need min-heap. The heap type determines which slope-change points you track.
3. Assuming convexity without checking. Slope Trick only works for convex functions. If your cost function isn't convex,the heap approach fails.

1. Wrong heap type. For min function(convex),use max-heap for left,min-heap for right.
2. Forgetting to balance heaps. After adding a breakpoint,the heaps might violate the invariant L.top≤R.top.
3. Not tracking the base value. The function's minimum value changes with operations. Track it separately.
4. Off-by-one in slopes. The slope change at breakpoint x is+1(or+2 for double insertion). Count carefully.

Each element is pushed to the heap at most twice: once for itself,once for slope adjustment after popping. Each push and pop operation is O(logn). Total operations: O(n)pushes and O(n)pops in the worst case.
Time complexity: O(nlogn). Space complexity: O(n)for the heap storing all breakpoints. Compare to naive DP: O(n⋅V2)with V=109 means 1018 operations. Impossible. Slope Trick reduces this to O(nlogn),making previously impossible problems solvable in milliseconds.

Make array alternating: even indices have even values,odd indices have odd values. Minimum changes. Split into two problems: even indices should be even,odd indices should be odd.
Rounding to nearest valid value. Cost: for even index with value v,cost to make even is vmod2. For odd index,cost to make odd is(v+1)mod2. This is a simpler structure. Slope Trick applies if we generalize to 'make non-decreasing with parity constraints'.

Standard Slope Trick handles convex functions(V-shaped). What about non-convex? Some problems have functions with multiple valleys. Need more than two heaps. General approach: track all breakpoints in a balanced BST.
But operations become more complex. In practice,most Slope Trick problems are convex. Non-convex cases often need different techniques. Non-convex functions have multiple local minima. Representing them requires tracking more breakpoints.

Some operations shift all breakpoints: 'add c to all elements' shifts the function right by c. Instead of updating every breakpoint,track a global offset. Apply lazily when popping. Maintain: actualValue=heapValue+offset. When inserting,subtract offset first.
This handles shifts in O(1)instead of O(n). needed for problems with many shift operations. Global shifts are common in Slope Trick problems. Lazy propagation avoids O(n)updates per operation.

Drink potions in order. Each gives or takes health. Never go below 0. Find the largest potions drunk. Greedy with regret: drink each potion. If health goes negative,'un-drink' the worst positive potion. Use max-heap of potion effects. When negative,pop max positive effect and restore health. This is the regret trick again. The heap tracks regrettable decisions. Time: O(nlogn).
Time complexity: O(nlogn).
Space complexity: O(n). The regret trick appears again. When forced into a bad state,undo the worst previous decision.

Start with CSES Increasing Array II. It's the cleanest Slope Trick problem. Once you solve it,try the Sonya problem with the transform trick. Then Solve Buy Low Sell High. It's less obvious that Slope Trick applies. Understanding why the double-push works here builds intuition.
After these three,search for problems with absolute value costs on Codeforces. If you can recognize the pattern,you're ready.

Signal 1: DP over a continuous or large discrete state x,with absolute value costs. Signal 2: Transitions involve miny≤x​ or miny≥x​(prefix/suffix operations). Signal 3: The problem asks for non-decreasing,non-increasing,or bounded differences. Verify: sketch the DP function for small cases.
If it's piecewise linear with few breakpoints,Slope Trick applies. Absolute values create V-shaped functions. Prefix min flattens the right side. These operations hint at Slope Trick.

Class with: maxHeap L(left breakpoints),minHeap R(right breakpoints),base value,optional offsets. Method addAbsValue(a): push a to both heaps,balance if L.top>R.top,update base. Method prefixMin(): clear right heap,base stays. Method query(): base value is the minimum,L.top and R.top give the interval of minimizers. Use a max-heap for left breakpoints and min-heap for right. The heaps maintain the piecewise linear function.
Time complexity: O(nlogn)since each of the n operations involves heap pushes and pops.
Space complexity: O(n)for the two heaps storing breakpoints.

Standard Slope Trick gives the minimum. What if we need f(x)for arbitrary x? Count breakpoints to the left of x. Each adds 1 to slope.
Compute f(x)from base+slope integral. Efficient query: use ordered set(like C++set)instead of heaps. O(logn)per query. Trade-off: heaps are simpler and faster for operations,but don't support arbitrary queries. For arbitrary point queries,replace heaps with ordered sets. This gives O(logn)query at the cost of complexity.

Slope Trick handles continuous states with piecewise linear functions. Broken Profile DP handles 2D(two-dimensional)grid problems with complex placement constraints. In grid tiling(dominoes,tetrominoes),the state is a bitmask representing which cells are filled along a boundary.
This gives O(rows⋅2cols)states instead of exponential in total cells. A different kind of DP improvement. With Broken Profile,you've completed the advanced DP improvement toolkit. Each technique targets a specific structure.

You've learned Slope Trick. You can now solve DP problems with absolute value costs by tracking slope-change points instead of all values. You know:
1. Piecewise linear functions are defined by breakpoints.
2. Priority queues track these dynamically.
3. Transitions shift or merge functions in O(logn)time.
4. This turns O(n⋅V2)into O(nlogn). This is one of the harder DP optimizations. If it feels abstract,that's normal. Practice on the three problems above. Good work finishing here.

You've solved problems where state fits in an array or tree structure. But what happens when you need to tile a grid,where each column's state depends on the previous column's incomplete cells? I'll show you broken profile DP(dynamic programming),where you track the "profile" of unfilled cells between columns as a bitmask.
You'll solve tiling problems by filling grids column by column,with transitions based on how tiles can fit. By the end,you'll recognize when problems need broken profile DP and implement solutions for grids with different tile shapes.

When filling a grid column by column with tiles,some cells extend into the next column while others don't. The "profile" is which cells in the current column remain unfilled and stick out. For a column of height n,there are 2n possible profiles.
Each profile is a bitmask: bit i set means row i has a cell protruding from the previous column that you must account for. This profile tells you which cells are already occupied when you start filling the next column. Without tracking this,you can't know where tiles can legally go.

A profile for n rows is a sequence of n bits. Instead of storing an array of booleans,you compress this into a single integer from 0 to 2n−1.
For example,with n=3,profile 5(binary 101)means rows 0 and 2 have protruding cells,while row 1 is clear. This lets you use dp[col][mask] where mask ranges over all profiles. Bitmasks make state transitions fast: you can check and modify individual bits with AND,OR,and XOR operations in constant time.

For a grid with n columns,the profile has n bits. Bit i=1 means column i is filled at the current boundary. Example: n=4,mask=10102​=10.
Columns 1 and 3 are filled,columns 0 and 2 are empty. Transition: placing a domino in column 0-1(horizontal)changes mask from 1010 to 1011(column 0 now filled). Or placing a vertical domino in column 0: changes mask from 1010 to 1011 and expects column 0 to be filled in next row.

To transition from column c with profile mask1 to column c+1 with profile mask2,you try placing tiles in all valid ways. Each placement decision updates which cells protrude. For a 1×2 horizontal domino,you fill two cells in the same row without changing the profile.
For a 2×1 vertical domino,you fill one cell in the current column and mark the next column's corresponding cell as occupied. You recursively try all placements for each row,building the next profile as you go. Only profiles where all cells get filled are valid transitions.

Process column by column within a row,then row by row. At each cell,decide: place tile here or not. If cell(r,c)is already filled(bit c=1 in mask),flip bit to 0(it's handled)and move to next column.
If empty: place horizontal domino(fill c and c+1),place vertical domino(fill c in next row's mask),or leave empty(invalid in full tiling). Valid transitions form a graph over masks. DP computes: for each row and mask,how many ways to reach this configuration.

Given an n×m grid,count how many ways you can tile it completely using 1×2 and 2×1 dominoes. Both dimensions can be up to 10,and the answer can be huge,so report it modulo 109+7.
For example,a 2×3 grid has 3 tilings: all horizontal,all vertical,or mixed. You need to count these systematically without missing or double-counting any. This is the canonical broken profile problem. Before reading the solution,think: how would you track which cells are filled as you go column by column?

To fill column c with profile cur_mask,process rows 0 to n−1. If bit i is set,that cell is already occupied(skip). Otherwise,you must fill it. Two choices: place a vertical tile(occupies current and next column,setting bit i in next_mask)or horizontal tile(occupies current column only,but row i+1 must also be empty and gets filled).
This recursive function tries all valid placements and accumulates ways to reach each next_mask. Only when all rows are processed does next_mask become a valid next profile.

For each valid transition from cur_mask to next_mask,add dp[c][cur_mask] to dp[c+1][next_mask]. This sums all ways to reach the new profile. Precompute transitions: for each cur_mask,run the recursive placement function to find all reachable next_mask values.
Store these in trans[cur_mask]. Then the DP loop becomes: for each c,for each cur_mask,for each next_mask in trans[cur_mask],update. This separation makes code cleaner and faster. You compute transitions once and reuse them for all columns.

Precompute transitions,then run DP:
Pseudocode


```
function countTilings(n,m):
    MOD :=1000000007
    trans :=array of size 2^n,each entry is empty list
    
    function fill:
        if row=n then trans[cur_mask].append(next_mask)return
        if cur_mask has bit row set then fill
        else fill
        if row+1 < n and cur_mask bit(row+1)not set then fill
    
    for mask from 0 to 2^n - 1:
        fill(0,mask,0)dp :=2D(two-dimensional)array(m+1)x 2^n,all zeros
    dp[0][0] :=1
    for c from 0 to m - 1:
        for cur_mask from 0 to 2^n - 1:
            Each next_mask in trans[cur_mask]:
                dp[c+1][next_mask] :=mod MOD
    return dp[m][0]

```

Time: O(m⋅2n⋅T). Space: O(2n)for the profile states.

Tile an n×m grid with 1×2 dominoes. How many ways? dp[row][mask]=number of ways to fill rows 0..row−1 completely,with current row having profile mask. Base: dp[0][0]=1(empty grid,nothing filled yet). Transition: for each mask,try all ways to fill the row.
Answer: dp[n][0](all rows filled,final profile is empty). Time: O(n⋅2m⋅2m)Time complexity: O(n⋅4m).
Space complexity: O(2m)with rolling array,or O(n⋅2m)without.

For each mask,enumerate all ways to fill the row. This is recursive: at each column,try placing or not placing. Recursive generator: gen(col,curMask,nextMask)explores placing horizontal,vertical,or nothing at column col. improvement: precompute all valid(mask,nextMask)pairs.
Store in adjacency list. DP becomes matrix multiplication. For large n,small m: matrix exponentiation gives O(4m⋅m⋅logn). Useful when n>>2m.

Count ways to tile a 3×n rectangle with 1×2 dominoes. Input Format: - Multiple test cases,one per line.
Each line contains integer n(0≤n≤30)- Terminates when n=−1 Output: One answer per line. What to notice: - For odd n,answer is 0(can't tile 3×odd)- For even n: f(n)=4f(n−2)−f(n−4)- Base cases: f(0)=1,f(2)=3

For n=2,there are exactly 3 ways: all horizontal in each row,two vertical pairs,or a mix. For n=4,you get 11 ways. These numbers hint at a pattern.
Notice that n must be even. With an odd n,you have 3n cells(odd total),but each domino covers 2 cells. You can't tile an odd number of cells with dominoes. The sequence 3,11,41,153,… follows a pattern where each term depends on previous terms. Can you see it? Each new term relates to previous terms in a specific way.

Let an​=number of tilings of 3×n. Using broken profile DP with 3 rows,you find that an​=4an−2​−an−4​ for n≥4,with a0​=1 and a2​=3. Why this formula? The profile transitions for width 3 have special symmetry.
When you work out all valid transitions,certain profiles combine predictably,collapsing into this clean formula. You can compute an​ in O(n)time with a simple loop. The lesson here is that broken profile DP reveals the structure behind the recurrence.

Here's the solution handling multiple test cases:
Pseudocode


```
function triTiling()while true
        n :=read integer
        if n=-1 then
            break
        if n is odd then
            print 0
            continue
        if n=0 then
            print 1
            continue
        a[0] :=1
        a[1] :=3
        for i from 2 to n/2
            a[i] :=4 * a[i-1] - a[i-2]
        print a[n/2]

```

Loop until n=−1. Return 0 for odd n. Use recurrence a[i]=4a[i−1]−a[i−2] for even values.
Time complexity: O(n)per test case using the linear recurrence.
Space complexity: O(n)for the  `a`  array. You can reduce to O(1)by keeping only the last two values.

Tile a 3×n grid with 1×2 dominoes. The width is fixed and small(3 cells). Profile has 3 bits. Only 8 possible masks. Enumerate transitions by hand or compute programmatically.
Many masks are unreachable. For 3×n,only certain patterns work. The recurrence simplifies. Find: f[n]=4f[n−2]−f[n−4](or similar). Small width allows closed-form recurrences. Width 3 is small enough that transitions can be enumerated by hand. Find the pattern in the recurrence.

For fixed small width w,broken profile DP has 2w states per column. With small w(like 3 or 4),you can often discover a formula(like an​=f(an−1​,an−2​))by running the DP on paper. Once you have the formula,code it directly without the profile machinery.
But for variable width or complex tile shapes,you need the full DP. The profile approach is the general tool. In contests,if w is small and fixed,try small cases by hand. If w varies or is large(up to 10),implement broken profile DP.

units.unit_name: LeetCode 790 Domino and Tromino Tiling - Problem Statement
units.text:
Tile a 2×n board with two tile types: 1×2 dominoes(horizontal or vertical)and L-shaped trominoes(each covering 3 cells). Count the number of tilings modulo 109+7,where n can be up to 1000. Trominoes add new profile states: you can have one row sticking out one cell further than the other.
This creates profiles you didn't see with dominoes alone. With n up to 1000,you need an O(n)time,O(1)space solution. Broken profile DP with 2 rows gives you states and formulas that lead to a recurrence.

questions.title: Domino and Tromino Tiling - Read Statement

units.text:
For 2 rows,the profile between columns can be: 00(both rows end at the same column),01(row 0 ends earlier,row 1 sticks out),or 10(row 1 ends earlier,row 0 sticks out).
That's 3 states per column. State 00 means both rows are flush. From here,you can place a vertical domino,two horizontal dominoes,or a tromino(which creates an uneven profile). State 01 means row 1 has a protruding cell you must cover. You can place a tromino to fill it and make the profile 10,or place a horizontal domino in row 1 to bring it flush to 00.

From profile 00,you can: place a vertical domino(stay at 00),place two horizontal dominoes stacked(stay at 00),place a tromino in row 0(move to 01),or place a tromino in row 1(move to 10).
From profile 01,you can: place a horizontal domino in row 1(move to 00),or place a tromino covering row 1's protruding cell and extending row 0(move to 10).
From 10,transitions are symmetric. These define formulas: let f[i] be ways with profile 00,g[i] for 01,h[i] for 10. Then f[i+1]=2f[i]+g[i]+h[i],g[i+1]=f[i]+h[i],h[i+1]=f[i]+g[i].

By symmetry,g[i]=h[i] always(the two uneven profiles have the same count). Let p[i]=f[i](flush profile count)and q[i]=g[i](uneven profile count). Then p[i+1]=2p[i]+2q[i] and q[i+1]=p[i]+q[i]. You can combine these into a single recurrence for p[i]: p[i]=2p[i−1]+p[i−3].
This lets you compute the answer in O(n)time with a simple loop. The broken profile perspective reveals why this recurrence works. Without it,deriving the formula by hand is tricky.

For small width,Broken Profile DP often reduces to a simple recurrence. Hand-find or compute numerically. Method 1: compute first 10 values with DP,then guess the recurrence and verify.
Method 2: the transition matrix has constant size. Its eigenvalues determine the recurrence's characteristic polynomial. Example: 2×n domino tiling has f[n]=f[n−1]+f[n−2](Fibonacci). The transition matrix is [[1,1],[1,0]].

Here is the formula-based solution. The formula comes from analyzing profile transitions for the 2-row board with dominoes and trominoes:
Pseudocode


```
function numTilings(n):
    MOD :=1000000007
    if n=1 then return 1
    if n=2 then return 2
    p :=array of size n+1
    p[0] :=1
    p[1] :=1
    p[2] :=2
    for i from 3 to n:
        p[i] :=(2 * p[i-1]+p[i-3])mod MOD
    return p[n]

```

This runs in O(n)time and O(n)space. The formula p[i]=2p[i−1]+p[i−3] captures all tiling configurations by counting how the two tile types combine.
You could improve to O(1)space by keeping only the last three values.

questions.title: Domino and Tromino Tiling - Implement Solution

units.text:
Tile a 2×n grid with dominoes and L-shaped trominoes. More tile types=more transitions. Width=2,so only 4 masks: 00,01,10,11.
Each mask transitions to several possible next masks. Enumerate: from mask 00(both empty),can place: horizontal domino(→ 00),vertical domino in col 0(→ 10),L-tromino(→ 01 or 10). Simplify: with width 2,find a linear recurrence. f[n]=2f[n−1]+f[n−3]. Closed form via matrix exponentiation.

Color an n×m grid with 2 colors such that no 2×2 region is monochromatic. Profile: the coloring of the last row(2^m states).
Transition: next row's coloring must differ appropriately. Constraint: for each 2×2 block involving last row and current row,check it's not monochromatic. DP: dp[row][mask]=ways to color rows 0..row with last row having coloring mask. Enumerate valid next colorings.

Count Hamiltonian paths in a grid. The path visits every cell exactly once. Profile: track which cells in the boundary are 'open'(path enters but hasn't exited)vs 'closed'. More complex state: represent the connectivity. Which open cells connect to which? Use a more sophisticated mask.
This is advanced Broken Profile. Connectivity-based representing allows handling paths and cycles,not just tilings. The profile must track connectivity,not just occupancy. This increases state complexity significantly.

What if tiles are larger than 1×2? Tetrominoes(1×4)or square tiles(2×2)? Larger tiles mean transitions look back multiple rows.
The profile might span 2 or more rows. State: represent the last k rows,where k is the tile height. State space grows to 2k⋅m. Practical limit: k⋅m≤20 or so. Beyond that,state space is too large. The profile depth equals the tile height. Larger tiles exponentially increase the state space.

units.unit_name: When to Use Broken Profile DP
units.text:
Use broken profile DP when you're filling a grid in order(column by column or row by row)and each step depends on which cells from the previous step remain unfilled.
Classic signals: tiling with polyominoes,placing non-overlapping shapes,or any grid DP where dp tracks "which cells in this row/column are occupied."
If the grid dimension is small(height or width≤10),think profiles. If both dimensions are large,broken profile DP won't fit in time or memory. But for one small dimension and one large dimension,it's perfect.

Signal 1: Grid tiling or covering problem. Tiles are small shapes(dominoes,tetrominoes). Signal 2: One dimension is small(≤12). The other can be large(up to 109 with matrix exp). Signal 3: Local constraints.
Each placement depends on nearby cells,not global properties. Not applicable: if both dimensions are large,if tiles are irregular,or if constraints are global(e.g.,total count). Recognizing this pattern early saves time during problem-solving.

Congratulations! You've journeyed from basic recursion to advanced optimizations. Fundamentals: recursion,memoization,1D(one-dimensional)DP,2D DP,state design.
Patterns: knapsack,LIS(Longest Increasing Subsequence),LCS(Longest Common Subsequence),interval DP,tree DP,bitmask DP,digit DP,game theory,probability. Optimizations: D&C,Knuth,CHT,monotonic queue,Aliens trick,Slope Trick,Broken Profile. Keep practicing!

Time: O(m⋅2n⋅T),where m is columns,n is rows,and T is average transitions per profile. For typical tiling,T is small(constant or logarithmic in 2n). Space: O(2n)with a rolling array(store only current and previous column),otherwise O(m⋅2n).
With n≤10,2n≤1024,which fits easily. Precomputing transitions takes O(2n⋅2n)worst case,but practice is faster because not all transitions are valid.

States: O(rows⋅2cols). Transitions per state: up to 2cols(all ways to fill a row). Total time: O(rows⋅4cols).
Time complexity: O(rows⋅4cols).
Space complexity: O(4cols). This is exponential in cols but linear in rows. improvement: precompute valid transitions. If many transitions are invalid,the actual count is smaller. Matrix exponentiation: if rows is huge,use matrix power in O(4cols⋅cols⋅logrows). Matrix exponentiation helps when the number of rows is huge. The matrix size is the number of valid profiles.

Mistake 1: Forgetting that profiles represent which cells are already filled,not which cells are empty. If you invert the meaning,your transitions will be backwards. Mistake 2: Not handling the base case correctly.
You must start with dp[0][0]=1(or the equivalent for your indexing). Starting with all zeros gives zero tilings. Mistake 3: Off-by-one errors in bit indexing. Bit i corresponds to row i,but if you're not consistent about 0-indexed vs 1-indexed,you'll set wrong bits. Test on small cases.

Mistake 1: Wrong bit indexing. Decide: bit 0=leftmost or rightmost column? Stay consistent. Mistake 2: Forgetting to process cells in order. Column by column,row by row.
Don't jump around. Mistake 3: Invalid initial/final states. Start with mask=0(nothing filled). End with mask=0(everything filled). Mistake 4: Off-by-one in grid dimensions. Carefully index rows and columns from 0 or 1. Off-by-one errors are especially common. Test on 2x2 and 2x3 grids where you can verify by hand.

You've learned broken profile DP. You know how to represent which cells protrude from one column to the next as a bitmask,precompute valid transitions,and propagate counts forward. Good job. You solved three tiling problems: general grid with dominoes,3×n grid with its recurrence,and 2×n grid with trominoes.
You saw how profiles collapse into recurrences when width is fixed. For practice,solve more tiling problems with different constraints: grids with obstacles,different tile shapes,or grids where you want to find the largest some value instead of counting tilings.
